Yet the ways of our life remain, in that, of the things we care for, fear, work towards and or dispute, so our inner way of living lies. Which, for a time, was truly inner, in that the reverence and delight in being, at first, healthy, and then letter on enlightened, felt both demanding and enough. For each though, the path became less important than our productivity; and like loose lines strewn about, we simply tied them up, and worked from sturdier grounds. These lines, of leisure and health, remain at the core of us. And all I'll say, in the prescence of evil or blurred delight, is that from those primordial grounds, the trunk of our tree came. Only, now, in full and dear fashion, the branches (so to speak) are their own base. "And indeed in one, there seems a break."
I've always been the type of person who emphasized what they did, rather than with whom they did it with. I revelled in being with someone who would do the same thing, but of that thing, so did it orient us. If we were invited somewhere with someone I did not know or like, then it was only after the fact (upon doing what we were doing) that I enjoyed myself, and had any interest in them. I recall someone asking me to the waterslides; and all I could think of was that I did not wish to go somewhere with them. "Similar to how sometimes you care not to visit or recieve relatives." As we got there and slid about, I was totally in, and he was partner in such. So by that, people may impinge upon my ability to look forward to what I'm doing, 'as if there is some chore prior', or their a cloud to my otherwise interest in something were set to do together ---cause I only see them, and in feeling like to them I must attend, if there is no present desire or like of them, the activity gets blurred.
 The error in my life is one of knowledge. For in my early youth, I made the choice to go to a different school, as the one I would have naturally chosen was blurred behind my current dislike of the folk around me. We had moved, and similar to our prior school, where time had procurred us many friends and relations, that so we had an oppertunity for such. Yet, we were not central the crowd as we were before; and whereas time had procurred such a place, all while we were naturally our fun and kind self, I could not simply be so in this new localle, as time had not enough duration to work her magic. As such, I felt myself pulled to the edge of the crowd, wanting it as one felt that friends and girls were there; but the garb of it all and venere, felt off. There's perhaps a natural nervousness in me and anyone, to be around and set for such things, but of the purpose in it, so was it covered in a haze of rowdyness, sometimes meanness, and par for its course, 'a sort of lapse of the purity and heart which we as children otherwise have'. Its the dressing up, acting cool, feeling odd for simply being a friend. Whereas time had entered me into the ring by circumstance, all while I was good and kind, so did she need me to be a certain way, seemingly at least, to fit in and thrive with this new crowd. I turned it down, hung out with dorks, and otherwise held no interest in the secondary school nearby. But this was an error. As I may have been told that there'd be other people there. Morever, as you felt fine before, in that you had the true things in love and friends you wanted, all while being yourself, that so could you be yourself in this new school, considering that there were new people around, but also much room and activity to be someone; and by that, fit in with whoever was associated with that.
 But I lacked. And similar to the times ahead, where I'm a bit lost and or alone, I find purpose in trying something, and taking it seriously. Going into grade 8, I felt this ridiculous sense of propiety to its esteemed standards. "I literally studied the planner." After one day, the trial of school was overcome, and I was simply set to show up, do as I must, and enjoy myself otherwise.
 In some way, I'm terrible at getting to know people. On the playground, you tend to learn more about others and become better friends with those you stand around and gab about. On the pitch, you may not even know someones name, but at recess a bunch of kids show up and start kicking a ball around. I preferred that.
It seems an obvious truth that most things can be experienced in life. There is indeed various things of worth, but truly, none ought restrict you from another.
 What tends to happen though, is that to truly experience any of them, one ought learn and discipline themselves. In doing so, 'likely by trial and error', they deeply sink themselves into a particular thing; and to great rejoice, but by a rather high demand. "Experiencing such a high does not come effortlessly, nor without a good portion of your time --which proficiency can narrow."
 As such, we practically ignore other things, as they hold less interest compared to our comparable high, or we hold less neccessary regard, 'for our attention is bent towards those other means'.
 Again, the point is that, in all perfection, that you could embellish in many things. "No matter the flavor, so can other sorts be enjoyed." It really is about how you orient yourself, and how over time, things become easier to accomplish, opening more room.
 One aspect though, is that down a certain line, as the nature of its demand seldom ceases its mighty ask, that even in your proficiency is much time needed. "An army comapres, in that even a well ordered one still needs large numbers and effort. The point being, that of its ends, its eloquence does not mean it can drastically reduce its size. In many ways, a million skilled men are merely preferred over a million bad ones: but a million no less --and thus the need to furnish them." In turn, because our regard is so heavily placed --again, be it tough and weighty, or light and vigrouos, while maintaing the scope-- our more nimble and or free time is spent on things in relation to this foundation. A farmer for instance, in levelling off all the rubble and trouble of the livestock, may grow a garden nearby; but not neccesarily a buisness down the road. At least, if time allowed, it would have to do with his farm, rather than something more ample from a different base. One's life naturally goes this way, in that a baker or fashionista, develops a buisness in relation to their trade. Their ability affords fresh effort and time, but in extension, or in compliment, to that prior source.
Our youth is one thing, as is our adolescence. But our young adult life has generally been a time of flourishing.
 Yet, the garvest effect throughnout has been one of consistency. Our health even is not unrelated. But, namely in our localle, where we left superstore, left Boundry, had to leave 49th. Now, were back in Pitt, set at work, with a great schedule thats earning my right happiness and security. Ought I leave? Well no. There may be nothing here which remarks the bastion of your dreams; but so can we live, if however longer with the aid of consistency in mind.
 My feeling for some time, is that our nature and hues are such as given by our credulous pedigree: of which our entire adhulthood has been apart of. Given the fallacy of, 'if its not now, then never, and all are leaving if so', we can see that of our interest and satiety in our activity and care, that so are these generally enough, withotu need of taking them into civility. When we lack, we seek this, and for whatever reason, as our doubts and or dispair caused a concern in war and god, so we also sought infamous contribution and comfort. The point being, that these things are so much ares, that when they are truly so, they need not begin some far off venture: their enough. Such bounties can draw you out, as can come from and or fortify against you lapsing. But practically speaking, the things we care about come from me, and are what I really seek in their fruition. "In the swings of it, I have no longing or doubt, nor rancorous fear or invalidity." Our ideas and care, like most things, do take form, and the clearer they are, the more motion they can take; for which our wish to go somewhere oppertune or accessible is based on.
 Thereby is the issue. Our interests have come from nothing, or at least are of the sort, where their inner potential is one of so much prominence, that for it to solidfy into something beyond ourselves is afar. "Where we'd bring ourselves into the world, or some place, rather than bring our will in line with some other point of being, where that was where you'd be." More or less. This is a developmental view, that of what we care about, so is it enough; and as regards further prospects and oppertunity --as you are right and free to assume-- so is this built from those 'less than established and or common' cares coming into fruition enough, whereby they are so innate and present, that a sort of trunk, or crust rather, takes shape, and affords (and in turn motivates) broader appeal and or application. Maybe you move to Vancouver or Hollywood then. But you need it not. So much as you do, it is not that practical, as life will just return to you trying to be yourself. The point being, as that being here in this family farm town, is no less a hurdle for you being yourself than anywhere else. "What brings it out is adorned only by you, and thus mitigated little by localle."
So it just seems practical that we work here at Superstore. Some reason may compell us to move, but let that be what does so, rather than some aversion to this place, or feeling of lack in not going elsewhere.
 We work three days a week. We would do this on our own, so despite having room to do two, we ought do three. This amounts to several days off, a shift, another day off, then two days on. Each shift begins very early, and begs us to sleep properly on our days off. I concede a bit less sleep on our work nights, but through the week, ought we be right in when we go to be, as we must wake relatively early for work.
 The shifts are not too hard, though they do feel like it sometimes, and this can put our attention on remedying our effort; and otherwise, makes our active intent a drain --as we expect too much, when were not in the mood.
 It is the case, that if rest and comfort are your aim, 'as if the flavor we seek on this downshoot, till the next shift', then so ought we do things essential, be it chores or health. Naturally, to some extent, our more propserous and or thriving state takes care of it self, in terms of our personal and house duty. We can forget, or at least be to drawn out from our leisurely efforts, that it benefits us to both a) know what ought be done, for in our moving state it is easy enough to do what you have to, and b) set things up so there is less disicpline required, but mere execution.
Generally our health is good, and all I can say, is make do on the things you know to be important. Considering this centers around me, it is important to be at peace with your lifes rythm and direction. "For you may feel absent, upon merely stretching well, then doing nothing." On that note, I see our expectations for children as near gone, outside our reason that is. And of our want, evny and or shame at not pursuing something, 'or travelling to exoitic localles', so is this a wandering mode of being, rather than on of great living. But also, I am to overcome by our propiety and interest; of which ryhtm is the reward, but also of objects who are not welcome to wandering sights and fancy; nor are we buttoning down any wish for so, as our objects take their form; and thus benefit and promise only as much as they do so, of which our time and rythm is the cause. We need not leave then, or feel absent from anywhere. "Not even the setting sun, as if the summer outside your door was held back by your personal effort." It is natural for us to work, for if not then by whom would we subssit; and someone has to, for satiety we need. And so, the work which is natural, of which I prefer a life which is rich in harmony with it, rather than compensating for being somewhere you'd rather not, is something which were bound to do, and not so likely change. The stream goes on, and as such, our amends to it is only good; and thus, of your notice and observations, make such changes, as to feel no burden from your demands, and experience the delight which surrounds this. Objective grandeur and or development can srpout from this, and only needs time and consistency to take form.
Most of the stoics are in line with the writers who I've grown to call friends over the years, in that of our experience, ought we be able to observe the qualities in it, and by that, judge whether or not the measure is right.
 This contrasts with some notion of reason or purpose, as if how we perosnally are is not critical to uphold, but rather some broader, if not divine plan. Indeed, this sentiment gets intwined into wisdom, as if abiding such plan will bear qualities which we'd observe as swell. But so is this not about peace in our hearts, akin to ones otherwise satiety upon comfort; but rather some sort of obediance and respect; which is itself garnered against our experience, 'as if we'd be punsihed or rewarded for doing so'; but also by some measure of shame; which is not ultiamtely that laudible, as for which reason is this so; but it is clear by the ages, that of our own ends, that so is this not esteemed or value'd, and in some sense demured, but virute of the love and sacrifice for something beyond us. In the realm of man, it is not clear why one ought interfer upon anothers beligiderence; at least, whether he does so for the poison that may spread, or for the wrong being committed. But, it is like the love we find in others, where we wish no harm upon them, and in fact shriek when it does: as if it were upon us. And it would seem, that the extent of this happening, 'albeit scaled by ones natural orient, in terms of their dearest friends and family, with a stranger who they care for in a less than saturate way', that so of that expanse, in which someone other holds a spot in our heart, 'ought it be so for all of men'. And as such, if one was attacking your daughter, or kind to your friend, so would you accoridngly oblige: in feeling good or mad. In this way, we can center our inquiry of self and outer regard on the basis of love, as such affection is in a sense part of what we observe as good in ourselves; as if those we care'd for were like donuts and wine, but of a mere difference in complexion, 'for which the form of our care is so'. Humanis, or the notion mentioned prior, is so that of the lenght of the pantry, so the globe lay; and of those alien to us also.
 Observation though does not do so well in fundementals; as such things are not exactly seen and shown, but discovered, or considered at least. In many ways, what we more clearly see, 'floating above', is what we use as to inquire and or deduce what lay beneath. As such, we ought not stake our regards on the pure basis of love and affection; as we don't do so for more obvious pleasures; which is to say, that we don't use some base sense of pleasure to regard things, but rather remark upon their evident nature and qualitites. For it is hard to say why soft things feel better than those which are rough. And similar to the compositions in life, where we might look at the indiviual motions of their parts, 'as to create the apparent orchestra', or literally on that note, that we may overlook the buidling blocks of a song, or of the grammar in writing, and actually read the words or hear the melody. By those counts, we could distinguish our regards as being of a more general and moving sort, and with such correspondance of things and their matter of regards, as so general; compared to our deepest inquiry and remarks; which, by our limitations and the speculative mean, that so our hesitation lays; or better put, of said remarks, we hold as much use of them, or of such deep regards, to things on such a level, as not to exceed the nature of the subject beyond the depths we know. All that is to say, that of things we'd otherwise speak of in a general and common way, and thorugh such means, that of what said things could be seen as, 'ie., a cell verus the atoms its composed of', that as so truly and well that we can regard things, 'as if, on the level of the atom, we'd found such scale', that we'd only care for and consider things on such basis. "If things could go deeper, and are even known to be so, ought our means of inquiry and regard be as capable; or accomodated to in our proccession: let the subject be foot which bends with our speculatively capable shoe, and no more."
 On that note, I am not at liberty to say what pleasure truly is; so far that is, as to make meaningfull remarks. As I do know that sugar for instance has a neuroligical response, and by that, bars a certain effect. But so does an apple lie in that class, but of merely different proportion. Indeed the discussion comes to be, in that of the apple, so is its medley of effects different than the cookie. We may know said effects, but who is to say that the more concentrated comfort is laudible in the prescence of the glycemic effect.
 As it were, this is what Stoicism is. At least, it is a kind of framework, a value set if you will , as to what baser qualifications things should fit to. Again, the source for such framework may be personally driven, in that it is souly about what we observe in ourselves; or it may lean more towards something beyond us, be it divine or civilly moral. Akin to love though, in that describing it on a baser level, 'and washing off that warm esteem', that so can our care for the gods and country be equally considered --on that baser level.
 The ontology of things can provide a levelled grounds for inquiry, in that of what we are concerned with, so may falsity and superstition hold no acclaim. But it is thereby so hard to do, for you are left with a mountain of theory and narrative, 'as if the world were not a bundle of actions, moving to and from the last,' but rather an idea, a concept in our mind, as art and plays reflect. Ultiamtely, this may be the mode in which our inquiry is placed on, as were all forming ideas and or descriptions, differing only in form; and as such, inspiration, or source for a given form. That is indeed the balance of all critical and or expressive culture; as to show how things are, what they ought be, what is poor, to be fearfull of or look forward to. And indeed, the world has shifted from theism and God to secular society and conduct. But its all wrapped, and were lost; as we have neither a framework which we can stand by, nor any sense of the truth beyond all these conflicting concepts. As it is not likely that nature is fundementally different for any of us, nor expressive and or distirbuted in any heterogenous way. As far as people are concerend that is. And as such, varied ideas reflect different backgrounds and sources, 'as if different batches of evidence were used to create a sort of idea'. This may be filched with ignorance and neglicenge, as not everyone can or cares to know everything; nor may they be without bias and or homeliness to their current foundations. But it is overall not resounding that any truth would be short of soul and singular. By that, I given allowance and respect to varied perspectives, if only because it seems practical and expected.
 As such, every man ought have his own set of beliefs; and as I like to say, the mark of a man is not so much that his views are true, but that he cares for them, stands by them confidently but also with some self respect and fidelty. He may admitt their weakness, but at least he says that their is a reason for them, even if its weak. He is allowed to be wrong after all.
When we have fixed all the rules of conduct, we are philosophers: When we have reduced these rules to practice, we are man.
For all of my adult lift, young as it may be, I've been enobled to purify the ssemingly futile ends of my common man. Those victim to cruelty, beligerence and misfortune, were never on my mind; largely due to their being no notice of this.
 As I grew older though, and my own senility, 'which I sought to impart', became innate, 'and no longer some ideal we'd formed', so did I have less natural motion and thus affirment; to which, things of an impressive sort, 'of whole and or concentrated kinds', dug right through me. Television shows began to exhert a terrible rancour, as if the vile medleys on display were something I could imagine someone else experiencing; or the precious statements and esteem of the news, 'or confidence of boys on youtube', sprouting proclomations and or attention, for which our world view became burnt by fallacy.
 I initially counteracted this grievance by looking into the civil side of the world. Prior, my efforts and conquest were centered on the quality of our lives, and thus the nature of things was what I reflected on and ordained from. But in order to remove the gratuitous reality shown from film and recognize the fact, that howerver severe and belidgerent, that so many experience misfortune and depravity, but without our flinching eye, that any of the prompts towards me where either truly fictitious, by whichever degree, or unbecoming of the world.
 I realize now, having read Humes Stoic essay, that the shortness of my life is in the adornment of social virtue. As however noble we are in our own charms and strides, up on the mountain of ease, so do we overlook, and as prompted, shy away from those beneath on the common ground. Indeed, there are fallacies and saturate views. But hath a man died no less. Are there not children starving in Africa. Will the lowlads of the world become increasingly subject to the northern melt? There is so much any of us can do. And what I figure, is that as I am ennobled by greater sentiment, that so upon exhibiting that, that so will be more or less noble, and ripe for ennoblement. "Spread the shine."
 I feel this to be our path. I see it, believe it, wish for it in our heart and bones. It seems counterintuitive in some sense, as such a bold aim feels smewhat a passion, or dream at least; born of our current idlness and grief, and thus, upon the lapse of those things, 'as fortune or summer come on by', that so will you not be so ennobled, but rather content enough to relish in your own personal glory.
 This is not the point. Absolutely, the inconstancy of such path will bring us to something true and reliable. But such end is not one of sacrifice, 'as if we only embrace it because our preferred comforts in falsity lapsed'. No. It is so, that of all our efforts, we prefer to share them with a friend. And of our virtue and might, equally. And as the qualities of our personal path, garnered with wonder and joy, are not in their automony gluttunous or vain, but truly of the sort which engenders me with scrumptous felicity and delight. But such a character loves nothing more than to be with others, or at least live so mightily, but amongst other folk. Such relations can be drawn out of contirbution, creativity, intamacy or friendship. But so, we plainly say, that of the good we seek, it is better, and in some sense enabled, by doing so with other people. Living well at least, is loved and earend through our place in the world, as we'd seek in any sort of affair. We are social creatures after all.
Glory may be the dearest yet hardest thing to acquire. As it means magnificence, glowing greatness if you will. Wisdom suggets, or to some extent inssits, that seeking acclaim by others is folly. "As if a counterpart to our other inward comforts; so is this equally so, but outward."
 Yet, what is glory then, but the revelling of one by their grandeur: the gleam they spread around, so brilliant and lush.
 The way I'd see it, is along the lines of passion, and how such things are not so much comforts and joy, but rather yearnings, fillings to our seeking heart. As such, we may seek the approval and or admission of others; and by that, banishment of fear upon them, or scrutiny at that. But this unnerving wish, is not what glory is emblimatic off. In its case, one is true to themselves, of a virtue both bold and and kind, and through that, bountiful in their time and efforts from such source.
 Passion is a sort of lack, whereby one is burdnened or bored, and thus is impassioned towards that which, in concept or acquistion, ceases such need. Virtue is about a calm and happy heart, a well pitched mind and steady soul. Our orient though through the world is such, that of its bodlness, so may we bring ourselves so lushly and formiddable through it; and adorn ourselves to the affflictions and grough of others, and otherwise elevate those with the means and or eye for liberty. "As the sun crosses the sky, enligthening all keen to such sight, so may we traverse the towns, scour the lands, rest upon the globe, of our ennobled hearts, rich mind and splendid soul." It is called glory then, as compared to pure delight, for we are giving, rather than merely tasting natures wonder, 'but instead showering it around'.
 I am a fool of a man, as to be so inconsistent that my engendered tastes are not enough; or disturbed at least by our lapse, and thus subject to more viorlent wishes. And as such, be it still, in leisure or employment, or broader contribution and aim, so is our indolence their something which has us reject the motions of others. "As if a sort of envy to those so bold and striding, while we sit all unnerved nor settled."
So, theres two things we ought consider, the manner of our exercise, and that of food.
 In the case of the latter, so are we imbued with the means for food; as, along our lines of fairness, we set the acquistion of money for food as part of it. And within that, spending the money is natural. And so, of the money we have, so may we consider how to spend it; be it for comfort or nourishment.
 As for our health, I see much benefit in altering our yoga, as to find those little sessions which keep us going and set. I feel our lack of pushups and pullups is so noticeble; that even our abundance yoga cant suffice. Aerobics are a great base, and be it at home --where we skip, juggle or march-- or at the field, running, so ought this serve as our continuity; but in that, foundation for moments of resistance to be done. I feel that pushups are easier and beneficial, while pullups have some vanity in the shape of our arm, but still some strenght in our shoulders and openess to our chest. Each are as accessible, as we have the bar in the annex, or the goal post. There just much harder. They will naturally take something from us, and so our abilities ought match up as to be somewhat solid. But, akin to our foundation of aerobics, may pushups be done as they easily come, and pullaps as they properly fit; even if we do them less.
So tomorrow might be a little bit rough, but nothing too bad. Were off the day following, so very little to fret about.
 We've got some granola but not alot. We'll live. Break is likely not till the end of shift, but we can just come back and do a large run. So in effect, 4 picks, then hang about for an hour.
 Music is fine, and something we'll fiddle with through the week. I reckon that we can just revert to the old notes, set up a to do portion, and zoom in for mobile. JSON will not work for cross over, but there is a free backserver, which some finnicking around can produce. That is part of our creative effort.
 I relate this then to our training, which ought be measured as crucial as our work. But in that, you err in forcing something; right now for instance, lets take a step back, have some bread, go for a scooter then walk, stretch, and tend to tomorrows day. We'll make our note thing pretty quickly, and return to the order that gave me.
 But, in terms of health, I think a quick edit of our standing, shortening of supine, and similar shortening/edit of neck and seated twist, would go along way. I think that 'chill' has always been solid, and we could benefit from some splendid form of it, with the red sweater arm stretch, the current side twist and turn, maybe a foot rub, then the back. Easy and productive. Our resistance training is lapsed, and for whatever reason, this putrifies me. Even our excellence in yoga and eating cant hold me up. I am unnerved though by this sentimnent, as the proper actions are not clear, nor does it seem that our respone will lack neccessity. I say then, that our creative and or leisurely pursuits may come and go as we please; considering that our life is a flow, ryhtm and form, of which this moment is not worthwile when it burdends the rest. Your idle shame and or worldly smallness are not true symptoms of concern, but rather signals that your amends are unheraled as right. To work we go; and yes, we elect for some of it, but this merrily does so for the stability and or means it offers. But you ought not feel bad for the short term offer of working less. It is not hard to reach that steady range, and having exhibtied it, moments that are our fortune to be less dont speak ill of the future --our affection may or may not lower us there. As such, you can see work coming, the neccessity and or good from training and all other remedial things. If comfort and or rest is what services this, fairly and within your means, so is this just, and worth no consilation. As such, your novel and or useful interests can go a long way by the ebbds and flow of what you can offer, but no more. The strain is not worth depleting what is neccessary, nor to the milder benefit of those cares: as returning to them is the greatest delight.
 There is much I'd like to do, namely perform some pushups, continune our running and juggling, try out our skipping if home motion is preferred, going to yoga monday morning as to remove that burden of an entrance; and similarly, set up our compass card, as to remove the limit of impulse. The rest will follow.
So I'm about to wind down and ready for bed, but I do have something to say.
 For one, I don't need more time to do what I please. I may make use of the time which is given, be it this week, our general point in life, or even the principle which simply guides us through our farther years.
 And so, it seems we have few shifts coming up. A couple this weekend, but only 1 next friday and Sunday. I may remove that, and pick up another. I wont stress though. Were just doing what we can, and our money situation is nearly fantastic.
 I dont prefer to work less, as you can feel distant and or apathetic. But there is much I can do. So long as we are abiding the rules, so be it. I dont want to be timid in our approach, but not otherwise begrieved if little work is to come. More will pass infront of us.
 By that, there is much for me to do, and a few basic things I'd like to commemurate (begin). The days will come and go, and our leisure and or industry is always there, and finely put into fair fashion.
 The elective part of this job is odd, as it slowing to a minor amount can feel incredulous and worth hesitance. Our communication though is the factor which promulagtes our time in this current spot; and of our happiness, so is that birthed by our use of time, and the proper candor and cadence of our effort. Let it be so, in the fundementals which commensurate such things, as to be more free and common. And otherwise indulge in the finer delights, as given or gleamed.
These thoughts in my mind, clouded by poor thinking, subject to outside influence.
 It is clear that our place in life is tenuable based on an acceptable value. But that value is never so clear; though, we are at libety, and otherwise limited by sense, to endow such and amend.
 As it were, the rules of conduct are never hard to adhere to, but rather feel their is truth in them; for which our waivering causes an unease in our practice.
 In the farthest sense I see, that glory is not something which rightly sprouts from your dreams, but rather tangles itself around your current predicaments. The itineray of work and committment to tasks, dealing with colleuges and friends, tailoring our health, ordaing the proper use of our time. So, if industry fashions our mind, that rather than live alone, or at least detached from things, 'on that temple above', it seems more just and to our favor that we'd be so strong and light in the world, commonly, and ever on to where the sun glows.
 I find the most timult when this candor of ours is drug out by foreign impressions. The source is a undying yearning, of which this is the effect of our past self. But so in this shifting want, so the care for things exotic and or wish for benevolence lie. There is much which provokes this; and the nature of these prods is such as to be somewhat admirable, as it does stirr me to action. But I am unconvinced that our wishes and or amends, 'of aid and lust, so far', is without fallacy, and by that count, evenly bent. For I see, in glory, that we are in full and open stride. But the dream of this, 'as if some farther road lied ahead', beckons me away from the points we already tend.
 As it is, we are on our line, and bearing ourself to that creates all right potential: be it near or far. But as I said, the clarity of this line is what is in need of certainty, and as such, inquiry and discussion. For I notice that we feel our position in life to be a little unchecked, nor consistent. This is so, that by our affection, we've never grown much to one place, as time and naievety have blown us away. Indeed, we've never felt fair in being so grounded, which however understandable considering the influence around, was in effect an error. I don't see any reason for us to leave. And even if we were to live on our own, so would this require but a minor adjustment within our current post. I adjurn all inquiries and or strife regarding family value. Discussion is welcome, but no pretense, or esteemed observation. As such, our simplicity is not unbecoming of what our family would be; and as such, be it in the scale of our current kind of post, or a managable extension beyond this --for which there is ample room and time, considering the scope of such extent-- that so will we consider ourselves fine, and able to commensurate any want and need.
 Similar though, to the glossy plea's from those conveying sentiment of the wide world, so is the path of family one that is equally far and of sentiment; differing though in that the extent is in time, ahead that is. As such, we form ourselves against the natural want and or fear regarding things blurry and far away. Equally, of what we feel is expected, or advised within something we please, that so do we equally firm agaisnt, as not to feel pillaried agasint our manner of preperation. As such, by time and extent, we are given reign to earnestly apply, but unsubject to far wants or dramatic views. I am not ignorant of a childs requirment, nor the cruelty and misfortune that pesters the land. But of what I can do, and may feel fine doing so, absolutely is quality the defining factor, and from there is extent but a natural procession: whereby you bring what would truly remark values and virtue, into a broader way. "We came down from yon high, tranquil and of might. But upon our entry into the towns and fields, we simply spent what we could commonly; whereby of those we passed, worked with, made love to, so did they earn our delight."
 And it is an honest man truly who bears their heart and soul to all those around. The difference being that through consistency, this flood of his love reaches the farthest bound. Perhaps knowledge and contempory insight may draw him out as to apply his efforts in direct ways, to those farther bounds. This is the choice though, to truly be so whole and strong, beyond that which is merely in front of you, but to the extent that you've come to know.
Here is an honest review of our time thus far.
 For one, removing Porn has done wonders. Our sex drive took time to come back, but it is here; and admitteldy, limited only by our health. It's also removed much of my regreatble portraits of woman; though I do still see butts as an attractive feature on girls. "And there is no stillness from impulse in our mind."
 Our health is a balance of good and improving. Our aerobic health is swell, yoga is at worst maintaing and otherwise consistent, and our general work flow is good as to keep us packed in and steady. Our resistance work has been more or less absent; and if it weren't for the kind of effect it gave, in that it grounded us and gave room for consumption, so would I be fine. I reconginze so little of our arms; and it is not just acceptence of some fact, but an unregarding notice. As of now, the progress is such that a field day includes much of our borader motions and activity, while skips, juggling and jump rope are good at home; and the balance is swell, as to be consistent and sufficeint. "Which till now, was never enough."
 A few notes, that running is fine, as is our eating pretty wonderful. We have a great balance of our day, in that since it keeps going, 'naturally, and in our sight', that so are we eating more and simpler. Music is mighty, and it must be some remark of our state that we are in love with the planet earth 3 theme. Its great, with more to come. Gaming sits like most of our creative leisure and activity, in that, of our work, its relation to health and the balance of this all in time, that so are our efforts there acceptable.
 I see that much of the world, in all its grandeur and glory, is densley exposed to us, and thus, if our sense is nearby, or our experience is deep, than it takes something from us to suspect our immediate reponse. "For so many formats of things offer information which, of the judgement made or ours given, that too little info is had, or not of a fair quality." I remark that our mother and father are burreid with such is a taunt to me; like most things though, being clear allows for habitation, endurance and peace.
 The rest of the world lies, on route to its entirety.
So, logic has the form of if and thens. A is so that if B is there, so will it follow. But, if I were to use real things logically, rather than symbols --ie. mathematics-- then I would refute this by saying that logic and reality don't exist. For, if something is real and defined, than it is so for it has no logic, but rather a nature; logic is there to the extent that it is not immanent, but rather governed. And so there being both is contradicted. Logic then, is eligble onnly for symbols. But in such ways, if we say that if A then B, and then state that B is there, so is this nearly refutable, as who is to say that B is present. We can admitt the nature of symbols, but what are we describing as to convey that B is there? In many ways, reducing our sensation to something real removes any ability for this, and renders the use of symbols in otherwise natural ways -ie. in manners that reflect causes and prescence of real things.
 This is an inquiry though. And I recgonize that all this runs contrary to are arguments that have certain axioms to them; ones which allow for symbols to be used as if they were normal things, or normal things to be used logically, while not being but features of logic, but real.
 Our father responds to certain bits of information, finds it absurd, but then advises me based on it. This I find a struggle, as not being able to say otherwise is a hard thing to do. Though, I will not say that our life is beckoning to this; were merely aware of the certainty we lack. But there is this other element, that when I see someone make a quick judgement, 'regarding subjects of strife', I get frigthened, as if people are taking up arms, forming enemies. I am unsure if the dynamics of this are seen in the world, or to be expected. It would seem the only thing keeping me from saying that there is not much which is radical, 'as our reaction would seem', is that radical moments seen extend their suggestion that more are doing so.
So the sun rises and sets, and through the year, it inches up a bit more day by day, and then less so day by day. The Solstices are the peaks of these points. It also rises and sets at nearer points, latitudionally, and equally further. The peaks of these are the equinnoixes. What tends not to change is your form of living. As we still work, need sleep around 10, up around 8 and 7, lunch in the afternoon, dinner at night. For me, the varying scenario is when twilight sets in, or the sun is high in the sky. You know that noon is awlays the same. All that differs is the sun rises earlier from this, and sets later after. In the winter solstice, the sun sets quickly after noon, and rises quite close to it. The opposite occurs in summer. What this means then, is that for twilight, which one is prompted to see, so is there much time after, for further walks and winddowns. While in summer, you have whole other periods of the day, where you go ought, see the shades of things, and come back, all to do it again.
 Right now, the sun sets around 9, and it is twilight till 10. This is perfect, as we go to bed at 10, and are apt to scooter around 9, then walk till 10. As it sets earlier though, there is a point where there is excess time after. I reckon, say, that if the sun sets at 8, with twilight lingering, that you may come back, linger for a while, then go for a walk. Right now, we just combine them, as the lead in to bed is all in sunset and twilight. Another balance will be our evening walk, as to wash off dinner. As the sun sets earlier, you may go for that walk in relation to the scooter ride you'll take for sunset.
The proportions of your day can change, but generally, we don't want to see 12:00 as bedtime, and winddown at 11, but rather bedtoom around 10 on work nights and 11 on off nights, and thus winddown around 9.
 There will be a point though where the sunsets around 7 and you have this big chunk prior to winding down. This conflicts with the evening walk, which is something you do following your siesta and or lunch, or something after dinner, if you've gone out in the afternoon. The point, is say you want to go for a scooter, and mean to walk after. Then go for a walk prior, around 7, then relax till 9 and wind down. If sun sets at 7, then surely your going to go for a walk around 930 anyway, so it may bethroth you to go for walk then scooter, or scooter then walk. Its hard to say.
So, the rule is that you must give 1 hours notice, otherwise its a no show. Three and your out. We have two. This means that, if you wish to call in and its less than an hour till your shift, then you cannot miss it; it is a matter of that or your job.
Compassion is the weirdest thing, for it seems the best, but like mere passion, is a bit of a longed for thing, rather than natural from you. Our kindness is not compassion, in that, if someone yells for help, you respond not out of compassion, but yuor nature, as human. "It calls on me not to rescure a child in the street, as it calls me not to breathe: i just do it."
 Compassion feels like something you humour, in that you have this yearning to help. This feels similar to glory, in that you recognize your worth, or at least gleam upon virtue, and cherrish the appearence of that. But this is a short measure, as one who is in fact shining, notices little, but is rather at ease and at peace. As Yoda says, you will see and beleive when you are in a state of rest.
 I lack the thinking though to retort the plea to aid one another, deliberatley. For I am in a good state or returning to it. And so our compassionate amend feels like something that ignores the position we'd return to. In that postion I see much, but am not enthralled by it, in fear or sympathy; nor, in equal manner, of things exotic, for which we are absent of impulse or envy.
I have many thoughts, ones in which I know will not cease our thinking.
 But I ought divulge them no less ,as to make that coming step a good one.
 Foremost amongst these is that fact, that of our nature and way, it is one of progress, adaptation, and broadening this bound.
 No one can rightly say, that of old issues that they are a) completely dealt with, or b) without oppertunity to improve. As our connection of tool and task is not itself a perrmit to cease, but rather means to employ; by that, our trouble becomes in the ware of our muscles.
 As such, we could unite all of this, in that, of our heart, soul and mind, so components lay. And of such elements, are they entwined and bound to the world they exist in, and by that, esteemed as having a nature bade for satisfying connection. "Our nature is that which remarks how we be, in procuring and gleam."
 The mighiest of questions though, is upon our right assembly, 'where all the parts lock in', ought we consider ourselves done? Or maybe we refelct on whether our 'handiwork' needs imrovement: admitting the effect of a loose thread. Or, is this all a pitfall of inquiry, that we think, upon lacking no structural consistency, that more can't be done. "As Alecander hit the west he found India, and what was once thought the edge of conquest, became the root of something new."
 What I would say, is that this conudrum, 'as if nothing else appears to be won', is a little flatualent, in that of the greatest escapades, 'of God high and atom low', that so stirrs in us the blood in our hearts, the creek of our bones. Are we not mortal, imperfect in our labor and rest, throbbing from the vices of these poor excesses, 'or unset pieces rather'. Are we not still man?
So compassion seems to be the oddest force in the galaxy. As I don't consider it comnpassionate to aid your mother or father, or a stranded child in the road even. But when you see advertisement for a charity or foodbank, when you hear of conflict and or belidgerence over seas, so may you look outside yourself --which your family, friends and folk in immediacy lie-- and impart your effort for the sake of something other.
 In truth, I am not appauled and or begrieved by homeless, crime, misfortune, common death and or fallability outside my door. It is really just awful begrudgements of certain autracities and war. I am lucky to live in a country and time when this does not happen; but so a century or two ago would I not be so immune to such.
 Where this issue of compassion lies, is in the oddity of its complexion; in that, someone may see a pamphlet on children starving in Africa, and be moved to a compassioned effort, where they donate some food and or money for such affair. Yet, they likely do not look into it further, understanding a) how their contirbution matters, in the way there personal budgets are acounte for, and b) what else could they do, for this or other problems. This is the issue with compassion, that it evokes a genuinely good response, but merely that. While a humanist direction is bound in the knowledge of all peoples affairs, and amened to act and uphold standards and or effort for the purity of that. This is a deliberate sentiment, of which one's life is bound to; rather than a response, an emotional reaction, for which compassion is a sort of cease; as glory is recognition of honor and virute, so is compassion a release of the grief we feel in sympathy, 'when the poor sight of another is shown to us'. There is a great deal of ill-proportion though in our general view of things. And rather than see the world for how it is, 'a network of complex environemnts', we have an average'd out view of instances we see, 'those bits which evoke compassion'. Indeed I find this to be the modern dilemma. For all is on the stage, and nothing short of our personal lives is hidden. But despite there being coverage of much, and accesibility to nearly all, we don't helm our intake of such things, and, foremost, our judgement and digestion of even a limited amount, to any credible and or laudible degree. The result, is that we see everything as if it were a snowglobe, and nations become symbols, leaders encapsulate governments and policy, culture becomes a standard, rather than something emblatic of a certain school or scenario: their, that or 'a' culture, particular to a certain bunch. Instead it is thee culture. All this does is serve our minds with jumbled concepts and imagery, as if every person were on the same boat, feeling the same waves, groaning about the captain. Indeed there is a tie which binds us together; and in heart and strucutre, so the extent of our humanity lay. But much of our modern diet ends up being an assortment of exagerations, predictivie fallacies, and pithy criticism. "It was easier when the pithiness was on our own shame; when our personal success was all that mattered, and we were dealt contempt upon not achieving that." Now its a garbled mess; a mess which is equally seen only in that there is a mess. "This even is not a fact or statistic which resembles anything about the whole of peoples lives." Its just hard not to see this, and have it confirmed, 'and in other moments irriated', from our chambers, 'of tv, media, scrolling'.
 As a man, I cannot speak to the truth of things. My place is rooted in the fact that all I can do is admitt my lacking here. Basic sentiment, upon reflection and critique, shows how our impressions and reaction to things is ill fit based on a) the quality and quanity of the source, and b) the lack of doubt and or formation in our judgement. "Personally, I struggle foremost in dealing with people inflicted with the latter; as they become so ingrained in their own beliefs, that they have no concept that their judgement could be wrong: that there is any reason to wonder and doubt." This causes me to reject their evidence, rather than sit with it; for we may think it weak, but our judgement need not be taken like theres; and thus as evidence it can lay, for of our own ardor, it has not claimed.
 The question then, is if honesty and resolve are the keys to any form of success, where is the point in which we become a global human? Are we bound to recognize the wear in our back, the toil in our mind? Is our aversion to CBC and thumbnails a mere instance of those, 'of things that we positivley or negatively convene with, as known by observing how were effected?' Why is the notion of deliberate and or bold humanism a concern? Does comapssion bother you because it is so pithy in comparison; yet perhaps, this bold resolve to the world is akin to glory, in that it is a sort of reward for being good, outside the taste and charm already given? Is teaching and or modeling something you really wish to impart? Why not to that homeless man you see passing every day? Global Warming, social issues and war fair more interest?
As we have thought for many things, the principle in this case is one of seemingly personal virtue and compassion. For in one, you are weighed upon, naturally that is; and your able response is exhibuted by virtue of what you have; in that, of what would be suited for such weight, you possess. Compassion, in the simlest sense, is about the regard of others. And not, to be clear, about acting as is proper in a community; which is to say, that you don't take away from it, but put in what you take out.
 I reckon there is a truth beneath this, that, of what we take, that being lawful in doing so is not merely what we actually receive. For there being laws may bring with it a certain history, or at least development, in which other people are not equally positioned. For our country, and thus the lands which feed us, however well obliged by its localle residents, 'in which we say, the sustenance was lawful', so may this country have bouldered over some other group of people. By that, whichever propensity or procedure leads to war, that so are other folks circumstance a motivator for such. And thus the awful afflictions dealt by such engagements are born of people moving as they see fit, per their situation. In a similar sense, a riot, or rebellion, is about folk who are so positioned as to want change in the structures around them. It is generally not nobles are middle class folk who are engaged in such; even if they feel the weight of taxes and legislation. Rather, it is those who are at best getting by, and in some cases, not even so.
 That is the oddity in life. For, in truth, there is a unified view, by which all things have come about. "So much as nature is the bearer of rythm and action, so hath the planet, life and humanity made." But some of us are at worst subject to the weight and or cruelty of taxes and regulation. While others are subject to the misfortune and depravities of famine, parched economies and or strife. Maybe the truth is that we have more problems than were liable to admitt; or at least too foolish to. Many of my comrades have issues of low indulgence; while I, perhaps in deliberate contrast, have shortcomings of more genuine goods. But we are short no matter; and thus were not driven to local misgivings nor foreign aid: as we are still conjuring our own.
I sense that our principles will further progress. As they did with the Gods, with us being human, of nature and death; to gratuity, linens, collectives and peace, so will these coming bounds make.
 Yet, in my heart of hearts, I know that more will need to be done. Perhaps a balance in my life is all thats off, and there being so, would make for a stability I only new as well. "For when were idle and of lack, we are more sensitvie to things; and what we deem as neccessary, is thus broader." So it may just be that: for which the principle we still wish to continue, will be but a point of strenght upon our ease, 'rather than some fortifying ointment, as if the world were actually more nearer than it really is'.
 This is a very sincere approach, for it doesn't revel in the apparent plight of folk around, 'the subjects of our current unease, and foreign irritability'. In many ways, I take respite with the fact, that of those who, in our indolence, appear in need, 'and by us particualrly', that so are these in relation to servicemen and communal folk, who will more directly, and seemingly situationally, protect, support, tend to and or abade. Moreover, that of whichever experience one is dealt, in normal discomfort and illness, or more harsh and grueling sickness and injury, that so are those subject to such still people, and by that, liable to fortitude and endurance. As much of this as they lack, and however much fear and hysteria their infraction lies, so are they but in a trance; in one indeluable sense of mortality, 'for which peace truly sets', and one of reconciliation, 'where in trauma or loss, they come to see what they still contain, and how alright things were'. Indeed, the fear in life comes down to rot imagery of assailants and belidgerence, who cause a complexity of dissaray and discomfort; of the sort which we have no fair means of knowing. As such, hunger, disease, torment and oblideration are exastervated in their causes, due to the mirage of effects. Memoirs and our own reflections --of how we anticipate these experiences ourselves-- irons out our paultry sight of this. But still, we cannot help be see the knowledge we lack, espeically when the sense of others is breathed upon us. The news fairs similar, in that, of unfortunate and or awful things happening at all, so do those words --by my belief empty, and by sense narrow-- weigh upon us, in so much as we lack. "The pressures of which our motion relieves; as all activity removes what is otherwise intrusive upon inacitivty (indolence)."
It seems not a weak assertion that all of your actions stem from your core; your heart if you will, the bend of your back, the tilt of your bones.
 As such, I see much fear and conflict, while also patience and joy.
 But the manner in which I conduct myself is not of mere acceptence, 'as to walk in stiff shoes', but to pay attention, notice, and as if an aim of its own, adjust.
 There is a great deal which I recognize as causing me to be poorly pitched. Or, better said, I am aware of what being well-pitched is; as I see our angst melt, our labor endured, our heart warm and at ease, and our activity ripe and plenty -and lacking contrariety. The point is not to bludgeon what contrasts this, but merely pronounce your abscence of such goods, and the relations which spurred so. For we see, upon indulgences and consumption, that awry effects occur. Equal to this, are normal appointments and or expectations, for which we feel nerves about. Only, in that case, the condition, or stimuli, is likely just, and by that, worth our submission. "Is working out hard? Well sure, but that is part of the process; and of the good awarded, so is such effort worthwhile."
 Wisdom is thus the compass which orients us, as to properly state what we must endure, or accomodate ourselves towards.
 The basis for our lives begins then, for reason and sense bear harvest hear; as to say what is positively needed. Like our ill-affects, for which we attend in as much as they are not what we prefer, that so of our course and deeds, will we remark that which is unfitting, in as much as its point mirrors not.
 I find, in this process, where we asceratin our duty, that we feel the heat from other provocations, so mcuh as our ceratinty isn't accomplished. I am humble though, that our quest for knowledge and right is a long one, and perhaps but a habit of progress; and as such, we are better kept by our morals and attitude, as not to be sunk in our unceratinties. For withotu any real standing, or goods from our wisdom so far, then we are vulnerable to all other things certain; things which bring envy and aversion, dryness and refutation. It is akin to one's ego, who, all built and protected, feels the force from others upon their protuding lapse. The reason being, that like a body which is vital, and can take the punches and regain composure, that so are those armoured up, 'or flimmsy in their quest for something alternative', that they lack any natural force, by which they may assimilate the offerings of others --of which there are many sorts.
 In that way, one's life is split in two: the manner of progress, whereby certainty and right are gained. And propiety; or that care and sense which forms one to be in proper motion, despite any clear bearing. For we may lack knowledge of the Gods, and otherwise be unlearned of the many acts of Man, but we can still be honest and punctual, prudent and kind. We've at least learned that much.
It seems a judgement must be made. We are wandering otherwise, unclear nor checked, without an end to bind us; whereby we may adjust, be grateful, endure, win.
 But making this judgement is the heart of all our reserve. For we know not what we are to judge. It is one thing, for instance, in our plain and common way, to adhere to it and find better ways to be. Only, who is to say this is the way, or what way it is even? That is the problem. Not that we lack an anwser, but a question.
 To begin, it aids us to speak the truth of things; for of family and comfort, so to the constance and joy we seek, will we have to amend. Our inquiry may lead us down the path of the Gods, or that of natural philosophy. But our effort here is one of observation, and as such as to admitt the mountain of insight and or beleifs we already have. In that, it is clear as day that more days will come, as will work and free time. This may suggest oppertunity, as if you may conjure your efforts to cultivate some asset, as to procure industry and wealth. But I say that this is inferior to being mature. For in that, we see not that there is contrariety and or vanity in this, but rather an abscence of finer goods.There is a balance for sure, as we all may strive for the crown, 'but as we admitt the needs and or cares nearer and dearer to us, so does this want become bare and or daunting'. None see legal professions while their pursuing that in medicine. It is too hard to humour such an adjacent feat. Nor do we care.
 What we want is a balance of life, one which is full of deep yet firm breathes, calm and steady moments, a lush vibrance to our day. I say, upon much observation, that wisdom is a source for guidance, as it says, within the rythms of our life and way, what is neccessary, pardonable, oppertune, and merely exotic and fanciful. There is mcuh which can be desired; and our experience through life will determine both our tastes and stock of goods. Personally, I have not garnered much friendship in my day, and so what I see and delight in ahead, is not based so much on the friends I have.
 The point being, that of what is needed, and what you maturely and dearly seek, so is the admittence to this your soul regard. For it happends, that much of what is asked of us is set up and ready for us to attend. We need only be punctual and oblige. All we then can do, is ordain ourselves to this address, and keep ourselves bouyant enough as to respect it and thrive, as we can at least, and let our wants and wishes come from this responsible state. What we see and or gleam as oppertunity is at risk of being un-wise, in that it is not something which in our steadyness we see and acquiese to, but rather something which strikes in our state of burden, and shines as a sort of relief: a promise if you will.
 By that token, the core of our life is in the proper balance of living well as it amounts to now; and in that aligned motion, feel the grace of what is present and may come.
 Foremost amongst this admissions, is that all that holds us in place is the rise and fall of the sun. Over time,
 buildings may rise, as may trends fall. But at heart, all that really binds us is the rythm of the sun, coming higher and higher based on your hemisphere, and so forth to the low. I say this, for, of the good we seek, ought it befashhioned as to exist in this state. For the provisions you need will not cease, nor the duties to reap them. And as such, so will this earthly motion hold that routine of work and leisure, rest and satiety. Principle to this fact, is that moments and or experiences are not to be heralded. For they will either fracture coming stretches of time, or be a result of some passion or whim, for which the approach is all too engrossing, only for the peak to pass; and more want to come. The best things grow, and cost us not. The issue is not this or that or anything other, but that we can contine, as if in a consistent state of prosper. The fact is, that we will always get up, eat, tend to our self and tasks, then feed, mozzy and sleep. It is a real fallacy to forsee some moment which consumes this norm, 'as if, on some vacation, these common motions will be gone, or unimportant. That, or they will be fashioned with finer appeal'. The error though, is that of the current which you wish to abate or puff up, so will it remain, and, upon that fancy moment, continue in its plain stead. Indeed, we may value oppertunity then, as regards the good of spending, as to make these basic aspects of our life finer. But so too does this hold consquence. For of the pleasure and fun that deep pockets may bring to breakfast and bed, so are you still subject to the matter of health. And as such, any effort you produce, as is unabated by spending, or encumbant to you having such means, that so do the garments of such spending tend to produce contrarietes. Moreover, of those baser currents, 'who, in the case of our everyday use of a kitchen, draw us to have it apolstered with granite and steel', that we overlook, and in some cases prevent, our view of the higher currents, where the our literary and visual love lies. I mean not to say that people lack these charming sights. Only, that of what I often see, and at the very least considering what I personally seek, it is a great harmony in our routinely basics and those things we love. Only, I want the latter to abundant and of the kind which creates strenght and peace. Of the former, that it was understood as just and achieved simply; and also done in ways which are sufficient, rather than being full of superfloous grough.
 So, one of the main elements of vanity is our aesthetics. We care so much for health, yet often make choices that bear thesmelves from vain pretense. We seek big arms and a chest, and as such, to be noticed for that. My sense tells me that you feel alone, as you really are, without the false confidence that your looks are drawing notice. In any case, this ought not guide you. It is true that if you put down your arrogance and impotence, and simply exercised and ate as was appropriate for your flourishing and health, then you would hold a physique that was not shame worthy. In part, the less than elaborate appearance of it would coincide with our seperate direction. But also, it would remark the fact that we were not attending to such a goal, nor noticing if by coincidence even that we were of such prowess. The fear may be real, nor the abscence of pity good enough to not feel the loss of our boistering and confidence. This is a matter of armour and strenght though. Don't let it get in the way of supporting yourself.
 Secondly, is the matter of food. Nothing sits so deeply as a point of concern. For all things in some way or other sprout from your nourishment, and also aim for it: as all pecuniary and or primtiive matters point towards the maintence of food. But it also causes much grievence; nor much freedom upon any intial wisdom. "As prior choices can leave one hooked, so to speak; and especially bitter towards wholer foods, as your pallete is stained." That is the practice though, and the reality of one's situation only begs a broader sense as to deal with it. Were speaking of rules, a post to pivot around. Foremost of these principles is the allowance to eat. So much do we overlook the fact that food is being needed and sought; and we then succumb to our interest by buying things of an indulgent and or exhorbatant sort. We pass by the very harvests which are near and plenty. Nor do we match this fact with the effects that the suns ryhtm provide. In the summer, when the days are long and hot, eat the mountains of fruit that can be cheaply got. During winter, the stakes are lower; asking that you be more prepared, as your bound to eat more ruffage and grain --and must thus prepare. The point is, that the right choices are there; and missing the mark not onnly parches you from exercise, and thus your admittedly fair despair at being gaunch, but so does your attitude and directiveness whither, 'for which comes indulgence and or strickedness'. One thing I'd add, is that, by your family pedigree, or general circumstance, that some scenarios may be more forgiving. And what I'd say, is that indeed, like employment, it is good to invovle yourself in ways that are present outside of a forgiving situation; and as such, ought we not be exhoborident in our spending, as if such means will always be a given. But, considering the smoothness that comes from our own living, so may we accomodate ourselves slightly by the resources at hand. We are deciding to be hear after all, and as such, the shifts in how we partake in our days and time, gives allowance to use the resources that run with such concessions. Consider particular foods and meals, their timing and prep. But overall, make sure you eat enough, and moderate yourself properly if circumstance has you outside your lane. As with exercise, do what is fair, considerign your lack, but what is also worth getting, as to come back to your lane, 'rather than rashly overt from your lack --particualry if those aversions are mundane and or vain'.
So our days follow similar complexions, differing only in proportion. Obviousy there is the core form which holds us to our will. And then there is what fills this. We always watch youtube, some sort of tv show or film, play some games, read some books, write, make something, walk, stretch, scooter, relax and eat. These are always here. But our forgoing sentiment is one of understanding, as to see the nature of this continuous way, and how it is not despairing nor worth grievence that you'd amend to it well, if that means submitting yourself to adjustment.
 For work comes in, as does the weather, the novelty of somethinig new. But our general condition is what orients us to pitch each factor, as to be respectfull of our state and how it can recuperate and newly thrive; and all things, that perhaps are secondary to this fact, bear harmony to such fact. As we may wish to rest more, eat more and at different times. Our sleep is generally early, but it may ned to be earlier; and we can consider the sun's way, and, out of practicality or interest, alter our activity as to better meet our sleep.
 As it were, determining how we should adjust is the heart of wisdom. For we must see the whole of our lives, as not to miss out on essential positions --for which, in abscence, we ought not rest, nor meld into our current sufficiency. We ought see the lay of the land, our aims creatively, the tamber of our muscles and bones, the holes needing sowing. And then there is what we have to do, in the week ahead, or the current day. In short, it is about doing well enough, by virtue of tailoring yourself as best you can now, as to then grown and learn what else we should do; and thereby find a steady relation with the bounds of our life. Right now, that breadth has come to peace, in terms of God and natural philosophy, our employments, and nearly that of family value. What lingers is that of civil justice and or right, and the queer idea of our life being bade by judgement and maturity. Of the former, so do we lack much sight and care for the world, and are thus imposed by those who'd announce things happening; but all while we do not judge, nor act, and feel both incredulous envy and or spite. Moreso, we just don't know, so even if our life lacks due course, so our ignornace acts like a wound for incoming provocations to infect. "It is the fact of living in a modern world." Of our life then, so are we somewhat distant and seperate, as we cannot firmly connect nor be present; as our lack of postivity, in what we so care for, makes it hard to relate to others on such level; as we do not bring our side of things forth. Nor, if we lack ease and satiety in our own affairs, will we be friendly or open to others. Which is then stiffening, and unnerving -as we feel awkward.
So, creatively, the first thing to mind is the simple task of changing GT vids to non dash ---so we don't see adds when we pause. This should be quite simple.
 Primarily though, I feel it is a priority that we figure out cross device saves. We may work on other things, but I feel that being unable to type my notes, or speak in the opposite case, drowns out my interest in using something. We may start simple, figure it out, and apply this to our collection and new apps. As for those apps, I mean to do two things. One, is an update to the collection --which we have implented in a prior draft-- where we can select parts of the text and leave a toggleable note. My sight on this, is one where the arrangment of pieces is made more deliberatley; while studying each piece is where we solidify our experie nce with a piece. Cross save will allow for us to use the table and voice record. Second, is a study app, that allows for some manner of section making, such as Phantom menace or clouds, and a way to make different kinds of notes. The aim of this is to remove the lifeless addition to our main writing, in that we add a sentence or two. A place to study is one where we gather facts; which is waht studying is about. I main writing is where we actually write, and give our thoughts on things. Cross save is less critical here. Lastly, to note, is our task app, which aims at letting us ponder and plan what there is to do creatively, health wise, work wise and chore wise. Our first go at this had a task list, which we could remove each item, and a thought list, which would add things on top of one another, and was quite good to use. I found though that I had much to say, and very little task assignments. For I had notes about things and things I wished nto say, and so I ended up making thoughts for those. There is the issue of the thought part, where the add button goes to the bottom, and overall, I feel overt from using it as it seems cluttered. I reckon an actual task section, or to do one, would serve better, while the other sections were about considerations. Moreover, let the things in your life which benefit from this be what arrange the app. Our food inventroy for instance, is working well, and needs only a note input above the list of needed items. Bringing this to Superstore was great, and was easy to make. And I feel the benefit truly. This may be but the moment, but little things like that make a difference. As the fct of the matter is, that if there is friction or loose placing for something, we just end up not doing them. Our trail mix jar is an exmaple of this, as is our side table --which has cleared up my room. Bringing gloves and buffs into my room makes pure sleeping more accessible.
As for health, I see that there are great compliments yet to be discovered. Doing runners lunge and or warrior is great and easy to do; as you get the benefit from its mere completion. I am finding that short little sessions make for the tamber in our day; while moderate sessions, and certainytly guided ones, make for more dedicated moments. These though fashion what you do on your whim. Standing arm folds have become a pure staple, and make for much openenss in our back and shoulders. This was born from morning stretch, and we've made a pure standing one as to further bolster this. Crucial amongst these is donward dog. I find myself not wanting to do it, as i bear down to much, espeically in up dog. Doing a vinyasa will bring me through the motions, so that I do them more naturally and often. I am thinking of a parallel to our low lunge and warrior; what comes to mind is forward fold, which is not something I truly do. Arm glides just happen clearly and easily, while folding does not. And you need that to get into your lifts. We might benefit from a folding practice; that, or pay attention to what your doing, try and learn a bit as to rexongize how to do it well, and find the insights and familiarity through your vinyasa. In addition, core work feels desparetly needed. Our natural lift may allievate the need, but we are not always there. And this is in some sense a last line of defense. I am unsure about which exercises, but bridge stands out clearly, and boat pose feels like something which can occur with similar reason and ease. I think the third compliment is some sort of crunch, or knee raise, as to trigger our abdonimlas. These feel crucial to do, if only because were absent otherwise.
So one of the things which can aid us, is in being simply honest about our efforts. If we've squated and need our legs to adjust, there ought be some form of, 'were stretching, recoverying, aligning, preparing for work, keeping ourselves ready and good'.
 In that way, it benefits one to see the grounds which they've come from. If we go and do squats now, we may do them well, but likely a) not as good as you could, and b) by that natural riggedness, or force, and your prior unacquaintence, so will the assimilation be awry. This is natural, and perhaps where virtue comes in. For the appropriate gains of exercise, in that we earn the benefit of having moved, lasts for some time. As much as our bones,muscles and joints are mobile and swell, I have peace. And so, I once your out of this 'next to nothing' state, then your subsquent sessions are more like maintence. This allows you to do them when it is fit. But, albeit unknown to me, it is prduent to be honest, in that, of your motions and your current state, that if your going to sink it off tilter, than it begets you not to play or work excessively. This is a crucial element to our day; and respecting that calm neccessity, is in pervue to the basis of our lifes rythm. As such, if consilation, comfort and or explanation help, then do so. As it were, your actions are being brought upon periods and or ages before you, and as such, through this and that circusmtance, 'guided by virtue or vanity', so are you going to bear a certain effect. And accmodating that is the fairest decree; for you will at least benefit through the days to comem, and thus to the more productive efforts which will bring your rythm into a state of vitality and grace. Practically, you may find more time in your days for rest, be it in warm comfort, mid day sleep, earlier eating. I will add, that the way you sleep is so important; and the means to it is in how you enter. If you don't walk, forget about it. But, you may lie on your back, or even go prone, and let yourself rest, recover and reset, then go for a walk. Applying this may allow for earlier sleeps, as you can, by yoga, effectively rest and recover, and then go for a walk; and perhaps be up, reaady, limber, for some reading or proper stretching. "Stop, then rise, and your body wont resist coming to the real sleeping stop, for your day has already ended."
So a few things come to mind as incredibly usefull. For one, our note app should have titles centered, as to meld together and form a wall --which we can widen as more come. Then, for each subject, you have more or less a series of lists, each with a title that is lined up with other titles, and toggling a list below. The order of things seems irrelvant, and the means to arrange items -despite them being html. It would be nice to have the toggled title on the left, with a plus sign beneath it. Generally speaking, each item ought have a checkbox, as to toggle an active status. Then, below the main title, is a row of symbols with functions, to delete for instance. And, position wise, the + button --which adds things on top.
 Second, is our core routine. I reckon that it comes very nicely before a squat session, or something stretch like, ie. a lunging session or deep squat session. You could even do it prior to runs or workouts.
I may indeed smell of privalage; be it in cirucmstance or my general life. But my fortune is not so exlusive when compared to many people around me; nor does my own particular scenario lack any consilations.
 I say this, because, of all the peace and joy in my heart, upon my easy life and sensible amends, that I still am burdened by the despair of others.
 As it were, the effect of my sympathy is played out upon the regard that there is any worth having. And I am usually in two camps about this, where I get a first hand look of grief worthy things, and of the person in question, so do I see both their suffering and strenght. I also am enlightened to the nature of their plight, rather than what I'd imagine, or gleam through imagery and depcitions. On the other hand though, are those inputs which provoke me, be it in depiction, or in how they judge the world. For if others hold some sort of claim, or point to things, it triggers this expectation of conflict; as if they are inciting a fight. When I hear others talking of issues, painting some figure as a despot, or even the remark of depravitiy abroad, I am not concerned of the subjects their talking about, but rather that the fact that they are talking colors my mind as if that is the manner of folk around me; or at least where things get done; and as such, conflict seems expected.
