 Oddy, this provoking does not extend to places outside the context of the provocation. When someone in the states speaks ill of the president, I am not driven to see any relation to South Sudan. My attention gets drawn to America, as if were encaged in that arena.
 I consent to the fact that what is proper to know and what is the right way to see such, is unclear to me. The idea that there are folk who's finacnes are sagging or flat, and by the prominence of the economy, is not irrelevant compared to the actual lives of these people, 'who day by day, breathe by breathe, are their onw', so is our understanding and skepticism (so much as we do not, or would not, understand) not the soul retreat for our us. At the very least, if someone is rightly or not deemd a pundant, or a worthy informant, or guide, so ought our own thoughts and judgements be privy to the reasoning and or evidence they provide; nor ought we overlook the extent of that knowledge. Better said, the truth we are given, or gleam by those more learned and invovled, ought be hoisted by us in a way that reflects what was actually given, rather than running above that knowledge, 'as if a flame beneath us'. My feeling is that, if you held enough knowledge and sense, that your reaction would not be so heated and sure; and thus you'd be more or less weighed down, by merely exclaiming the basis for what you have to say. Judgements made upon these ground are solid and unvehement, as their substance comes from stating all the inspiring parts. As many as we lack, or see unknown, so will our judgtement be so less forward bent. And as clear as we are, so will we be kept by those points, rather than leaving them to fall as we clatter around, by our emotion.
I'd say, that our confusion, dredd, irriation and what not, come down to a dear understanding of things. There are concepts, or at least happenings akin to the weather; but the experience of each person is different. This is hardest to know. And of all things good, ought we not pretend like exotic excursions removes them from the basics of life; nor in grueling pain, ought we pretend like this consumes their life. But, of what we presume, or at least imagine, 'as in some sensible notion about people in a country', that so the more we have of this, can we make better and bolder statements. For so long, we've been under the guide of things being present; yet we knew them not, only that they were there. That there was this or that, yet, of its inhabitants and way, we could not speak of. "We new the diagram, but not what was created." As it were, preachers, teachers, journalists and or hosts, spread information about the world. It may be enough, too little, mistated, or angled even with meaning. All it can do though, is simmer the judgements of people, or their grasp on something; and those who within official positions respond to this, out of the manner of their expectation, and so forth to how the position is filled. But, where my humour lies, is the matter of effects, and how this is emboldend upon people, 'who deal with it on the other side of the haze; presumably as we know anyone to deal with anything, and of how we percieve the things being brought to them'.
What do I want. In truth, a friend, a woman, someone to inquire with and relish Star Wars. I might find this, or something other, in community, be it drop ins or yoga. We did something like this before, with coffee chats and church. But there is literally a yoga venue right on by; and we've the means to attend.
 It is never a hinderance to remark upon what you have. For one, my family is a delight. My father is old and stirn, yet very open, bright and funny. My mother is charmingly goofy, easy to talk too, and a general warmth. My sister is very funny, asks me for little, and is resptecable to me. Our two cats are no slouch either. I would not rate this part of my life higher than what is below, but my two close friends, Tristan and Matt, are good fellas to game with or talk about. Our work though houses a harvest of acquaintences. Teresa is an old darling, as is Kim and Carry, and Carli moreso. Andi is a bud, as is Alex and Joey. Aman, Sam, Tera and Amit, are all sweet and kind, and very easy to talk to. Throughout the store there's this sense of commradery, from Joe to Lene. The work we do is important, as the goods on offer must be made avaialble to those wanting to buy it. Encumbant to this, are the folk who've committed themselves, for the moment at least, to do this. Better said, there are people who have built there lives here, like Amber and Ann. Their not going anywhere. Even if friendship doesn't cross our side, so do we have their support, as fellow workers. And their my brethren, as I enstate myself as part of the team, for the work is imperative for them. We are at least a part of our community and colleuges.Then there's me though, someone who is no burden upon us, but rather a swell and merry man, full of natrual riches and warmth, inebriated to nothing but peace and plenty. Around, there are trees and flat streets. Our main road even, is angled as to see the western dusk. A field rests near its far edge, full of grass and sight. Around, are close paths and routes, to keep our feet moving, and more or less pleased as we see the south and northern above. My means are mechanical, but so the roads offerith. And to their local edge, lies fields and rows, of which all directions gleam. Mountains of varied sorts sit in the norht, with Baker in the east. The sky, so high, collects all the setting light, and that of the warming day. And the clouds, so many, that even a sheltered view would show their puff. And around here, are neighbourhoods for travel; from hammond and Haney, to Fairview and Yennadon. Far out, on the swiftest bus, lies Merry Hill and Hyde Creek, with the Coquitlam woods near. And further, lies Barnett Bay, and Roberts Edge further about. "The woodlands of Burnaby heights and Vancouver sit on the final stretch, till theres not but the seas scent."
People and pleasure comfort one, though the former brings one security; while the latter as it were, pleases one, but does not settle them into life, as if they were fine; and thus shielding other efforts and or intrusions.
 My experience with that more settled good, is quite low. In my later youth it came about, but soon fell flat. I found myself somewhat detached from things, feeling as though all that something had to offer was not enough, considering I was hollow. I would then yearn to feel so full and secure; and upon one who sparked such sight, I'd succumb to day dreams and attraction.
 Oddly though, through my life prior, I had always been averse to such things. People, over time, would show interest in me, and I would overt. It seemd as though I woudl have to change, give myself a little and thus be out of my norm. This, I deem, felt off, for peace and stability is what I cared for, and felt off upon lack. Of school, sports, chores and expectation, the cease of worry hear had me feeling more or less settled, and able to enjoy the pleasures at hand; in part, because the duty never ceased, only our harmony with it. Bethrothing myself to Taylor or Pam, felt like I was stepping aside from this steady lane; and at that, as my young mind deemed, more would have to be done by me, in terms of money; for which my lack meant I'd have to ask, and I did not wish to have that relation with my mom and dad --as I felt embarressed to be so open with them, and pathetic to require it.
 No matter, I was otherwise fine; surely missing, and victim to an adjustment I knew not I could make. But all in all, my harmony was good. Only, this manner of harmony slid into dissaray, as our efforts could not only be mozzying and sufficient; as the issues at hand were not simply placed in front of me to oblige, 'and then go outside and play', but rather be elected by me, for some independance or ambition. It may just have been time and chance, but having more or less dipped into this side of things in my later years, the admission to friends and relations was appealing. For whatever reason, upon that failure, I was left in a more forseen and heavy lane, but lacking the goods which one with such weight acclimatizes towards. I found drugs quite consiliating; and our standards, as refelct that lane of broader duty, felt easier for me to slump; which did not sit well with my parents, who's communication about to me, did I well recieve. After reconciling that, we amended to do something with our life. We went to college thus. Yet, our actual care and aim was absent. I enjoyed the study, but not the purpose. Indeed, had I more of what one on such path deems good, in friends and elation, than perhaps I would have felt more supported, and thus more hopeful and cheerful about what the future could bring; for which our education was a means to ---a reason to take it seriously. But we lack this, and thus fell burden to apathy.
 From this point on, we aversed ourselves from this feeling of apathy, and embalmed ourselves with a personal quest. The matter was propiety and leisure, born of such, and the manner was of devotion and credulity. This never brought me friends, as such kinds of devotion were, in either attitude or content, uncomparable, or shared at least, by any. Only my friend Justin aligned with me. Yet he, over the years, fell wayside to this. As my lackings were of people, so was his of fortune; or at least the broader ventures born brought through ambition. I felt that, if women and company were by ourside, than the riches of our time would be in check. While he, I felt, considered his efforts less than they could be, in regards to finances. I reckon he was impressionable, a little rot by other's expecations; nor as embalmed by any inner strenght. So we parted ways.
 Where I lie then, is upon a decade of this personal fashioning. Where all the duties I have are held with might and sense; and feel thus like reasons for living, rather than burdens. Our balance of this, as would proport our industry and time, is finely yet vitally woven, and thus the matter of such craft is buoyant.
 There is a piercing string to this though, ladden upon those other threads. Simply, when we were younger, embalmed to our personal path, we felt a mixture of envy and shame, and the thought of the structure and comfort we lacked, and the ambition we were unprivy to. In the most bare and obvious sense, to things rather far off and abound, the chains wore off. We communcated our feelings and care, and grew habition to such thoughts, and experience with them through the world. We learned what they needed, and how they could fit together with such asks. And our appreicaiton of flame which grew, fooled me little into thinking that, of something so personal, credulous and true, that we were warmed, crisp, steady, light.
 The error in my life is one of decision making. For you see, the gravest effect of this personal living is in the practical consistency required, and the sensibility to amend this; but, this sense, as if the matter of our life's direction, is hard to make; upon yours or others ignorance, 'for which many sparks have flied'. And so, as it often happens, we go on our own, seeing things as we are pleased to, and with belief of. Our ignornance though sees that contempt follows, and upon our freedom, to really think, we feel that our life is more open than my ideas and care can fit. And so, the question of our life becomes ever so present; and the weight of not anwsering becomes a burden. We generally resort to propiety; and in the recent years I merely have this posture of progress. But so do we feel like were brought away, from what is suggested and or expected of us, in who we could have company with, or have profession. It all relates to that moment at Douglas, where the lack of friendship and joy, had me unkeen to any promise or need from such pursuit; and thus it seem'd feeble, and but an expected motion; and by the amount we thus cared, apathy ensued.
 Indeed, this feeling of apathy, brings with it that eleemnt of credulity. For, in our strenght, we see skill and craft, resounding merrit. This spurrs in us a feeling of anticiaption, as if we are going to be seen or rewarded; or, as our later years have come, recognized for the enlightenment in our words; for which we bear some sort of glorious salvation. Oddly, this feeling was once entwined with our sense of shame; as the expecation which brought thought, felt curbed by our tenuable dreams. "The good things we lack will come." While, in the years that followed, we grew less a stake in such thought, as it really remarked our envy, in that, the idea of living as we would without such glory and claim, had me feeling pitiful when compared to others who do. This ceased feeling laudible. But, as we then settled into our progress and real care, we found a great insight into the world; that of the impressions which were right and fair, that so the world seemed less evil and of malice, nor as damnig and chaotic. "Much of our thoughts, as were weighed down by the feeling otherwise, or the pronoucements by others, who merely exclaimed the world to be along those lines, felt full of fallacy's, nor much breadth in what they exclaimed. False, no; insightfull, but a glimmer."
 Here then I stand, lacking those things which, in their nature and breadth, sing to me, bring life to my heart, 'as if there enromity, of which I am so lucky and right to owm, or expect at least, fills me with easy breaths'. Normally, this is what I exude, or at least conjure to less than mighty things, but a spattering of bits of pieces, each of a delectable sort. Only, this brings with it a sort of protuding, in our literary interest for instance, where we assign some farther worth to them, or at least value to us for doing it. Nothing beats the warmth of another, nor the union of literal hearts. And so, of our industry, propiety and care, do we embolden it when and if we slouch. "For there is nothing to catch us, but our own back; and the pull of others, or feeling their there, is what brings you out of that dip." And my current quandery is merely so, that of the reverence I placed on knoweldge, the insight of a view which I felt more than average, but great, has left me only feeling, that the meaning of this, the obejct, the matter, is not so definite or clear. That of the glit and glamour of it all, the nobility and worth, that it is really just some weather in my mind, overcast above our normal feelings and satiety. It draws us forth, acts as a motivator, in that it feels right, and that we are privy to the best it can offer. And, indeed, pittering thrugh this course, has brought with it a truly great sense, a sort inquiry and care that not but our unfamiliarty can refute. But it's created a standard, of recognition that is, where all things in the world are as such recognized. Horrible things notably, as we dream or know. But, there is a drawn out plan of this, as if, progressively, we merely further this sense, grow it, have it spill over. Yet, the manner in which it would ever take form, beyond our ease upon just having it, and fashioning how we scurry through the day, is such that it breaks upon the insistent portrayals of drama, and the equally insistent exposes from media. "The world it seems, is here, happening, and ontop of events already happened." It feels so hard to live a wonderful life then, where truly great and remarkable resolve and thinking is done; when your asked to see and or adhere to foreign belidgerences now. So hard is this to assimilate; and, of how we know of things, and the properties they hold, 'for which true referene and intercourse can produce', upon this rolling away, through senitments and or imagery, are we sincerely disputed; for we are not, of what is said, or how we see it, doing anything. Were just mozzying along, accumulating brilliance and insight: our issue, is that, as far as we know, of some manner in which the dream of promulgation is realizes, that so is our lacking of this, an ember to this burning flame, 'of the feeling that all our intent is for not, for our actions are as such, not so'.
So my name is Ryan, and should be enough. But, when I see a hurt dear, a baby crying in the street, someone suffering, I am not pleased. There is likely something deep and causal to this, but near the surface, I feel the reason is that we have no one around me to bring some sense, reaffirming the fact that people are strong, nor villanous, and that we are no different, and thus in need of little fear of something similar happening to us.
 Our judgement is easily the farthest thing from complete. As we know not the truth. Because of this, we fall victim to fallacies, of everyone appearing together, and thus being entirely well off or not. The truth is, that some people are worse off, and many are less so. I find this hard to admitt; and, in effect, stirr uneasily at the thought of living as well as I am, and like many others; even if there are those, spattered about, who are not so well. "Its as if we went our way, enjoying it, dealing with the trouble at hand; which is in some sense lucky, or to be grateful for, that the woes are of toil and common illness, and not belidgerence, torment or malaria like disease." And we see so many people around me, who share in luck of being in Canada, the developed world, 'and on the grasses of it, for some still live in poverty and slums'. So, in sharing our good, we somehow feel enligthened, as if there is so much good, that this shines out and of those darker shades.
At any rate, we are to return to a deliberate lifestyle; one which is determnied in its eating, exercise, and sleep schedule. We may reflect on what things need, in terms of the daytime, portions of sleep, siestas during the day. I feel strong in our yoga, but weak in our formation. Squats feel important; and despite feeling awry from the pushups prior, I know that this is from sub standard form. I find that there is a node of effort, be it one day or back to back, even with rest. But you have to incorporate. The matter of your training, your manner in doing so, does not exclude it in relation to work. Its more about the effect youll bear, and how work can tangle itself in that. Thats all that needs done. We'll try and finish our work; as it will upset me not to finish, but also erode us if were doing everything else. Lets go over our files, for organization sake; figure out firebase, whether in pure add on to our current app, or remark the note app with firebase installed --i thought it'd be easier to add on, with some things already going. PWA is enough for me; though i would like to invovle firebase. We can hopefully get that working. It is fine though for now, considering the primary use on our desktop
The absolute force of nature is perhaps to much to bear. For, a dear sits in its groove, happy upon the abscence of threats, in dissmay when present. Man though, in all his reason and season, is not so different, in that, with enemeies and famnie approach, he frets; and equally, is he at ease when absent.
 His distinct feature, in industry we'll say, is all to the effect that his ends are regularly met, and thus his stability is not but a moment he's glad to be apart of; but something consistent, at least often.
 It is a basic anthroplogical point that upon this content, he looks around, finds things that strike his unoccupied mind. In far ways, so does art and mysticism arise; of the old world, so comes metalurgy and writing.
 And what is most odd, is that for some folk, the green is overhwelming in the pasture. Some are just so well off, in lands that are full of plenty and lacking in threats. And so, to this modern world, are their continents, where accidents and off crimes are the only terror. There is no belidgerence or superstition, no torment.
I realize, that my objective, or course, is to merely attend to the right things. There is a confusion about this, but this is more or less fine.
 By that, it would fair us well to address how we can be, properly and alight, when it comes to over studying. For we can burn out from it, and loose ourselves when it comes to eating, exercise, leisure and sleep. Or, if we do all these, the burn is even worse.
 As in all cases, give yourself time to rise and set. Dabble then, getting a hang of what you'll do that day, then later on, having walked, showered, eaten and stretched, go. An hour or two going into noon and later into the evening, seems fine for our efforts. Sometimes something may not be working; that is your cue to retreat, and come back to it when you can take full strides. Consider the workplace, and how you might come back with a full upload to GPT.
The most contemporary certainty, is that, of what we knnow, so is our claim on that a farce; and as such, considering that reason, or demonstration rather, relies on pre-existing knoweldge, as to both a) bind some property to the elements in such equation, and b) indicate, or at least posture the fact, that they exist; and thus, of the reasoning, it is real, relevant, true. Yet such pre-existing knowledge is no-where to be found. The identity of all things is progressing, including the until of late misconcieved notion of mind. There is nothing which, ala Hume, that you can imagine or see, that is not built up of your personal apparatus. You have seen color, but you've not seen true x-rays. Representaitons of these, as in the collquial X-Ray machine, are but nets to catch the mysterious form of light, and orient it in some analgous way. Pictures work similarly, in that they are nothing but caught light, held in place by a chemcial. "As wine does upon linen." I remark upon Hume, admirably, and reference B.Russel, who exlaims, 'amongst criticisms of Hume's other proposals', that the notion of association has not to this day been parlayed. All contemporary science has done, is make the compositions which we associate, or combine associatively, less confusing; in terms of what we are actually seeing, 'as in our intake of light', and as such, how; in virtue of what, in this case, light is, and our appartus' are.
 I feel that the assembly of all stimuli is lacking only the matter of self-regard, the epitamous source for our thinking that in our mind, lies something other. But I reference the fact, that of a triangle in your mind, or an object who's two sides, at some generally square angle, create another longer portion comparable to the hypotenuse; and that, similarly, that red, as imagined, holds in it properties of red we feel to more sensually experience, that so in the case of motion, that ala the relation of sides and the encumbant hyptonese, 'that one, in their mind, can not imagine or concieve of a square sphere', that so in our very regard, for which we intuit some sort of relation, 'akin to how we relate to seemingly real things', is not in any reasonable sense, something more than our regards being of a motion, and by that inhibited with properties, of all manner of sorts, as remark and or equate to what is real, in the way that the content of our perception indicates. This isn't so much to say, or at least bite down on, that of a car in our mind, that it holds neccessary properties of a real car, but that, of our apparent view of the car, outside of sense but in our concieved mind, that so is that act of perception but an array of consequences, or relations, as would make us see that car with the properties that, upon sense, we'd see.
 The thesis then, is that, if we saw the car, and of such properties, by our eye's, that the impression got, or sense had, is by and from the governance of what were seeing and our receptivity; perception, or innner sense, is merely a different bend of existing mechanics, as to faciliate the comparable view of a real car, but less vivid (more or less): similar to how the input of light upon our eye's, provokes the experience of sight, 'as if we are drawn to composition, attracted by its every bit; for which, our impression, is in fact, as a composition, a granular response; for which, the experience, is one of awareness, or stimulation, by each bit: as to create a composed experience --as if one were to manually prod a single nerve, rather than touch your whole hand; for which there are merely more nerves'.
 All of this is to say, that of our view of a car in our mind, that so am I skeptical about this, as if it is in fact anything different than what we deem as really seeing a car. That is, the qualities of our experience differ, but not what has till this day been esteemed as something transcendant or worth demonstrating. "Literally, if the car were in haze, you'd see it differently, but still real. Your mind, perhaps a bit different in its process of sight, and surely of the qualities in what it sees, is as such a variance of that hazy reality."
 Indeed, the endowed statement about this, which, I'd say, in lacking, refutes all but who can make this statement, 'for all things identity fail consquently', is that, of your supposed act of percieving (innerly), and of the thing you perceive, that so is this all bound in ways that (beside quality) compare, and by that, are equally levelled, as the otherwise deemed reality. I don't believe in this, for of this statement I don't entirely know. But it is surely where I bend. For what I lack otherwise, is the knowledge of anything; and, what makes the subjects for all this so unknown, in their true lack of identity, 'considering what an identity ought be', makes me confident in the promise of rightly stating such identity, and thus bearing a truth to the false perceptions that were deemed known.
 I'd suggest that more will need to be described and or discovered. But, of that fairs solid bounds to an identity, considering the mass of experience we have, 'from light far off', that so may we at least fill out all of this input, and see it as best we can: as if, under the regal laws of identity, that we had filled out such measures with all we've observed: ready, to further find what would make all we have, better known.
So were home, set to work Saturady 630-1130, with some days off after that.
 I am not sure what binds us; but I do know that food is important to get and prepare. Exercise ought remain light, with some training additions onto the field. Otherwise, keep things limber, while we work. I'd say, that Friday is fine to do this, or something at least, for the day following is a fair follow through. I think the rule is that, when you do it, adjust your time to incorporate that. If this means work, or further sessins, great; but know when your done, and let things fall inwards, and recuperate, eat, sleep and play accordingly.
 For sleep, I found last night that 8 hours was fine and tenuable. Prior to our walk, it makes sense to do your yoga. You could literally fall asleep, get up from your pure rest, then walk, come back and sleep. Broccoli and beans were real nice, and I was not hungry at all today. Tonight, I mean to have quinoa, corn, a few lentils and sauce. Tomorrow, we'll have potatoes. It is more expensive, but the value is so high, for big smoothies. Their filling and nourishing at the same time; and great for the afternoon part of the day. Trail mix sits as support. And there is probally some room for chips, crackers, buns even. We've got some nice english muffins.
 For simplicity sake, I mean to redo my note app. It begins on the index, with a set of tabs that act as main entries, ie. Phantom Menace, Yoga. These open up a set of lists. Items within, interact with the button bar, which allows to delete; or you can collapse the items. Something I'm not sure about, is whether we should have 'movies' and put New Hope and Phantom Menace inside, or if each should be its own option. Truth be told, 'movies' does one a diservice. For I don't really want to do movies, but ceratin things which are movies. If we could collapse the main set when opening one, then it would be good.
 Surroudning these though are two apps: notes and food. Food is as it is now, but with some additions (margarine, blueberries), and the ability (in the red section) to add notes. Secondly though is the planner, which amounts to a day, in which you can add bits, then reset, and also remark on your intent. Simply, long form section, which accomodates work and things to do, a day planner, where you can say what needs to be done. And, again, its not about forming sides to fit yourself to, but putting the sides which remark the effort you have; and as such, our industry needs a section.
I think, that love and pleasure occupy us, and make us comforted enough to not otherwise see what bothers us below.
 For me, I find little which i am not willing to let go; and so i'm grateful, well off, and otherwise ripe with industry. But I strive for not, nor feel bound to anything. "What is that can be good, upon me, by my free stride, or amidst employment, so I see, appreciate: this is enough."
 Only, this sort of living, however warm and broad, 'like a cloud', is not like bounded love, where you have something permanent. In many ways, our marriage is to our heart and soul; for such candor and peace makes all good.
 Yet we lack fidelity here, 'as if we've been cheating on ourselves, with lethargy, buisiness and junk'. The effect, is that we are seperate from the main thing we have. Yet, we are still we, and like a memory, so the good lays. But much ardour and grough comes from this, for fear and peversion trickle in; filling in those detached holes. I find so much peace with the world; in that, of all things which it puts upon me, so are we not beheaved. The Gods no longer want upon me, in their fashion and esteem. I simply lack a strong belief; and lean otherwise to natural concepts. Of children and men, so are we giving, astute to the deeds, and fine by the right living; of which, friendship and grateful love would fall in line; but so are we not lacking this, merely allayed to the duty and merriment that would be added to. Our health is a chore; but I see, that as all ends have some good in mind, that of my efforts, are they for the vibrancy I seek; and to quote Epictetus, 'appreciate my success, in as much as that good is experienced; for were making true progress then'. "There is no pretense for what our health should be, in vanity. What we seek is the scale by which our means are bade by; rather than starting with a mean already in mind." And our liberty, progress, promulgation and delight, these sway in and out of our life's rythm, asking only that we keep our efforts (to this path) contained. Of our reflections and inquiry, ought it be to bolster our steps, to be near, present, mighty and tough, but also delicate, and with sense of what bears motion; and, upon reflection, persistence.
The simplest idea is that, of our notes, we have specific tasks. I'd like to have place to put such notes, so that I'd use it; but not so, that I'd find more things to make notes of.
 What comes to mind, recently, and more or less relates to all things of the past and to come, are the outlines of films and some of their dialogue, a similar treatment of books, which is to say, marking quotes, our writing of something. Study is important, in that we'd remark upon the sky. And all things use //// to denote titles and ---to show breaks in a thought. We may collapse items, and have multiple stacks per branch. I am not against having each film/book be its own branch. But we may benefit from a tight functioning item list, as to simply have Star Wars, then have the outlines for each film, each an item in a list. Then you may have Phantom Menace Dialogue. In most ways, I find myself using // and -- to make longer pieces, rather than splitting various thoughts.
So quickly, our food. We've got berries, but need margarin. We got milk, but need kale. We got 1 muffins and bread, no popcorn or potatoes. We need quinioa also. I am not sure what I need. I feel smoothie is great for the day. Unsure if I want simple or whole food tonight. I wouldn't mind a beans. Dont need the calories. Sleep wise, were up at 6 Saturady, no stress. Sleep is a bit strange, as ours last night got thinned out by going to bed out of fatigue. I slept well, but wanted to continue, and so slept in. There was no way I was up at 630. I felt heavy just walking, and resorted to go to be sonner. I feel 8 hours is a good amount, but consider whether your up and ready to go later, or fine to do so earlier. A solid sleep, of 8 hours, should make for a rise at such times. So, if you want to go to bed at 12, and up at 8 tomorrow, then groovy. So, if you want chips or crackers, go back tonight, and have em prior to 11.
 In short, margarine, muffins, kale, quinoa, beans --popcorn is for another time.
Help me lord, so mighty and wise. How do you fair with your brethren being burnt to a crisp, obliterated by cannonballs, tormented under scrutiny. Doth the wicked smile even need to present for this to be considered bad. Am I wrong to nuture my own strenght by removming the manner of these deeds; making them seem like things reasonably conjured and deployed, and thus nothing of evil or devilry. I have seen, as one ever could, the calm might of men, torn asunder, soon to be lit. There is seldom a comment of anything pithy or of complaint. There was once a joke even, of one torn in the stomach, who said, 'Doc, I've got me a tummy ache'. I can't help, but under the force of deduction, 'in which only the gods seem to exclaim', to feel nurtured in our own misery, of seeing such injury and plight, when the manner of gratuity, vileness, wickedness, slyness, burliness and love are removed. "Things so awful and damaging feel more like actions of a different stripe, when the evil that ones love for harming others is removed." My own anwser is that, we are not done, and thus in need of an ideal. Instead though, I simply proclaim that we are still going, moving through the world as its so long been. This is where the ideal comes from, in that we see whats good and bad, effective or not. But in moving, I see that there are those whose experience is one of harm and injustice. "Taken from their liberty; and even with kindness, given a time of pale and filthy strife." Am I to receive counsel, that we are ourselves under said condition, however varied in side. For nature has produced this lane of living, and delibered results by the way folk react. That is why we fight, find good in company, relief upon peace, and endurance upon unity. But there is still squabbling; and, I admitt nothing blatantly of the esteemed criticisms of modernity, only, that there are valid criticisms; more or less, to the effect, that by those more unified, that from others they have stolen. The balance of this needs fair delibery; for the use of African slaves in the west is not a complete enditment upon the effects of Africa. The developing status of these countries, in which the matter of the economy and allowance for belidgerent crime, is not so completely at the hands of the West. Their influence was there. "But so in times more recent than that of our own civil wars, were there bevies of slaves in Africa." Tradition brought with it the custom and discrimination, which would ease the hearts of westerners in their handling of slaves. This tradition was not exclusive though; and, as a near matter of fact, 'this stain is seen throughout the breadths and eras of the world'. And Africa held its own tradition, like Arabia, China, India and so on. The west were merely agents, acting from their own nest of tradition, in and to the nests of others. "Each were rooted though, and of a trunk, that permitted and exhibited such imperfect humanity --where any were considered property."" This is indeed an ideal criticism, but only in that it correctly points out that the application of law, whereby someone owned something, or held some status or stance against another, 'as a policemen, in a civil sense, has to an ordianry citizen', was wrong.
 We ought be able to marshal our criticisms as we would our views on natural science. "We ought not forget that their is a reason, beyond sentiment, that calls out the consquences born by slavery and tradition. That anyone were subject to that, no matter how strong they were, or but an ancient arrangment it was, so our sympathy lays. But, as these misdeeds are no longer present, ought we still deliver the conclusions of their effects. As one who saves money has more later on, so is the matter of time, whereupon chains were removed, a key factor in assessing the state of someone now. For they may simply have been injured prior, and unfairly." My point, is that, of those positioned in the world, so are they so from actions prior. From their family, their country, the aspects of the world, and the effects all this carries. Why do we fight? For preservation, to acquire, defend, assert, execute --in that, of a patent for instance, we must act on infringements in order to validate the claim we hold, as to legimitze the authority which we otherwise potentially have (the relaity of which, comes to be, in that, of the hindereance upon those who would otherwise acquire, so must the hinderence upon them have life, or an existence of threat: for which the hinderence is based on). The balance of this is not to be simplified, for well off people may tend to take less than those who are not; but so are there not simply well off people, nor countries. They are merely arranged; and, with proportions to all sides, for which they are, by each part and of the whole, famished or pleased, so do equally complex responses lay. The macro arrangment of things, for which macro activity occurs, to the micro strands within, that so the impetus for thievery, laundering, bribery, assault, and sometmes murder lay. Organized gangs are about the only esteemd sources for severe violence; and even then, this is more akin to the savagery of indigenous tribes; who, in custom, may be brutal and barbaric, but are not thus maniachal brutes or fiends. In fact, that word, fiend, is perhaps the oddest thing. For all may claim to know one, or that all are some; but what short of common day rough housing and or brawls do they know? I find, that what I feel to be bad, is in such fiendeshness, that, rather than the corporal deliverence of crushing ones legs, 'done by someone with a name, and with nothing known to them but by which you deem typical, or assume from some other sample', that we may picture him as a bully, and as such fiendish, whereby he doens't man the instrument, but rather scorns the one subject to it, egging him on, grinning if you will. This is awful; and I admitt, sourced mainly from the seperation we have with our dad. Who claims everything, yet, of serious matters ,which we, in our own studies and affairs, deem incredible in dramatic and or graphic sources; those of which, of such topics, he'd only know of them then. "And I fear the most, that he'd assert to me, in the sure and imposing way he does --which I, in my knowledge otherwise, innerly refute as brevity and cavalierness-- that such fears of mine are true, that all people are with fury in their deliverance of pain, and writhing for more, 'as if their fucking a whore, but in this case some helpless victim who they wish to render to a pulp; with fist, iron, heat and rage'. Forgive me lord, but I cannot, however drawn out and self driven, appreciate the grandeur I see, and be pleased by, to some extent incessantly, and ignore the reality in which I am thrown by. So much beauty, so little rape, I'm pleased by how much good there is and little bad. But I am caught by depictions which have me feeling like anything which is bad, is such, that of the manner, I lose relation with the broader world, 'and am isntead consumed, to see such malidies as being the mirror of the world, and thus a mark for its entirety'. I seek to only know more then, and see how fold die, deliver conflict, approach trouble.
The Gods have graced me, with the sense that we may not so much ignore them, but rest upon their mystery. For when one asks about my feelings of God, I say that I have none which are strong; and if they inquire as to my belief, I say that I lean naturally.
 This development in the recent year has brought much peace, as I for so longe ardoured over the question, as if to respond with something I knew. Only I could not fully state what I believed, as what I beleived was not full. What seemed so obvious I laundered over, trying to make true. But, somewhere along the way, I grew so near and dear to myself, that what I saw was all I needed, and was thbus not perturbed by any potential request.
 Similarly, in goverenance, our time was spent in militant defiance, that war and politics were conducted in any manner proposed. Again, my ignorance lie; especially in these cases; as I thought not only the weakness of those which triggered my thoguhts; but I knew nothign else. In the case of God, I found simple certainites about it, by virtue of the ease in which the natural tenants came to me. But in civility, I had no prior arrangmenet, and thus, as the Gods came infront of me, on our path, so did the rural world lay. I hated every depiction of this, mostly from film, but also from short form media. I knew it wasn't right, as I did have a solid standing in history. But I could not fill in the blanks; compared to the Gods, where I'd learend so much that I ceased to exhault myself, my bare knowedlge of the public world was easily consumed, in those depictions and provocations. The rancour of the former bleed me most; in some ways for yeats. So I started there, reading the journal of Robert Rogers; and early in, when he said he dispatched a sentry, who, upon approaching him, he offered quarters; upon refusal, and lunging, he dispatched, I knew, in my heart, calmy in my mind, that the wrongness of the depictions were no more an issue of mine: as the truth had laid its claim. "Our exholtion of putridity was dripping away." Later on, the same refusal came from governance; as I saw such, ultiamtley, as the breeding grounds for conflict; and in many ways, it blinded our otherwise human wiew. "Especially because it's in your face nature, was the foundation for my own parents persuasion." In that though, I am nearly able to breathe; for I a) have settled into our sight of things, in reading Barrack Obamas memoirs, shedding of that arduous mantle as we learned more about presidency, congress, secretaries and foreign relations. The media even, who, like my parents, held this scorn from me: as if they were pouring unbecoming mud over our bare bodies. "Now though, I run through it all, caring little for the mudy: the cheer of someone made."
My dad is going to die, someday. I used to revel in this moment, for the fortune it'd bring. I have no sincere aversion to death, so I pithy him not; and thus, I have no emoitioanl guard agasint seeing the developments which would occur.
 I think though, that of such changes, they are not worth much, as we would still only be more able to do as we please; and such want, is not so unavailable now. The draw of such endowments is in the ability to do far off and expensive things. Yet, where? To some place that will bear you no friends, nor meaning, no interest. Seemingly, this wish comes from what we once thought, and perhaps experiecn in little abruptions, that go far off is a cure for idleness; a whist of excitement.
 We are not in the land of promise though, rather one of concurrent effort, upon times unerring wind. We need nothing, for we are only lacking the fertility of noble effort; where the weight takes care of itself. "One with the water." The fortune ahead is right now but a token of us waiting, and the delights which will come upon us having done not. "Why imagine yourself to be then pleased, and instead worry about your time right now." For the dreams of change and promulgation come from our stilted process; and are merely the endevrous we can imagine right now. Surpass that, through posutre and mind, and your care for fortune goes away. "For we fear not having it yet, wishing for it, irking over whether our idea of it will come or go." Meet it, in a way that we care not for it. At least have a reason which it's resrouces can contribute towards
I recognize that our life would have more pleasure if we had more friends and relationships; as this would amount to more fun, trust, cooperation, romance even.
 Why this is not present is a hole other thing. But that we are empty without it, has just never been the case. I find myself regularly in a state of solidity, comfort and ease. The sky never ceases to delight me, nor my writing and study, arts and craft. Much of our young adult life has been spent cultivating this. And, indeed, many of our choices were made as to humour the aversions in this; or at least improbability of those pleasures being congruent. But, more than likely, we just didn't humour enough that our life, in the good way we found it, merely needed time to establish itself, and by that, draw other people in; or at least give me time to exhibit ourselves. This occured in our younger years, and that was so because our position in life was set naturally; in that we spent years in elementary then highschool, where people could learn about me, talk to me, and I them. Coming out of our youth, our longest time spent at a job was 1 year, and similarly for places we moved to. Some of these were from circumstance, others our wanting. But the point, is that, whether you branched off, or did what, over time, could be promising with a little bit of resources and effort, or simply were present to the patterns of life that came in contact with you, upon your established place, so would our life accrue. Making friends is hard, espeically one's of a deeper and or intimate sort. As are buisness' and trades hard to develop. But time is the real factor, and thus, like a plant that ought not move as to let time's effect work, so have our efforts been contrary to that, in all our moving.
 It really comes down to a balance of life and stability. Where, upon our need, we submitt to duty; and by that, orient ourselves to make this more deed like than burdensome. And from there, earn our harmony happily and merrily: whereupon, what stirrs in us for either delight or oppertunity, can become fashioned. "This is a flourishing process."
 But this attitude, as if, what is upon us, is not to be beckoned to now, as if we can not currently breathe, is not something which I ever felt in control of doing. The world felt so insistent around; that we were wasting away, missing enviable pursuits, or coming short to inevitable hurdles. I could not rightfully it seemed, proceed.
 In summer 2020, we moved to 122, and I recall stictly amending our attention to the scurrying effect this would prodcue; that people we need things of you, on short notice; that you'd have to lift and drive, without huff or refusal. The ordeal was not really so, and our transition was realtively nice. I comapre this now to our time in training, in that, of its ask of us, so we benefit by orienting ourselves around. Its good is long lasting, as our we subject to the wear of refusal.
 Truest of them all, the point of discontent which my otherwise amendment to content could rebuke, was merely of that attiude, and all actions consquent, where we saw our duty as both enough and warrented, nor the promise around as either worth begging for, nor enviable but of its exotic gleam. This is what felt off. Yet, our life back then had more ideals, at least illusions of the future, where the gentrified wish and ideal of divine knowledge and moral purity was spourting on the horizon. Something which I have not till of late left. Surely, there is more to life than I wish to admitt. And, in this time, when we can arrange ourself to through lifes movement and flow, I see no end, no bearing for our direction; no dreams or glory, no magnanimity or purpose.
In my reflections I see, that all our cares and dreams, as pertain to far off glory or promulgation, lie in our seperation of position and self. "As if the boat were on is wafting side to side, and our strokes are either soft, or not connecting fully: for which we peer far ahead, unable to see what harmony would bring nearby."
 It is so hard to remove yourself from this. In an odd sense, if we consider these conceptions like passions, in that they are grips for our wanting mind, that so, like a taste for something, or an addicition in the extreme sense, do we get hooked, and or reliant on such grips. Even now, when were standing up more or less by our own might, we still see them, in the distance; were not clung onto them; but, in our open and merry way, we still wish for determinance, some sort of activity and resolve. This is fair generally; the difference being that our direction and amends is closer to us, rather than far and wide.
 It is not unimportant though, to remark the value of our lives. So much does this trigger a flight response, as if there is something wrong; or some belidgerence born from our ideal sense. Only, as our choices and attention ought sit on things nearer than farther, and for what is of both interest and in our capacity, rather than what is of hope and grand confidence, that so is this fairer lense one which is still without, rather than in.
 I'd state it like this. That we have duties and deeds, adjustments and diligence. But, as the rising sun, and our constant need to breathe, that as our ordinary measures are of itinerary and work, that so are we in any given moment beholden to the weakness of our heart, the ignorance of our soul, the distance from love and clarity, the doubt of what is good and bad. That is the value you see, of learning and philosophy. For it is not ideals which our passion elates, but rather the comforts and promise that more hppefully sweeten our stale present. Ideals, are far off, born from short definitions, or an abscence rather, of knowing. This is what gives our direction in life, in that, we know what is good and worth seeking, what is bad and worth avoiding, what it means to be a man, a spouse, father, citizen. And it is not so much a weight against passion, as to be more pleased and content as we steadily go; but to rather know who you are, what things are, the matter of your heart, mind and soul. These, are the spring for enlightenment, and perhaps, virtue.
If a billion people were at war, or a 1000, so would my sight of things change: even if I was not in either case invovled. Part of this is from sympathy, others from our own lacking, and the imploding effect of that sympathy.
 An intelligent view then, which is made by the form and substance of your view, overts this issue, and allows you to humbly and peacefully be; all while admitting the reality of the world, and not shun or refute what is being said. "Which tends to happen when misfortunes trigger our hollow, or at least illforemd view."
 On the broad scale, you have something like a market based medical system, as compared to one more bolstered by the government. However each side sits, and whether of such side it is smartly made, and whoever is effected in this way or the other, 'as most changes in these respects, tend to come with changes in other areas, so the complexion is whats to be judged, not that there isn't some balance', so is it hard to say. But the senitment, in which only our idle stance is drug by, is comparable to the notion of NATO and or the more direct economic and defense relationship between Canada and the US. Hearing Carrney and Trump say this is well, along with NATO, makes the whole world feel sure and stable. "So much deterance makes any laudible threats feel unlikely; and so the world on the whole is fine."
 On smaller scales though, the harm to others can feel exasterbated. In the end, our heart being full and true, beating and upright, removes the obsessive and or scrutinous sight of any single person or animal, prevalent or potential. But so, in our huamnity, are we imperfect; and, reasonably, brought to something else, and as such, by virtue of our own likes and deeds, and the lingering, if not sizeable cracks in our reception, so does knowledge levy the burden. On one hand, knowledge of ourselves and nature brings much fortitude to ourselves; and thus irreverance to much of peoples plight; for we have no fear of such things ourselves. That, and we see the might in others, and our confident that in right action, proper dealings, adjustment to errors and or dealing with pain, that they generally do and are alright. As in most things, it is this general confidence, which exudes from our own indifference and (for others) respect, irons out what would otherwise be a prickly posture, of which real incidences and or our thoughts could snag on.
 Certainly, this manner of motion is smoother the farther you go out; and, of our general sensibility when it comes to the matters of war, and our overal security within NATO and the US, polishes off what would otherwise be snagged by the many impressions of the world. Locally though, our sense is less so; coming mostly from our meager experience. The impressions though, are in some sense more weighted here, and thus our view of things succumbs to them; as we have little form, however much substance from our experience. "And the heft of our soul shines over much which is near." Because of this, I'd find much respite in learning of Canada, its poltiical formation, the matter of our province and districts, and otherwise the assortment of analyzing metrics for such area. We need not such specifics for the world; despite polishing, they don't add to our form. While the form of whats near us is quite known; and, albeit worth knowing, in the way that a state has premieres and a house, so it'd be worthwhile to understand where such things are in our neighbourhood. Like a Cytoskeleton, we need a shell for regard, in our municipal and electoral formation. We also need a cytoplasm, as to give volume and depth, and not let anything bear the weight too much -- the bouyancy from our heart. And then we need fibres which tie all together, the spicks and spat of the world. For much of our grievence is in the fiendish elements of crime, or the atrocious miseries of unbridled murder. These are lost on me, and sensibly, the farther we go out: for which our view is banked on broader pillars. But at home, where a cat is hit by a car, a person attacked in the street, or some boy murdered, we ought know at least what else happens, as to dilute this happenings. The ground were on ought be known, that there are police and fire, health and governemtn, as are there car accidents, deaths, robberies and death. Animals may be left alone; for the one reason that they don't have shamefull fear or apprehension of death. They lick their buts infront of people, and upon injury, either fall and die, 'less their more or less instantly killed', or they hobble off, and sit. Our cat Moby was hit by a car, and we found him a little while later in a far off part of the house, bleeding; he was hurt, and went away to die. Our cat Dora, or any old one at that, is known to go off when age has found them. Our fear and fright, as if were helpless, mad, dissarayed, ought not color what is otherwise their experience. Animals are not like humans in that way, where they wish for help, or feel guilt, or anything beyond their response to the injury. Man is not so different I've found; as people losing arms and legs, or having their bowls opened, do not flail in hysterical fear, as if their frightened of the event. More likely than not, if such things happen, they were already embroided with the efforts prior, and understanding that such thing could come. Their heart and devotion carries them, and more or less permitts them to feel that for others their sacrifice was done. Civilly, attackes, robberies, killings even, are not so fiendish or devilish, but out of aggression, foolhardiness, confrontational as some people do, out of self confidence or drinking. Their may be emotions and anger, or irritability and a sort of lashing out; it just sort of happens; and likely by one who is not so endowed by any sense as a citizen. They more than likely cared not to hurt someone, or woke up thinking they were going to do it, but whether by drugs or irrate conditions, or as a means to get what they sought, the act of assault or homicide were not humane aversions; in some sense the law is there to prevent any possibility of humouring such. And such folk who commit such crime, are in a state where their behaviour is off, or are of a condition which renders their dispoition of such off, or their off the track, downtrodden, a bit grumby and or 'unprofessional', and thus act without the law in mind. There would be more killing if people were mindless fools; at least, if, unbounded by the law and provoked by their behaviour, that killing would be a common result. In the land of Fraser, with near 3 million folk, some, gentrified as drug addicted, street lying low raft, 100 are killed. I cannot say the trend of where and by whom, but there is at best a sentiment that these acts are random; and likely by rift raft, or odd ducks. No matter, it must be rememebered that, given some leniacne to our assumption that such killings are by certain candor of folk, at least where they are, that of the matter of killing, it is more sharply positioned, and not then even a remark of the broader 3 million.
So, peoples judgements come with good intent. For they want whats best for you, and thus urge you try; even demand it, for your own sake. In my own case, our domain of acceptence is sprawling, as no one cares if I'm a lawyer or plumber, or that I'm even a professional: they just want me to be employed and living.
 But that last part is the trickiest thing. For arguments that end in such a way as to merrit them based on some abscence of something, are not so convincing; but rather, to say, positively, what you should do. Everything else is not a negative of this, only lacking that explicit positivity. Experience ushers this in, and we nned only align, rather than condemn everything else. If we are free to do as we please, than what would please me is all I need to attend; without justifying it against other things, or merely saynig, 'that this is good because it is not that. It is good because of what it is'.
 There lies a depth though in which honesty can only give you so much acceptance. For whichever reason, we strike upon fidelity, which means proper action; and from there, the things we adored as children, 'ones free to do as they please', are heightened, made visible and secure. What if such things call for little money, nor much interest in friends. As kids, we enjoyed having someone to do something with, in our pleasures and adventure. As an adult though, this weens away, and peoples interests fashion around the trunk of responsibility. It is a rule though, that breathing comes naturaly, as does water; and food comes from the meagerest employments. Providing this for another fairs but a combined surplus, of which another node of simplicity is needed.
 Yet, if this portrait is granted, there is still an imbeded friction: you. For despite breathing coming easily, and food being but a shop away, so is your energy and vitality at risk from your choices. The irony being, that as children, our duties are easily met, and so we have no contention about delighting in rough housing and make believe; and these come so easily, that we don't notice any effects from the donuts and ribs we eat otherwise. As we get older though, and people naturally settle into roles, they don't run around as much; and that in combination with your natural age, and its effect on metabolism, you cannot eat sugar and crumbs without feeling the effect. Moreover, we do have to provide, and thus need some sort of job. This requires that we wake up early, can produce effort, communicate, and on a consistent basis. The toil may be small, but there is some, naturally. Indeed, our cares tend to warp from this, in that, of the dleights we once saw, our fatigue blurs them from us; and in combination with the good that we can otherwise see (as not to be bare of good at all), and to serve as a remedy to this effort, 'as to soften the despair', we turn to alcohol, frivolous and or high maintence sociality, drugs even; and food becomes expensive and buttery.
 My life has endured this transition, and not broken us. We work little, make enough, and have plenty of unbattered time to wonder and explore. The nature of these interests though differ from what they were as a child. I still like the outdoors, recreation, movies and games, but our satiety from these comes in an alternate way. For the deeds around me, and the fact that we are not so privy to friends 'in our grown delights', means that we don't merely watch movies, but engage with them, 'as if we are not merely clapping along to the dance, but taking someones hand'. That, and instead of cartoons and simple things, we partake in the grandest delights. Things which are by definition richer; but thus ask more of us. We may ardor over an essay, or merrily read a novel. But breezing through the former is like firmly lifting a massive weight, compared to a small one. Your simply engaged accordingly.
So I had a thought, one which I may expand upon, but will more importantly state.
 That is the fact, that in philosophy, and the descriptive process of science, there still remains an almighty question: perhaps the greatest of all.
 Simply, if all things are the same, and but weavings of the same fabric, then so conclusions lay. But, in contrast, lies a more or less diverse view, or distinct one, in that things are seperate from one another; which raises the concern of governance, as to bring them into working harmony. There is potential in this latter mark that immancence, which is to say some deifnite thing just being itself, bears the relations we see. Or, as is often stated, there is some imposed force, as to bear the object into relation with other things; and this being seperate is where the attribution of intelligence, or design as its often reffered to, come's in.
 My feeling, is that all of these elements are a result of poor defintiion of terms, and how, the question may ultimately be worth an inquiry; but, from where and thus supposedly to, ought such point be fair in its ideas and descriptions.
 For we say, of things imposed upon, what is the mechanism for them to be imposed. "Where does the hand of God and his children meet. And can we asusme, if their is such contact, that there is in fact any seperation? Which is to say, in that case, that there is no imposition, as the activity is more or less present, 'as in the case of one side of a line from another'; but, better put, that if the webbing is all connected, than it is not really a blend of individual things." The imposed sentiment makes little sense, beyond the appeal to sentiment. The unified proposal though comes with it the source for all inquiry, in that, if we remove any supposed phenomena, in which things may appear seperate, and we assume that our mind is like a whisp of the wind, and we forgo any impression of seperation based on higher compositions, 'where there is this planet here and there', but humour the fact that like a hole, the upper casym is not a remark of the lower attachment. Let us state that all is linked together in one way or the other. "Admitting, to be fair, that at this lower level, still a may a network lay." But, in keeping the unity, we still have to ask, where it comes from, how it moves and bends as to create the features we see. Moreover, where it exists? In the void? Indeed, my feeling about this, as a man humble to the enormity of this question, that of the properties we seek to find, or understand, in such ways as to verify the plausibility for unity, that such qualifications ought be coalescent with the nature that unity befits. Like our mind, which in concept and awareness, are more than not forms of the like which more seemingly real things are of, that so of the properties which are within all, wholeness, continuity, temporal being, that so are these beckoned justly in as much as these properties are understood along a 'humian line'. Which is to say, as brought by inquiry, that our view of such properties may in fact be false, and there is but a perception of such things, perhaps ungrounded by our lack of understanding of how things functionally are. For our minds, as part of rock and rain, are but features of the unity. Yet, as we awkwardly describe such features, 'as if the wind were concieved as someone blowing, when it is in fact not even propelling, or of itself, but via the roation of the earth and the current it brings', that so in such a blank and confused notion of mind, are we equally blank and confused about continuity, beginning, location --as if all had to exist in something else.
So history is a great tell, and assures one in what they do. Knowledge works this way; as you familiarize with things and what they cause.
 So when I look at our life, I see an abundnace of good; and there is seldom a day that isn't full of great moments. There are times though, in an evening or night, where I don't feel so embalmed or enthused. Boredom sets in; and not even our younger remedies of food or smoking will do; for we do not humour them in any real sense.
 This inquiry is about the source of this. For, having food and what not has often been a resorting factor; as we were in a moment, or deliberately entertaining ourselves, where pleasure was shown in the day ahead. Such relief was always promising. But we have moved past this, and found more delight in things which we end up feeling torn away, upon such pleasures. Only, these matters of delight, the harmonies and or strings near and dear to us, are not either easy to explain, nor know.
 What brought me to this inquiry is in our reflections upon youth. We saw that our parent's asked much of me, that we have a job, go to school. For the most part these things did not excite me, but for the good that came in being on a path, and without bearing from our parents. We felt free to do as we please, and look forward to things, as if our ground were sturdy enough to see ahead.
 But this predicament is one worth describing. For the reason we were not indulged to the promise of woman, money or profession, was that what we sought were the goods of liberty. Those things which delighted you upon your chores being done. And so, taking on more chores, felt somewhat arbitrary; as if we were set to get more, of things we cared not for. That, and it did not seem that we needed such fortune to experience what we normally sought.
 But I am brought to our point then: why did you like game's, exploring, eating food and watching tv. Wouldn't travels, partying, exxcursions and a gaggle of friends be more pleasing? My anwser come's down to what I could be a part of. For, it seemed that the needs of those latter, and what I then even cared about, wore against the joy of those former. For whatever reason, I could not let these go. And I made myself quite content with them, role playing, running around, and later on, with propiety and stability through our life. "As if those partying settled into the labors of their day; those rich only doing so later on; where they had but the extravegence and occasions their achievments gave them. Yet they had no peace, no fidelity, no sustaining joy. Mere fun. And for some, only modest amounts, or kinds at least."
 This is not about the fools errand of partying and success, but rather, why we struggle in experiencing otherwise. For there have been times when I sought, and thereon appreciated, the like of others, their warm reception to our own like, to the promise, or at least esteem, of good grades, recreational status, in gaming even. Where I, in social standing, and some of its sweeter pleasures, found refuge, or at least satiety with such. Contrast to this, is our time playing Black Ops, following a rainy day at Soccer. Something about that just felt nice. Going to Colins to play a game and watch movies was always a comforting delight. And this only came later, past our youth, but walking in the evenings, feeling full upon a workout and smoothie, to our first walk across the land, these things were empowering; as if our completion was upon the Gods and Man. The libery which that gave sprouted joys of immesurable worth; the sort of appreciation and care for things that only lethargy and roughness exclude. This is but an embellishment though of that younger state, where looking outward, reaching for status and or gain, seduced me not.
 Perhaps the truth, as I have somewhat exposed, is that our choices have brought us in and out of life, as not to garnish us a place amidst the world and men; whereby we find satiety in doing so, but of a fair and full degree. 'The follower' as one may say, is not actually fufilling; and there is some constraint upon them, whereby being heard or seen by others is relieving; yet such is both a fleeing and fuzzy pleasure. As a leader, or one who is themselves, in so much as they are walking, running, talking, reading, writing and playing full and true, is so the better if it is near and around other people; or, as my growing age found, around scenery and places.
 I look back on our life, and many forks in the road are clear. Moving to Pitt Meadows was out of our control, but we still could have gone to highschool here. Perhaps not essential, but if we humoured Taylor's interest, or literally reached out to the hand Pam was offering, who knows where we'd be. Later on though, where circumstance again ruffled our side, so could we (upon it's close) gone to Douglas, worked part time, eaten well and moved healthily. Maybe our motion was already set, and our line of indiviuality was grooved for the ends we've now reached. But at least you'd be young; likely of better health, with folk you new closer by. It would have been easier to learn skills, as we sometimes novely imagine; as would it be easier to see and consider the future ahead. "For the truth which became apparent upon our personal quest, may not have been so clear or present, and thus so weighing: for me, as one so steady and tall, to act like so: or consider as such, and not take what your ego and dreams are worth being ashamed for."
So, my aim is generally to find the truth; which as it pertains to us, looks at why we feel the way we do. If we are well, we ask little questions; and more if were not.
 I reckon that if we were set for a trip, we'd feel better, just knowing there was something ahead. Our life though would not really change; as we'd come back, and be where we started.
 The issue in my life has been in forgoing the good from this; as getting away from things can help you recenter to what is otherwise the same. I think of my life and the many moments of solidity and joy, and think that we need only tenure this, as to be with this more often and amply.
 Our largest mistake is in avoiding things. There is some reason, as deep down, the relief of our living is less reverred than the formation to actually produce living. But we tend to, reverently and more or less ceaselessly, attend such formation.
 Our work schedule is in fact a relief, as we are working more than usual. And I appreciate this; as we've spent the past weeks prioritizing ourselves. I could get away for a lil while, and merely ride the stream of itinerary.
I would like to steer away subsequent questions by saying a big pre-amble, but I'll just be plain and simple. You asked for a budget; to me that amounts to income and expenses.
 Considering I'm living by myself. Biggest thing is rent. The only places to find rooms are Craigslist or marketplace. Each room is unique, and every month the supply changes. There is always a range of prices, some being as low as 500, others high as 1000. For years, and up till spring this year (when I checked) the average (or mean) is 700. This is in Vancouver area mind you. And they all include utilities. More often than not there is no lease; sometimes there are, like my last place, and that was 6 months. Long term, you may find better/cheaper places, so even in the worst case scenario, if I had to pay 800, I may find one for 700, or 600 even, some months later on. And regardless of what may be deemed a risk, in all my time there's never be a whisper of being kicked out or asked to leave, or price being raised. That is rent.
 Expenses wise, my PlayStation cost 15 a month, as does my phone. I have no other fixed costs. Grains, produce from markets (and superstore), bread and simpler things of the sort, have not gone up in price (a little in some cases, but generally they're all cheap). Sometimes I will spend more, sometimes less, but I balance this out --including PSN and phone-- to 300 a month. 2$ toothpaste and pit stick lasts so long and costs so little, it's a write off.
 In case of an emergency, or some need of cash, that is it's own question, and something I let fortune and resolve handle --I trust in that at least, for better or worse. But, in terms of my cost versus income, sometimes I go over, sometimes I go under. The excess is never that much, but there is generally some. Three things I take into account. One, and this shouldn't be underestimated, but my basic career choice is Superstore. One of the perks of this is the easy ability to pick up shifts, which can counter any excess costs. Even if I wanted a new PlayStation, I might pick up an extra shift for a few weeks. Second, is that by working regularly, for a year, you earn a 2000$ tax return. Nothing I've experienced about taxes tells me this will be different in this coming year. I don't intend, or like, to rely on credit. But, say you have 320$ on it and you only have 300 to take off, now you have 20$ credit. So long as you pay your minimum, you are charged 10% --or 20 depending on your card-- meaning that you procure a 2$ interest fee. I may not have explained properly, but this is indeed how it works. My point, is that over time, if you have some moderate sum on your credit, that you do get a large sum every spring. I don't mean to say that I budget accordingly, but it is a factor. For, thirdly, you have to take into account your situation in life, the money you have, who your related to. Christmas and your birthday are a good example, in that you just get things. As are there opportunities to get some certificate online, or do something of the sort that earns you more money. Values don't change, nor your needs, so earning more money becomes an excess --compared to some who just match their expenses with their higher income. That is my sensibility at least, as to admitt that my day to day costs may not even out exactly. Where I stand now, how things are likely to be around me as time goes on, the oppertunity down the road, makes me feel confident that our balances will more or less check out. My contentment with simply having enough feels just. And as for having more than enough, in equity and reserves, these are questions for the whole of our lives. Yet I can't really appreciate these concerns when the rest of my life is unclear. I am not sure what I want to do, what is worthwhile to me. I've done nothing but think about this, for years, and my concerns grow smaller and smaller. But for security sake, and finding more oppertunity, I take these for g ranted as I am figuring out more basic and interesting things. If you want me to take my finances seriously, help me figure my life out. Because I will never care about the former so long as the latter is in question.
There is nothing harder to explain, yet easily know, than the peace and tranquility of philosophy. All divine regard and worship is merely a form of this, as are all personal literary measures. And, indeed, hearing other's speak their word is comparably pleasing.
 Yet, the reason for this satiety is hard to state. If I say I am at ease, with a sense of strenght and awe, so is my full belly or sore head easier to describe. Most descriptions of the former come down to, 'well, it's just this thing, this feeling; I can't quite explain it, but, as my words best define, its as if my heart is calmy beating, yet with might and integrity; I trust myself and others, fear no harm, especially to those far away. There is something cruel and powerful about this sense, as it fills me with righteous cause and anticipation. Perhaps it is because our will upon this flees without us being near and present; as if us being in harmony with things, through words and action, creates that spark which correlates with our heart beating. Yet, such embers come easily only in times for which wisdom would convey. And such insight is either unknown, are hard to uphold, particularly, as my cause may draw motive, when others around are equally beholdened.' Indeed, nothing stains philosophy more than the belief in it; for others are more or less not around to encourage you, or share in your adventure. Yet you may not be there yourself, and thus find it hard to even know what your seeking: simply the memory of a warm heart, flourishing without the normal grit wihtin duty, nor the envy and spite found through other's fun and travels. Concerns about the world equally fair, in that your are not taken away to some ungrounded realm; as if, outside your clear and known sight, where things have both timely and material proportion, you otherwise get swept into the seas of illusion, grandeur and pedantry. "Such waters hold turbulent dpeths, where the unseen, yet gripping subjects drown you, when they are of violence and suffering. Nothing is so hard to unintelligently revieve."
I may be alone in this; at least, many of the people that surruond me may not relate. But, my life is not entirely occupied, in that we have places to go, people to see, things of an elaborate sort to do. We tend to just keep to oruself, mind our buisness and tend to it, enjoy our peace and leisure.
 A side effect of this though, is something akin to other's seemingly judging you; for you are more aware of them doing so, or are at least not occupided enough to ward of small feelings that they might. In a similar vein, we watch alot of media; and like porn, we've done so for the podcasting world, and bits and pieces there of 'ideaslits, coaches, dreamers, haters, critics, comedians: folk with opinon'. And so I have this sense of a culture, or at least shared mindset of people, that they have ideas, or a view rather, and orbit around others alike. But so in these orbits are their conflicting, and sometimes combatting ideas: be it political, controversail, in entertainment, social. And my feeling about this is akin to perceived judgement, in that, because our life is not too occupied, we get the impression there is this consuming effort by all around, in their battle of ideals. This, at least, seems to paint our broad view of the world. As if, this was how the foreign places of the world operated around me.
 And my point, is that I cannot say whether my naievty here is right to be distanced from, or if such people have a point; in that they have some puirspoe, some aim, some effort upon ideals. Even if they are terroists or extremists, or are of a religion or community, of some polticical or social leaning.
 This is the harshet part of life; as its shown upon me in our ever sensing state.
