This idea, that there are people who have poor footing in life, are victims of neglect or abuse, harassment and assault even. There is a gaping stair from folk up to the perceived lords of these happenings; pearing at them, in fairness, to ask what they are law makers, govenors and officials mean to do. And, I, having but a deepened view of things, with little practical insight, know that a) these folk are not so extraordinary, but for their interest and place in high authority, b) are not evil or, neccessarily, of vice, because there is misfortune under their royal watch, and c) that the manner in which they execute their authority, is not so simply able, or accesible, by some quick and promising resolve. There is a system. In the case of Kings, who may let peasants flounder, so is the culture one that perhaps ligthens the perceived misfortune; but so, with all their might, can they off set the imbalance of what may otherwise be but a inefficeincy within a standard system. Peasantry may be feudal, but some may still suffice within. It is the imbalances, or misdeeds in it, that cause those --admittedly with little to spare- to fall out of the small threshold of stability. And this is hard for the King to quikcly and easily fix. For a system, or standard, needs fixes that are reminsicent of the sort. "If he had all the coin in the world, he would not, with some moderate restriction, benefit those by leaving all of it in the town square, for them to take; but, more realistically, in invesitng it fiscally, into infastrucre and other things of the sort, to counteract the debris and off standard."
 I say this, for little of what is personally or presentably thought, respects this, and instead, conjectural and or suggestive observations are made; and then appeals, or at least highlights, of judgements, sometimes scolding. Those around, who are at best with opinion, are not invovled in any of these matters. Likely because they are occupied. For if we did care about the subject matter, we'd not use these presentations as reference. They are there for those merely keen and or satiated from hearing about things. "As the news, is by defintiion novel: be it of good or bad things." To me this is a partial indulgence; as you admitt defeat to any standard in either seeing the world positively, or ideally (for which you may act towards).
So I had this dream, and Charles was in it. Now, he is a nice man; but he had some advice for me, and I just went off on him, saying he knows much less than he is aware of.
 Now, why was this my response? Well, there is something about me, unfirm, unchecked. And because of that, there is this inherent misguidedness of someone to offer me suggestion, and not detail the premises in which it's based on. I feel similar about bankers, who advise one to start saving for a home, with the assumption that I want one. And they may say that this is laudible for financial strenght; yet they presume that I care about finances. Don't you they'd say? And I admitt that smart choices are better than not; but that my life may not have an income to be best fashioned. I merely get by.
 When I hear the news, in some sense it relives me; but not in a way that I care for. It come's down to wanting something, considering our view of the world; and that because it is so postive, we latch onto this, feeling both pride and sense of nobility; for which I'd share. So when I hear the bells ring, 'its news time', I hear, 'the problems in the world are such'. And oddly, there never to do with me. But I have a hard time feeling free of problems; for the absence of positiviy otherwise leaves me somewhat unordained, nor hiddenly committed; as once was the case heavily in our younger years, when our passions held far off destinations, deep down
So, the mater of experiene come down to your appreciation for the tasks at hand. It seems we are given such taks by the decree of neccessity; that we'd eat, sleep, brathe. And I find most people who have grown old, provide me with guidnace as to better faciliate this. Yet, I dont feel as thoguh they offer anything beseds that which makes things easier, rather than me stronger; or able at least to make do with what I have. "They seem to be folk who are only wise in that they've lived long; but in tat old age, they onnly have advice on how to not be so old; as if not to make the same waseful mistakes they did. But they don't know how to live. Coming from someone who does, it is not just that other approaches may serve us, but that, of the harmony we have, so does it procure interests, which ordain us to certain tasks, that we may otherwise not care for. And the suggestions we recieve are neither for those, or for things common to most, but with resepct to those other."
 Dealt to us, are tasks, and these seem immutable. I find it lofty and unfitting to imagine what it'd be like to have no tasks. I am not sure what otherwise draws your strenght. In any case, we may make smart choices; and indeed, by the time your in, so may wealth be gained. Or oppertunity at least. But, for most of us, to feed and earn our bed, we must annoint ourselves to tasks. From this, we have to compliments, which earn us a steady state. One, is that you understand the worth of your effort, as to not see it as something unfair; plus, by your strenght, you appreciate how hard it is not. This blends nicely with the neccsitiy of the task, and that, of this thing your sentenced to, 'aint that bad'. The other side of things is about joy, or at least good around your deed. This elevates, warrants your work, makes it acceptable, 'for what follows'. It just takes your mind off of it; as if you, the person subject to work, be it releieve or not by the grough of such work, are also one subject to the upwinds of industry and joy. And, like our time in yoga or running, where our muscles become lean and limbre, so are our heartier, 'or rough in any case' efforts, more amply done. "Stiff bones bear weight rather accordingly."
 It may be so, that Kingship, a bevy of friends, all of wealth, for which travel, feasts and recreation are of mighty and plenty. But this life is not so easily, or in any case often achieved. Even if we take for granted, that for those who earn this, or are given it, that of the former the road was not short or easy; and of the latter, you are merely the progeny of one who did the former. "You may lucky to be so gifted by one who took such path." What I'd say though, is that for those of us who are asked to offer time for labor, and thus be a part of a routine, so is our fruit in life with respect to this. What I've found, is that of such life, it holds little of what, in all freedom, one would otherwise sometimes have. For one who is King, beyond the caliber, partakes in what ordinary folk do amidst their labor. Food is had, as are drinks; trips are taken, as are things bought. Some may do it more, and the shine may be brighter. But the substance is more or less familiar. What I seek is different. For in our respective task, we are given two wonderful things. One, is the reward of steadyness, the feeling of stability in motion; the understanding that, if such deeds were taken away, that you'd be worse off, without financial maens; and thus, having this in harmony, makes one rather pleased and secure. To be secure as King is likely better; which is to say, that of my needs, I'd rather own them so much, then barely so: but the equilibrium which in both case is your treat, is to die for. Second, is the boon of strenght. For it is one thing to have both what you need and what is enough: the feeling of tranquility. And another, to experience this with vitality and strenght; so that the tasks are not burdends but deeds, things that are agreeable not only in their purpose, but their manner. A fish that is asked to walk on land has a rough go, while the deed to swim is one he is all to easy to accept. Man is odd, as he may do all but fly. Yet, like a common person trying to do a Ple'a, or the splits (in dance), the fit for a task is not inherently smooth. Like a screw, our fit may be loose, or jammed in there. And indeed, the consquent friction is what motivates most, as such roughness spurrs us to seek relilef, or a change of position. But, if it is equally demanding, but of mere difference in form, the result will likely the same. And if it is easier, than you are missing out on the effect of your proper response. Which is to say, that if you fashion yourself, through committment and propiety, to fit that mould, not only will your tasks be ligher and well taken, but you alsoo become formiddable yourself. Someone who trains for a marathon, experiences leisure and recreation, the benefit of respiration even through their normal day. Training for something less leaves them ever so smaller; for things which, in their acquired might, they may apply themsleves towards, or experience.
 Biography is the attempt to mend all threads in our life. And indeed, this parchment is like a tree, always needing nourishment, as not to wilt. But, I can say, that of those threads, they are more or less brought from our years prior, and all the effort we achieved. Our care for games has been a pouring delight, as is our fancy for film and youtube videos. I feel a substantive sense of bonding and fun; like I'm hearing people talk about things we really care about, in a meaningfull way. I love music, and relish the hues it brings me. The outdoors is wonderfull, near and far. And writing just affords me an adorned experiemnt; where I can see and align with David Hume, Plutarch, Aristotle. Ourselves even, in our reflection and study; the world feels calm, ever going, rich and plenty. These are coarse description, and speak little to the wonder I have. The pure joy in entering the beta last weekened, or finding this new long form channel, talking funnily and smartly about Obi Won. And all around, ever and truly, am I sparked and excitred for more. New things, familiar warmths. Our heart always beating, orbited by reverence, but thick and through to the iron bones of our nature and way. As man, of duty, with little need, ceaseless enough. Our worry is fleaitng, furrying away to someone not so steady. I may accept our sitatuion, and not reach for grander pastures. But our use of what we have is so encouraging, that I haven't cared to try. Perhaps a dowry worthy of a prince would be nice, and I could live flourishingly, with prime novelty around. We are not though. Middle class sure, but not of indepenant wealth. Perhaps, our past sees little which would be both agreeable and smart; or, of what we could do, and happily, maybe our knowledge of such is sparese. "And so the options available are too few, to likely fit with our current fair."
It is not uncommon to relate to someone, in terms of a shared interest. It is perhaps a unicorn worthy discovery, to find someone who will invest their and time and effort, or make choices at least, for that benefit. Perhaps it is because of the leisurely nature of it; that it should be effortless. I just find, for instance, that so much scrumptous joy is found in reading essays and reference, that the demands of this, 'as to read it smoothly', warrant to me the effot to do so. All such efforts may fall under being discretionate; as the subjects which our chooices fall under are more or less present for all: sleeping, eating, etc. But we might, for simplicity sake, distinguish between things which are not so normal, but perhaps common, for which you abstain; such as drinking, smoking, eating sweets, scrolling through phone, watching porn. And then there are those other matters, for which we all do, and to some degree well; but of which we take a solid hand. As we all go to bed, and have some sense, or routne, as to do so well. But, through reflection, honesty and regularity to this approach, alter and fashion our sleep, as to make it beyond average. Much of the ease which one may find in activtry, or leisure rather, is not taken away when you consider all this potential foramtive efforts. For they only amount to choices that you always make, but of a better sort. That we'd eat now is simple; but that we'd eat this over that is not; but it becomes so. It's just about the sense, as we have as a child, that some experiences are better. And, perhaps motivaed by work, and how some choices make it more agreeable than others, that so of our more enthused and elated leisure, that so may certain choices bring this out. One prime example is the morning sun; something few of us ever see. "The red coming out of the night." I've experienced but a few times before; but in a sense, it committs me to keep well, and tailor our means, as to one day reach a point where all our linens are woven and set; and ripe for me to make a choice to do what fits me for an early rise. The night catches the days wonder, but also holds up its morning rise. I long for the latter.
 In truth, and I may say more of this in our notes, but our choices thus far have laid us the present, for which new choices are making. And of those choices, some of them are for matters that behoove us: for money, relations, position in life. This is not crucial, nor so relevant to our self or delights. But, as it were, there are smart efforts in these matters, comapred to others. For one, as our current day shows, being employed is nothing but good. In some sense because it preps you for your time alone, or independant rather; and otherwise, keeps you upright into a steady but also firm schedule. Progress actually benefits from this; as you are more or less kept down a line that demands you be well; for you will notice your leiusure slip. Generally, our progress may find respite in the moment, and feel good if freedom is on offer. But none is given forever; which remarks our adolescent frustration; but also our slow and grinding path into our own given solidity. As regards money, there is much that it can afford; notably, in how it can support your progress, as transitions and or preference have room to breathe, as you slice off the top to make up shortocmings. We used to see it as a priceless fruit, that would feed us for some sought for period of time. But progress benefits, and indeed after some time, takes refuge in, lasting movement. Perhaps the effort and solitude, and at that uncertainty, of early progress, bodes it to seek freedom; even at the future's cost. "Where you are given no leave, or amiability, to a directed, or elected return to duty."
So I see much in terms of our life, across various subjects, that could use a development. Much of their worth is unstated; but of our views of them, so are their bubblings of inclination, terms, defintiion, renewal. Government for instance, sits as this thing that bears it's credence through development; and that all things of civil and or political sort, has come from our history as mammal and man. That we could think and talk, and by that form views that guide our aciton, so is this rooted in the annals of neurology, it's relation to sound and hearing; and even our rudimentary professions through the stone age. Not our bigotry nor our conceptions feel remarkable, any more than the mircale of life some 4 billion years ago. All that remains is the mystery of mathematics; or rather, the seemingly divine ability to deduce, or make arguments. "For that is all math is."
 For whichever reason, the world, or nature rather, is drawn out as this thing to be defined. For, akin to Hume, we marhsal and move with respect to our taste in things. Our resolve to family, better jobs, public employment, these remark baser values; perhaps passed down, and seen as obligatory or oppertune. But we still delight in breakfast, or music, and averse ourselves to rotten food, or the cold. In many ways, our remarks center on 'making do with what you have; and what ought there be, for you to orient around'. For one is a moral concern, in that there is right, oblige, purpose, and some call to contribute. While the other is, not so much an ethical, but rather a virtuous regard. The reason I inquire into nature, is that virtue has some sort of principality to it, whereby the sharpness we evoke, as to be deemed virtuous, is so that our aptness is to something we deem important. "For being good at something unimportant is worth no acclaim." But thereby is the problem, that our time in work, or in converse, where we show sensibility and forebearence, or reserve and or kindness, is something that is more or less a virtue; but is, at heart, an indubatably aptness to such motions. They are indeed present, but the direction of our remarks is not made from moral, but rather sentimental grounds; where the subjects, or tasks we'll say, that we use as scales for gauging aptness, are not presented critically, but rather as what's deemed normal and common; and by that, worth assuming as to be somewhat relevant and important. Sports are a good proxy for this; as some, in the Olympics for instance, may show themselves to be worthy of the Gods, in that they perform like those on Olympus. Yet, Zeus manages all, from our hearts and breathe, to the world and death. We mortal men merely dedicate and exhibit ourselves promptly in picking up heavy stones.
 There is something to be said in being moral. For more than not speak, or at least act and assess from seperate grounds; where we take the concerns of our life for granted, and merely amned to them as well as we can. On one hand, you may understands such things, and in some cases see them in a different light; which may reveal them to be more fair and natural, and by that, in their demands, just. Also, many folk may make ideal claims, or moral ones, that fair more as pessismism or scrutiny, of other people and or organizations; or customs. Knowing, or at least having, a stronger moral ground, or patchwork so to speak, keeps one unperturbed by such expressions; as you are not recipient to any real guilt, nor concerned that other's may by it. For some reason, if none were morally discretionate, but merely common, then the meagerest of moral claims does not excite them. They may react; and indeed, preaching, books, radio, and now televsion further create such reaction. But they make little motion; as the depth of the provocation is not enough to assimilate any real moral care into the recipient. "Only in their free time, do they vent and espouse the foolishness they here; or their own critical take. Little else." And so, having your own moral fibres prevents you from feeling parched and or bare, as other's make their own moral claims. Those which are deep, in some likely way, reference the real world, and by that are weighed down by here breadth and enormity. There is no provocation thus. But if it is full of either fallacy or brevity, having your own weight keeps you sensible to the best presumed happenings, and forthcoming.
 In short, having your own moral backbone, removes any provoked guilt, frailty, or doubt regarding your own common resolve. The world is indeed a messy place, and much that could be is not; and throughout the past, poor moral thinking, or at least civil frictions have occured in relation to the tasks laid by nature. But the developmental approach, or understanding, removes any shrinking of this into themes and eras; and thus no king or queen, nor ravenously percieved solider or peasant, is colored with such tone. In some sense, as memory is a great tell of what's to come, that so is the cause for this being so, a tell of the boon of moral sense. For in the case of memory, you are more or less ingrained to the properties present. For instance, when you judge, or see, a portrayal of two people in a painting, the style of it will sit as an innate foundation for the figures; and so part of the given proprties are such. Equally, of the world, that it would remain the same size, hold the up and downs of the day, is given in relation to any concieved event; for such properties were present in all prior ones; for which all forthcoming one's are fairly taken with so. Moral sense then, finds benefit in being more clear and present, in that you form more proportion and detail as regards our behaviorial, civil, economical and or reverent quality. That a war may come, is no doubt possible; but that it was ordained by those in government, and through prior discussions between parties, is fairly taken, based on what has happened prior. It seems, in other words, that moral sense is about the world, and seeing how people are through the ages. But, with depth, you have fine moments and actual action, rather than tales of a time, or statues of some person.
I tend to see the death of one as acceptble, when it is of an illness or malady; as this feels natural, and part of our basic condition. When someone steals, or attacks someone, in ridiculous fashion even, I am upset by their actions, and sympathetic to whom their belidgerence is received. We are not so fraudulent though, nor are we much but kind. It is hard for me, as someone who has bloomed further and on, as a mark of their feeling secure and fine, 'in not doing anything, seemingly, or evidently wrong'; for which the things we see, along moral and pleasing grounds, is sweet, enormous, and otherwise with not much but the mystery, or misfortune, that beyond organized conflict, that smoebody would cause harm to another; and in a ludicrous way; at least in a display that feels obscence. For shooting somebody in order to steal something, or even out of malice and spite, is rather straightforward, and worth the felony. But stabbing someone repeteadedlyt, or beating someone up, a stranger even, as has been reported, is a real oddity; and a hard one to cope with, considering the violent nature of it --of trauma too. But I will not let our moral compass lessen, nor our proper sense that, of our actions, that they are human and well; and that, as if caught a gust of wind, that they'd ride their sails; no matter who else had worse sheets to work with, or not wind. Perhaps the turbulent sight, is where one's ship is compromised, or at least shaken; or that, as the captain, they are the recipient of someones awful and or innae behavior: unjust action no less. Here lies our conflict. That being so far ahead, we wish to really ride the wave; while being further back, lends our actions to be of a complacent sort: for pleasures of a low, yet filling kind. Our satiety, or at least content, 'whether it be in the moment, or through the day', leads us to see things in a broader way, and not get hooked onto any irreverent, or at least unwelcomed element; for which we drift on. Only, in our more lively state, we are more or less aware; at least, we tend to come off our strides of bloom, and settle into propiety and rest; but therein reep the thickets of rumour and or display. If we were depressed, we would more or less turn inward, and huddle; and hide away, as one would in ceasing from a surrounding flame. Only, our mellow state is one of renewal, or at least mozzying till our stride rises. And so were already leaving, ready to move on; yet this thing is near and in front of us: and were doing nothing. In many ways, the manner of the law is for legislatures and committee to decide; and indeed, we'd be wrong to think that our view of something innane was entire in both the factors of the assailant, and that of the prosectur. Simlar to immigration, there may be leniancies that are ill founded, and perhaps infuriating, considering the abhorent nature of the crime. But our moral standing is not onto this; as if in defiance of the law which surrounds the incident. We are part of the comonwealth in so many ways; and there is much which occurs that its restrictive nature must apply. The more blatant ones, ie. drunk driving, and consquent deaths of a child, are not detriments to all other accidents, or effective drivers. "Not even the misdeed of such things are enough to claim smoeone is poor at driving; only that their actions were of an accordingly poor type. For me at least, that someone makes a certain act, is insurmountibly hard to explain by some form of disposition. that there is one is hard enough to say; and even then, of such root, it can only be deemed to have some part or bend, as to produced this action; as many other are also made." That is my primary aversion to hearing anything of an incidental report; as the actions are not truly clear, in all their moments and detail. And even so much as they were, it is hard to speak ill of the person, when you don't know of all their prior actions; for which there are a plethora. Some may be comparable, and perhaps but less explicit, in their abhorence. Or they may be ordinary, and there are but rumnblings of the aggression, or, at the furthest, innaneness; for which the present incident is a display of that, fully. Hard it is to judge an insane person; for less they cannot speak their name, nor have any comon need, of food or bathing, and that they may communciate, then I can't say that any vile act, as born of, or at least seemingly kindred of insanity, is a entire remark of them. For are they not still a person, with other memories, words and deeds. Reprecussion and adjustment ought not be made on enviable or malicious grounds, but fair ones; for a law is made as to allow no mishaps; which is to say, it is not a guidline that you cannot kill someone, but something constitutively forbid. And so, if someone does do this, upon investigation, that the force of the law must be exlcaimed as to substantiate the restrictice nature which it means to uphold. Otherwise it is but an absolute suggestion.
I can't say why, but noting our prescence within, say the economy, but more particular the law, is something which I feel will give greater clarity and understanding to such thing.
 As it were, I have not committed a crime, nor anything indecent. That, and I abide the procedures and or prescence of authority, as to arrest and prosecute those who do.
 It would seem, that adjustments to such process be done by those professed and or ordained to such domain. Indeed, common folk, who are not experts, nor likely to reflect on their views, and by that form them, may still see something wrong; in say the act of someone, who's address ought be given, whether plainly or beyond. That, or we may see the dealings of such person, and feel the verdict off, or the scales which determined so. Again, we folk are not likely to have sifted through the evidence, and with equal scruple, concluded upon it; and, from there, determined a right consquence, or judgement. Part of me wonders whether we detest such conclusion on the basis of evidence, and that, given the evidence that proportedly was, that such a verdict would be fair, but that we see the incidence differently; or, that we admitt, more or less, the depiction and thus assessment of the scene, but feel that the consquences should differ. On those grounds, it may be legally so, that of such assessment, that there is still some deliberation as to consquence give; which is to say, that it ought be considered this or that crime. Perhaps the evidence being so, that the nature of the crime is encumbant; for if we say, so and so did kill so and so, then a murder this is; qualofying this, as to deem it first degree or not, is perhaps within the evdience, in that, of the consquence, or decree, it was of the actions done; which may be said in way that the manner of murder is stated. At any rate, we may still repent over an agreed upon situation being classified as it was; perhaps by our refutuary position to the law, and it's innate manner of judgement; or that, of the thing, which we may say, in an active sense, is first degree murder; or at least, what that is considered techinicaly, that so to some, the same actions are not considered that. "That a certain kind of murder was so, that it was such based on the prescence of comdey in the kill." So the disputes and or grievance of litigation lay.
My wisdom gives me sense, as to respond to things well, adapt to their uneveness, appreciate their reasoning, and by that, my reward. But my moral sense is weighed down; perhaps, in all fairness, and maybe truth even, when our wisdom has not prevailed; or our amends to it at least. But, when we are round and through, we still see the factors of morality, or those pertaining to it. Only, my strenght is more or less pitched, and by that forward to the progression of things, whereby, to where I may go, contribute and provide, so are we on such path.
 And so I rest. While, seeing something in need, but feeling not the care or wit to tend, leaves me feeling meloncholy, and that oh so retched rancour.
 I walked across a distressed cat some time ago. Not evidently injured, but meowing for some reason. And my thought was one of sadness and grief. I had walked up to it, seen what's wrong, and besides our confusion, saw nothing, and so walked away. I felt I was leaving it, as if in perril. "And like a mon or dad, who sees their kin drive off, all they see, as rooted in their bubbling worry, that bad may occur; from others, or in accident." Yet, I found recourse, in the sense that, of what I could do, I would, and of what I can't, I shan't. And this insight grew upon me, as if our own problem was being dealth with, and by our strenght and ease, adapted ourselves to that. Why I feel poor at the thought, or sight rather, of some hurt pud, or am peeved by the callouse, or at least glamoured assertion of the evil within man, is hard to say. But, that we would fall into such pits, 'whether they be dug deep, or are but mere shallows that we loose our footing on', that so is our own uprightness and sense, a counter to such friction, 'or holes no less'.
 Frantic is the word I'd use to describe our feeling; akin to those parent's, who can't quit rest on the proceedings of their child, moving off, or driving at night. The remedy, more or less, is acceptance, but of an integral rather than slack sort. For, if said child needed aid, you'd move mountains. But that they may need some, that is not for you to control. You may be confident, based on what memory serves; nor may your need to overcome such angst be so great, 'as much of our ways my overly excite our mind, upon such concerns.' `
So, I reckon that our style of speaking, or at least approach as to tailor a form, is what seperates me from most people. When my parent's for instance ask me about my finances, I can't help but include values, which more or less swirl from the wishes to feel great, at east, while also constant, but with cares dear, and not overly far -- as bad habit, or the pressure from circumstance may elicit.
 I feel I cannot speak in this way. And, indeed, I recognize that, in say our quandry with government, or at least comentary about it, is that, of those watching, I don't feel that I can speak about such things in certain ways; but, better put, I recognize that there are such ways to speak of things, as if there are indeed other factors to account for: many of which remove our worry and contempt.
 Saying what things are allows me to rest; for if they say much, but miss a certain thing, than, of a manner of speach, it is only of a certain sort; as regards a topic or kind of thing. This amounts to governance, where monetary, fiscal and secure policy is noted; civics, or some civil act, likely a crime; then traffic, which I use as a broader word, as to sincerely see the approach of those who talk about a variety of things, in literal traffic, the weather, a cool thing or festival (happenings that aren't worth remarking upon, but are going on through the everday of our world). Then there is worrying, from pundants, jokers, hosts etc. On Dr.Phil, or a monring show, it is usually cheerfull banter, and with jest, but then serious fear or finger pointing. Whether it's Mouer, with his no bullshit attiude, or a panelist on MSNBC, it is very much a book clunb approach, where on speaks to an audience, brining them in; and their is a way they ask specific questions, where they bring it up as if it were forbidden, or taboo, but their saying it no less. "As if there's this thing their curious, beyond what's obvious." It is not an inquiry though, for such thing never a) makes up it's mind, but then b) proceeds through progress and checks; the integrity of which is in reserve, but reaching where they go.
Some words may aid me, as they once had. I am feeling stressed, due namely to our lack of working out. I just feel heavy in my legs and arms, and thus, our food is not fully taken in, nor are we able to breathe and stretch. We just need to increase our activity; carefully, but with some must, as to break you out. Not for the sake of your muscles, but your system. Some sort of intensity, as to bring your body back, and allow you to breathe and stretch, and thereby form you for range of motion.
 Making choices and thinking of things feels rough. Notable is our care to stay here; which I feel somewhat off about. I have reached a point where we are coming in and out of work; and our view of governement, civilty and money is in check, for of that which we see, we are held accountable, but any grief and grough were brought through. "As prodding as it is, your brought forth in some way worth commiserating. So I accept that." So I may not move. But I do feel, as all other things are unclear or seen, that we will make furhter progress, plotting along, doing what were supposed to, enjoying what comes our way. As I see it were fine, but that, of our motions, they may be bent in smarter ways, as to save more money, or learn new skills and trade. These manifest into something sure and credible. But it is hard when were but on the path, and not approaching something already. For not much, amidst the rest of our life, who, in moral and creative fashion, is unclear or unconcerned with aims, finds much meaning or strenght to try. "Were not so megerely or enthusiastiacally going back to school. It will be by the rest of our able efforts and process; a smart addition, for cultivating."
 We have 5000$, and make about 5-700. Were likely to get money when we move, and our means will be catered to through our transfer. Which means were here till spring, and thus, our savinngs will reach some 8000$. I am not that bethrothed to short term gains. Whether I have 5 or 10 thousand is of no concern. But losing money is never great. And long term, it is fair contributions that amount to a far off sum. But I have no such means. Nor do I plan on having them soon. Of video making, cinematogrpahy, or some fortuned path in life, we may reach. But for now, were moving as one who is already pleased, nor so concerned. This mode of life earns only what is neccessary; and thus has little to save, less that which can aid us in transfer.
So, I mean to give some focus to our finances. One day, we may have a child, and or need some funds --beyond the annuities by the government. That, or we will rely on our children, who may have prompted themselves earlier than I. Indeed, I care not for advice when it is ignorant of our life, where it's been, where it's apt to go. But I do see, that we may make smarter choices; or at least have such regards. As it were, regarding work, my greatest apathy towards it is in the quality of the job, and, of potential education, how it affords such job. But much time has come to pass, and the room for enthusiasm, and by that dedication, is rising. Our finances are a weird thing, as I count on our frugality, or that of our parents. But that we would still have our own money, and feel secure with it, is fair. For none, in the decreed wisdom, make much of a sum over a soul contribution, but regular. Our position in life, is that, at home, we are able to make such additions. But when were on our own, this scenario shifts, and our additions are minor. There is some benefit to having a sizable sum, as 8% on 5000 is still 400$. Nor, is an 8% drop anything more than 400. Over 3 years, you could be up a 1000$. The point however, is not that you'd earn much, but that, over time, you may endure any risk, and more proficiently hold your money. Contribution is required for serious growth, be it in pure savings or funds. But we may still be smart. Indeed, the risk is less based on the nature of the fund; and that the longevity of the companies makes the sustainiility of their combined stock a solid one. Making 1000$ over the next few years would be nice.
 As it were, we'd benefit from a security fund. So that, if we are on our own, we may transfer, adjust, or make do upon injury. The given rule is based on your monthly expenses: 3000. So we put that aside, as our reserved money. Not for scooters or PC, but security. I reckon, that our transfer will not cost us much; as perhaps we are too much, but also rightfully so confident in our Superstore placement. "Our labor career." And so, besides that secure fund, which warrants itself along the same domain of reasoning that warrants investment for the future (as to at least pass on), that your otherwise common savings be spent on more momentary matters, be it in deposits; and your ability to buy a scooter or pc is brought from this.
 It is not easy to say, as not all are on board; nor does our action permitt itself to be judged and or esteemed in such favored conditions --as such are not exactly the norm. But, modestly, we can participate in the movement of our family, and rely on them that a) if we move, we will be given some money, which is likely only the deposit and first month's rent; and b) that if we put some money into a fund, placate the amount of addition, that if it goes bad, that we will be covered. Or at the very least, as to how serious that drop could be, that if it interfered with any of your expenses, that so would those be accomodated for, or given support. The balance will average out.
 We have 5000, and need 3000 in reserve ---that we will not spend. At least, we ought concern our money as divided into that which is safe, for spending as needed, and investing. I'd say, perhaps, that we contribute moderatley to the investment, assume more risk, and let it be money that sits there; and perhaps, rewards us in heart, rather than sum or principal. Let us take 2000, which I'd be fair to give up, hold a 1000 for me, and 2000 in safe. Based on what we earn, we'll allocate the 50 or so $ we have for expenses, a fixed amount for investing, and enough as to save some for me, and to our safe. In the year or so that we'll be here, some mean of 150 for the safe seems fine, to reach 3000. I would like to have something above 2000 for cash, which again, a mean of 150. For heart, and by sentiment, principal, we will procure the gains through our index; and assume the risk; at least our need of it. For, over 5 years, the increase could be some 70-100%, which leaves you with another 3500$ on the principal alone; and some potential 1000 extra principal, and its 6 or 700 extra. So mean of 5000, that you let grow.
I may say great things. But our breathingn seems enough. Indeed, like a nice sight or a walk, we write; but freely, and without care to assert.
 by that, I relish the principles by which you understand things; be it in the Gods, and who they may and by which nature; for which there are many schools, or at least forms of thought; which in all decency, are but leanings and or agreements amidst our investigation. While in government, there is monetary policy, where the banks are concerned, fiscal policy, where tax rates and spending are concerend, and security policy, or defense rather, where relations and or unity with other soverigns is concerned. The civil side of things is tough; for we judge people unfairly, or pithily. We say, 'that people just want everything done for them these days; or Bob is such a gossip, or air head; or, that guy who committed that robbery, or assault, or murder, is bad', and we say this as if were seeing them from without, and stating how they must neccessarily be within. That they are as corresponds with their efforts. But I feel there is an obsessiave, if not clammy posture by those who say this. The truth, is that some people drive while drinking, and cause an accident, fatally sometimes; or that some people are rude and abrassive, to women, or general strangers. Some are disrespectful, or at least conninving aginst the law, and steal, or cheat. And some, are disposed, in anger, frustration, or sanity, and at that unchecked, by stupidity, militance, or deafness, to hurt others. And when one of these thing happens it is a shame, for there was likely no neccesity to it; nor is it pardonable, or to be ignored, as in decency and orderliness, the case must be addressed. We may not like someone, and feel disdain and or aversion to them, and as such, not wish to hire them, or see them in any communual way; but we ought not belief our view of them is so complete and solid, and that our feelings are anything more than reaction to something that we are not pleases (and perhaps abohored) that they've done. Obsession, and all the non partial and or 'clammy' compulsions -- which, like erotica and her impulse, can be brought by imagery of the like---make for a certain sense and or grasp of knowledge. And I find myself, accepting that people are blinded, mutilated, harrassed, disenfranchised, overlooked, lied to, spoken poorly about, or pithily; and moulding this view with a proper dynmaic sense, that of however people are, in militance or obsessions, that their actions normally pass that of others, and that it is but select, which is to say indidivudal, and if in groups a select kind, where collisions occur. This is our proper sense. That, and we harckon to the form of our world, that, in it's people, that they are, upon retreat of our obsession, fairly good, or at least more capable within and focused on their own life; and be it with honor and servitude, or mere custom and duty, that of the mangerial aspects of our world, in how it is formed to prevent and provide, that people do this more or less well. "Our obsessive criticism tends to focus on what things could be, or what comprasmises are made; and look past what good actually occurs. Inquiry, and the propiety of ideas, serve to mould this, and find truer ends and proficiency."
I have not done this in some time, as things have been well enough for me to focus elsewhere; or I've been too off and focused. Our work on nature and such has not come far in recent memory. Nor much regarding government. Our thoughts on the manner have levelled off in the meat of thing, rather than our inquiry.
 This is all to say, that I am pretty well; in all regards mainly. Our health, although out of practice in some sense, is near complete in it's rules. Our time at work, although naturally demanding, and full of little intrusions here and there, is more or less a dream; considering it's demanding nature; namely, in our relations, cease of awkwardness, ease of participation, and generl rythm of preparing, going, returning, rinsing, and living.
 That being said, there is no defined rule or measure, or at least path, or direction, in which my life is on. My life is such, that the fairest admission of it, is one that bears content within a obligate state; and thus, of our truest regards, they are of our relief and liveliness in our harmony with that: but while the deed is there. And so, of those old thoughts, regarding the Gods, war, infamy, sainthood even, we don't actually have much room for these, or reaching care; as we are not in fact pinned by the motion of our life, and our nature being properly exhibited. There is merely us, our duty, and our fertile way. In this, comes curiosity, interest, pure leisure and form. But the sentiment of purpose, which laid by our side for an age, has washed off. Indeed, these being felt with all but the broader world, 'who's subject matter was not of genuine concern to me; compared to our impasssioned plea (and concern) for others to eat healthy, and know about the Gods': such has gone away, and instead been replaced with a contentment, which is all to free and pleased to be drawn, and then embrace, that which pulls our sight; and, as such, from a freedom that comes from resouce and liberty, upon fair and lawful duty, and as such commissery, where the time and ease to see is there, and our amends. This has been so. But in any case, the matter of governnance, pedantry, and what until of late was unknown as opinion, rot me to my core. There are reasons why; as our duties were not without burden nor yearning. And so our wish to save others and be seen, was not fully engrained, in potential, when we sat around, more or less pleased; yet, hearing others, made the problems we'd attend seem more near and pressing. That's one thing. The other, was this grim malignence; as if, upon our cease from leary learning, or unending inquiry, that we lost our grip, or at least coherence, to the world we saw. And so the regular suggestions by others, and our folks, had me more or less pressed upon by things which felt in error, yet certain. What this is, are opinions. Which is indeed a tricky matter. But only for the Gods to tell, in the making of every bit and thing. For opinions are how people feel, what they see; and they do include reasons and evdience; but only to support their view, or give insight as to why they see it that way. That is all though: such view. And it is clear to me, that a thesis, for instance, is merely a confident claim, that has no opinion, but a demonstration: a proposal if you will. Whereas opinions are not built upon reasons which justify themselves. It's more like description, in that, by this or that, you give sight as to what your seeing, and why it seems the way it does. My point then, is not to determine myself, what these opinions mean. Only, that they do not demonstrate much; and thus do not potrude foundational principles. Such principles, may hold evil and mal-practice. They are also of the world, vast, intricate, connected in combined ways, for which results are not mere distributions of likely ends. "Many people may drive and not be killed, based on their actions; rather than what we may deduce from statistics." But I wonder then, the world being a certain way, and many people talking about it; but not in a way that we feel overwhelemed with, since their just opinions. "The issue being, that we were so weighed down by our knoweldge, or view of how long, vast and slow things were; nor people so poor in that dynamic; that we held all opinions to be worthlees; yet without our finger on the pulse, much of our sense sunk under the ppinted pressures from opinion; and our principle was not solid enough to thwart every claim; for our broader view to remain in tact. We learned a bit, saw such breadth in action; but more importantly, gave credence to there being opinions, but as such, things in the world which we had reproached from: for which we found it easier, in in some sense neccessary, to be present to." I guess I lack the words or ideas, to see, and perhaps guide our want and concern.
Are we not a man? Ought we have some gauge by which we produce our actions from; and some sort of practice which brnigs habit and ability into harmony.
 We feel, perhaps foremost, that the key factor of a man, in as much as he is adult, is that he has roles and duties, and thus a commission to them.
 Much of our life though has been spent amidst the recession into one's commissioning; where liveliness and self-regard were not esteemed as fitting for such. But rather, you should buck up, show up, put your head down.
 I have reckooned for some time as to why we hold the cares we do; and part of me says that our childhood was one, where we could do what needed to be done, whereupon our interest was open to run and play. We never wove out of this; doing everything we could to return to such state. Of things mundane and or deed like, we deemed them burdens; and in youth, we ignored them plenty; as if we had some claim to being unbecoming.
 As a grown up though, this was neither tolerated, nor quite feasible. For you either lived under firmer rules, or went your own way; which in effect asked the same thing. Sometimes children merely wish to avoid being told what to do; and clean and what not as they see fit. Being independant has this wiggle room. But the effort required there does not amount to much less than the generally small tasks required to live under another's rule.
 There is a notion I see, that those in youth, who detach from their commands, form alternative prusuits and passions. And doing things as are commanded is seen, or at least responded to, with hues of disrespect and dimunition --as to void any esteemed worth in the claim. Indeed, there is a pillar of duty that holds this up; as such an alternative has it's own needs, and thus provisional requirment. I never saw this 'alternative' as lively or fitting. Perhaps because we were not so grieved nor chastizing towards 'the common plan'. Better put, I felt fine in our duties, so long as they were simple; and that we could finish them, and live our way. Our life though was bent to the movement around me; and we were either a) prompted to respond to a higher deed, or b) adopt a baser form of that standard, ie. labor. I find such a life short and simple, but of a manner befitting the name. I've run from this, or at least taken oppertunities to be elsewhere. I also have had dreams, as to where we could go, 'as if in line with our play and care, but in ways that satiated the need that labor did; and in some cases more'.
 So I know not the rule, as I know not myself. Indeed, riches have flown thorugh me, and in that, like water down a mount, channels of depth and complexion have been dug. But not always am I like this; indeed, in such times, I wish to learn and grow, as if our sense, or some sort of motion, will bring life back to me. I am not sure where to go; whether we are headed for the right things; in consistency of the good we've grown so far, 'and to an increasing degree'. Ought we continue to garden? Is our wish to do so based on false ambition or hope, or more relevantly, fear? We are not so minimal after all, but practical, as to balance out your duty and time, for what is dear to you, and drawing of your enthusiasm and care. Things I'd say, of looks, games, words, breathe. Were just doing this for that sake. But have we been left with this, and unnecessarily? The dryess inquiry would say, 'could you not live well, and relate to Bella, or Amon, Bronte even. Could you not be a man worthy of their interest; yet one who, like in the way your dress, which is more or less open and uncontrary to such aim, so could you eat well, sleep proper --when your by yourself at least. Like drugs, could you not forgo what is pertient, for the enjoyment had; and let the comfort and fun given, soften your grief during the work ahead?' Are we a man though, of vision, to reason's offer and claim? Are we not able to experience wnoder and delight beyond that of a burdenless duty. Romance and friendship would be fun; but ought you change for this, or rather, ought you grow into the person who is such a man, living by reason, through practice and thus rule, and bear yourself to that? There may not be many like this? Imagine the anwser to this question was of the former sentiment, that you should moderate, and be more like others; amidst them at least, as to feel more pleasure and or fraternize. Imagine there were little folk here, and most people were as you are disposed to now. Honest through writing, spoken about our condition, able to elevate and or bear, concerned with felicity and experience. Is there really value in the folks who are like you, or are doing anything of this or that matter; or is it the elements themselves; the things and ideas, the right produced, that bear you deeper breathing, a sense of firmness in all duty and strife; a care for reading and grandeur, immersion and grace. Are these not good in themselves? Haven't you experienced them, and thus tilted yourself in that direction. Is this not why your so disposed? Do such things, however you are in pervew of them, not warrant both a respect and confidence; in the good they've brought and promise to further come? Has your sense of this, in all its naturally challenging development, not brought you away from intimacy and converse because of your choices? Being yourself takes time, for such things to come to fruition; as you are not trying to like other people, or get anything at that, but rather have someone like you, as regards shared interest or affection. For someone to wish to hear you, see you, hug you, maybe love. Someone who'd be alright with you dying for them, and per your own scenario, reasonably depart. "Someone who can share and exonerate my words; who knows and is fair in commpany of my concern and care. Who would humour my pithiest and fairest admissions and belief." I find much about women, which has been either tarnished and compressed by culture, and thereby men, or is just not natural; but there does not lie this content to an generous, spoken, howling way. "I generally meet no men, and more or less never a woman, who eats you with her mind; and is magnificent, wise, regal in virute, leisure and study: vibrant and kind." SOme old friends share our own flame in such way; but there memory at least holds nothing but embers; nor much but doubt, as to any flame far away razed. Our hope is not.
 The hardest thing, to be below, as it is in many ways not enough, is to live with such propiety to the mind of duty and liberty; that is, fashion deeds from burden, marble a pleasure baked in craft, recreation, measure and play; and somehow, enbody this with integrity and trust, so that when you fall, or your condition off, that you don't fall prey to melencholoy or doubt, but return to the grace you had. "The universes fabled trouble is in being not: as it is all there is: being different has the capability of not, and thus creates the hardest pull." Friendship is encouragment and passion for such thing; so that when failure happens or were lost, so doth wisdom, or at least assignment lay: were given aid to remember and feel fine where we are.
Hopefully we can have some constructive thoughts.
 Before speaking of elements, let us describe our contention.
 For one, we are stiff, again, and our neck hurts, and we want to curl up in a ball. I realize that this may just be from our exercise consistency, and returning to a present state; for which the adjustment is hard. And that we sit on the floor, which exasberates all this.
 But I notice primarily, that of what we lack, in ways directly about our neck, and things which encourage this and or deflate our care, that so are these burdened by unclear ideals and aims. As such, we feel we have to do more when we really don't; nor can we care for what we really do, and by that, tend to it as we are pleased to. Were so burried in concentration and work.

 So, I focus on what I like. Always have. As we got older though, the deeds of the world weighed upon: in their need and load. We altered ourself as to need little and feel less, but we've still learned about what is required. Your choice though is limited by initation; for once you are at some place, and in routine, then you are fine. And there is no place for wishes when you see, that what you want, is already given.
 I realize that in our development, as we wanted to be more personally, and sat on the floor, that our indivudal push was ever more; and thus, of basic norms, to did we rub against. In God and war. Much of our recent years have come in remedying our ignorance there; as we could only be so much. We are not perfect though; and we are holding back from things we'd like. I am not sure if i really want much though. There is room and reason to mingle and talk; but we get this at work.
So, our routine ahead is rather defined. And many of the elements are of a given; and only apt to change by our fancy or fortune. Overwhelmingly, these are of a good kind. Around us are mountains and fields; our job has good people, a good department, a gym, and is close to home. We are part of this company at any rate; and it does give some benefits; a fair amount as far as i can see; and this extends to potential family. The beat of our days ought follow: standing, lifts --including warrior, dogs and runners lunge--- recovery ---ie. twists, hips and neck--- and then yin, ie. deeper holds. These can vary in the mixture of poses (as is often the case in guidance), and in size --sequences being something which we go through, in short order.
 I feel as though our life has been pinned on our indivudation. I care not that were told something, or that there are rules; but that, of the rules, and the aim bounding them, so is this where I feel off. For whatever reason, we have never been able to sit with our own views; and had to both validate to ourselves that it was good, and by that extent, form dreams and promise; or justify to others, as if we were making a reasonable concession, 'a lesser yet personal life if you will'; or that it was sufficeint at any rate, 'for which we thwart shame and scrutiny'. The more we individuate, the heftier the repost. Much of our life has gone this way; and of the results, or at least devvelopments and soliditiy, so have these created friction in our want to be otherwise; and, it has created an enormous standard, for which we are worn from.
 One insight is that of choice; and that, of this path were on, that so have we positioned ourselve at various points based on circumstance, or flawed motives. As such, we are generally well, but feeling the weight from past choices, in where were currently at. As we reconcile that though, so may we also reconcile the bounty of other elements in our life; for which we have taken for gratned as being something in need of change, when in fact, our insight says their not. I accept the crime of others; nor do I humour the fiction and or tales of this. Of children, we are bound to much, in legacy, the times, our ability, and the reasonable need. Nor do I wish for one at all soon. Of women, so are we friends; and our comparison to others, who more actively, and fruitfully, ease the want of their loins, so are we not in despair; nor are we any longer fooled into thinking that our want for someone is a reason for us to exepct so, nor that they would want me. Our interests are uncommon, and our everyday life is surrounding work. I am grateful for this. Oppertunity lies ahead; but we are in fact not so poor, nor lacking content. I merely see room to apply ourselves towards things. But I need no excuse to feel pleased already, and at liberty to enjoy and pursue what we do. Trasniting to meet a woman, or hanging out with some friends, need not notice any imperfrection in this; but rather appreciate the good in it. But I also needn't fret if these are lacking, for I am warm elsewhere. Our choices and wanted bent bring us litte but peers; which is nothing little or to be unapprecative for. But of other bonds and or commradery, we either lack interest in the things commonly done; or we are not privy to an invite. Time opens me to this though; especially in our depth of relations with people you work with. These are not flaunts, nor for fraternizing; but you may share more and more about yourself, and be more present with them, as you persist in the duty which brings you there. Some may leave; but the store will forever be there; and many stay along. We've already eased into this.
 What I struggle with is in really being ourself, but for no other reason than it naturally sets us as so. For what I truly want, and am apt to address, brings less proclivity to mesh with others, in ways at least that bring us beyond simple/work friends. That, or we are interested elsewhere, and not compelled to go somewhere else. But this posture is an odd one; as you feel the weight of the world, the assertion of others, the opinions too: of the world, from all, and of what's important in our life, from old folk --especially our parents.
I cannot say with any positivity what there is to do. What I seek, and mean for.
 Much of these thoughts have been stationed above our passion and discontent. Where, of the ends that these ebbs and grooves made, so did we measure ourselves against.
 But I am not sure their there, in sincerity. The promise of things keeps me on; yet we seek this not for the material, but warmth of a path, and the clarity of direction.
 My issue is that, perhaps, we give too little notice to the thingas actually at hand: the gauge which accompanies us, no what where we go. For we have for an age relished oru content and the ease it brings. But we find this to not be truly fair or stable, as work still neeeds to be done. As such, we dream of a time, and our effort, when it can produce, or at least afford, a era when such content is truly so. But that is flaw you see: in what you hope for to really be content.
 For it is like the weekend after all; that upon its rise, we see the fun ahead; only to fret as the set occurs and work is near; that, or we let that return occur without us noticing, and feel its effort even more.
 I prefer, or at least finbd room to address this, in line of how we already live; that our content is not absent of effort and deed, but only of that which purely serves me. Food and stead is no small matter, and is not much able to be achieved; less your gifted it, which tangles what it means to be rightly. And so we must work. But, as exercise gives more than a mean to craft and breathe, so does it feel good, give some form and feeling to your day. It is more ought than taste which binds us to it: but of both we earn the effect ---as needed, or glad to receive. And work is no different. In fact, what we rightfully meant by our choice to be here or there, is as guided by our arrangement to work, and all the solidity it brings. For we are either gifted it, without, or providced for by our effort. Lacking all of these proves near futile, as you must eat, and it is too cold to surive. And if given a gift, then so, of something fundemental, so is the precarious nature of this station one in which your never so principally eased. For you fear that you will have to go back on your onw. Only, the secruity of that is undubibale; as the condition for your satiety is in showing up. This is the hard part; as the demanding nature of work is not so brimming with excitment, 'to be forever present, as your provision is ever afforded'.
 I nearly met the point now, and at this point have near mastered it, that of this placement, of some work and stead, that so is this manageble under a peaceful standard. It is rough, but so can you amend: sleeping well, adjusting your shoe, eating good and stetching. Our time at work, as it stands, is not much different than what it would be: and so I percieve myself as being part of what our needs are in need of. And it's not so bad. Foremost, is that your moving, going to and returning to work, with life and breathe between. The upward draft of this holds much promise and oppertunity, excitement at least, for on times banner, you are set. In a low state, this brings gloom, as if your awning path is to be the same. But the matter though, for which rightness and comfort are claimed, is when your in such a arranged state, but happy. "For which you are not but glad to further see."
food has always been a challenge. For one, comfort food always carries some degree of vital costs. While whole foods, may still offer comfort.
 Part of the issue is that you don't always want things that would in any normal case be good. I lookt at milk for instance and see, how I really would like some when were not to apt for fruit in the morning. Eating Kale and Tomoatoes are good, as they fill you without cost. But I am not always prying for an orange. An apple is a bit more neutral, and thus can be had with less discretion.
So, what would I like to do? Well for one, I'd like to, with the mean in mind, go over our Liberty book. I am familiar more or less with every bit of the Mason, but Liberty is far and about. There are gems and insights that I haven't read for an age, or even regarded as present. 'Near Me' for isntance, has several of them. I attempted this with our collection, in bringing similar things together. But our strands group has near 50 entries.
 I would like to find films and or music from corresponding directors, writers, actors and composers. Sorkin comes to mind, as I really like Moneyball and the Social Network. I found great tracks from Howard, in the Village; and I believe, besides him, that I could find other gems from Shore and Randy Newmam. We could also explore overlooked lists, and find novel entries, or at least nostalgic ones; or those which fit for slow down.
 I'd like to make a chart, that shows, through the year and months, where and when the moon/sun sets and rises. Our location is likley bound for this region; but the method can change. But our idea of things is off here.
 The fundementals of nature, government, science and lord, give room to see far ahead. "There are things" that justify this; and these are more important than peculiar dynamics, or cases of things. In our local land for instance, there are towns and services: this is more important to know than any specific figure. "That there is Vancouver and Ladner, is more important to know then what this consists of. For one thing happening in one place is blocked from seeming of the other. Being so spread out removes the saturation of cases. Specifics it would seem, allow for particular areas to have form. Maybe were lucky to live in Pitt Meadows, and hold no concern about what goes on. So, for Vancouver, where notable crimes occur, remembering that there are other towns, keeps our regional sense upright. Similarly, knowing of various countries, in say Uruguay and Peru, or Mozambique and South Sudan, so do these hold the world high."
In the case of health, you cannot just form yourself to the ends you seek; as you have mechanical limitations. As regards words, the adjustments can be dealt with via your mind and emtoins. While the incorporation of strenght training is not so liquid. Moreover, and writing does compare here, that you cannot neccesarily do exercises without the requisite rigor and ability. Doing them, or attempting to at least, only leads to a disgruntled motion: and an according effect.
 We have struggled then for some year or so, in our aerobic capacity and overal strenght. Were active in other ways, nor despairing over our appearence. But we still ought run, or do some form of training. But our present state is not too welcoming to it. As we cannot do much without reaching that overbearing state; nor do we sit so easily, while were on the ground doing other things.
 Mainly though, the issue is one of form; and how we have too little volume in anything we do. And doing higher things in a poor way, only leaves me more wonky.
 In that sense, it makes sense that you'd be content with normal squats, a few sets and reps; maybe practicing against the wall, or holding a Bosue ball. Equally, for pushups, elevation is key, as you can do them more or less, but in a full prone your bracing leaves the motion rigid --and your neck and shoulders bear the load. It also isn't unimportant to note, that your training is not so great when it is done dry and hasty. These bring range of motion, but no real vitality. An enormous amount of stretching and recovery is needed. Generally, per any effort done, we need some. But an special amount is needed per the grundge of movements. Warming up, and cooling down, can really aid this. Along with any groundwork to follow.
 Indeed, as you recuperate, or are privy to a solid and enduring base, so may you do more, and of higher kinds. Since you are establishing, doing that which affords volume and form --per your threshold and skill-- is priority. The treadmill allows this; though, the actual running fairs the oddness of doing so on the field; only the impact is slightly less; and there is to be fair an upbeat and constant effort during the process --and for some time too. This is an alright means to increase your aerobic capacity; and also warmup for some squats, pushups and chin ups. These are only needed in the amount that you can do firmly and true. For squats, as they can be a bit mindless, just focus on your hips and arms, and do them with a sense of contentment. Pushups similarly: you should be pleased their being done. Chin ups ought be easier, if your bar is right.
 It is also essential to note, that your grievences regarding health are not entirely so, if you don't work out. It is moreso the blend of activity, eating and rest that you do per the state your in. One or two exercise sessions won't change this. Even if a moment they do, so will you return to a prior state; in which the need to recover is needed, along with rest. My feeling then, is bring yourself back to a firm standing, by going on the treadmill and doing some full range. This will help tremendously; and bring more rigor and ease when doing free range and cardio. But don't push this. Even if you only run, or at least speed walk on the treadmill; that would be fine. Then, from what you know, have determined, or have gathered as a resource, stretch and recover, as you should, and feel free to. Make sure you eat, in both smoothies, kale and nuts for breaky, milk and or fruit for breakfast and lunch, then some small bowl of barely for first grain, followed by quinoa and lentils --with some corn, rice and sauce too-- then lead into breads and other starches; ending sometime around 9. Milk, carrots, tea, kale, maybe some fruit can be had after. But 4-9 (with some barley right before) is your grain bay.
These words, which come tricling from my mouth, have the sort of intent of one who is both calm and with peace; but also as someone who is unsure, reaching, or otherwise qualming the unwanted stirrings in their heart.
 For an age it feels like, we've tenured a depth to our way; a course which is rich in both character and resolve, but succour also.
 But along this path the soil has been speckled with rot, 'the passion of our soul'. For by nature's oblige and our awkward response, or our mere entaglment with other's humours, so have roots and or tungs nestled in, 'catching us when were down, or elating us too far when were up'.
 later in the age we addressed this; finding less satiety nor stability in merely tending to ourselves. This turbulence, or friction at least below, became more apparent as our formation faded, 'and we were but ourselves; and thus aware of uneveness'. The result, is that we curried an end which was less prominent in harsh edges, nor prone to inlauded inputs; which is to say, we altered our knowledge of things, as to not be intruded by poor views and concepts.
 As it were, we marbled our ways with the latter, but tended foremost to the former; landing in a lane of extremes, to one of normalcy, yet fashioned with sweet resolve. As such, time went on, and we were high in our equilibrium. But the holes in our knowledge gave way; as perhaps, rooted deep, were passions that our equally lack of deep knowledge could not content with. At first this lead to enthusiasm and excitment, 'about the promise of what those deep passions dreamt of'. But as such sights became mere visions and nothing solid, we atteneded to the breadth of things around, as would poke and or prodd such depths. Only now, a chapter at least ahead, have we realized that those depths are what need mending; and that no matter how much you know, so will you be the measure.
 Indeed, it has been our candor of want that has sprouted discontent. For in our time of peace and plenty, those uneven depths pushed us to seek more, or be more at least. We studied war and philosophy, wrote endlessly: to both rich and weary ends. But all did this lay upon those uneven grounds; where natures of purpose and satiety lie. Indeed, I still fiddle with the cranks of inquiry, as to temper those flames of discontent. Truly though, I see the need to strengthen our heart and bones, and worry not that their kindling.
 It is ever important though to understand that this approach is none of pure self regard, but one that admitts to the reality and place of things abound. What is best said, is that as we need our own solidity in care and truth, so we need this for leaf and throne. For so long we've tried expanding our views of those latter, and lessening the effects of our ignornance, and or the sparks of our callous views. But these have never taken hold; as our own heart and way bears no set or bound foundation: and what surrounds us has no means to fair better.
So, it seems a good starting principle that these words not be judged on anything other than our pleasure; and as such, we spell well for we want to, but need not avoid declamations and or preludes for the sake of avoiding poor form or brevity.
 As such, I see that our rules in life are not made as to relieve us of thought and care, but rather worry. For if we know what needs done, 'as is determined by that which, if missing, makes our time unwell', that so may we feel calm and free, and approach our attendances well.
 In this way, I see that work requires our headphones and their charge, along with our phone and its; our thing of trail mix, and kale if we want some --as fiddling through a bag in the morn is poor. We need our shirt and sweater washed, along with underwear that will work, and socks at that.
 Generally, for sleep, we ought have our linings by our side, our overwhere too; whether we begin bare or note, we oought be able to reach over and put (say) our gloves on. When we wake, we ought forward fold, and perhaps squat, then lie on our back. In general, and this goes for all days, if you wake up, 'and know not the time', simply stay on your back, perhaps one knee over the other, and lay. Going to your side or front only brings you to sleep. As such, move your blankets over, and remove whatever layers discourage you being on your hind. Yuo may naturally rise then. Indeed, if it is the middle of the night, recede to your sleep. But if you know you know.
 Another thing of importance, which counts for all days, is packing your food, as cleaning your pot at night is questionable. If you have wallet, phone, buds, trailmix, and your baluclava and bottle are in your bag, your good. Since the colder months are coming, we will have to keep check of our buffs and gloves, and that of our jacket to.
 As for our merry ways, it seems fine and dandy that you stand and fold in the morn. Leave the twists and turns for more active points; this is the case for recovery also; recognize the difference between your back and resting legs, to holds and knots, 'the crossvers if you will'.
 As for stability and rise, your downdog is enough, and chaturanga if it is right. You may inclue runners lunge and twist, with warrior, 'which I'd say is all too looked over, and akin to D-Dog, is an essential given to your day, enrichening you from merely doing it'.
So I thought that we were a being, and that this was evident by our motions and activity; and that, perhaps not clearly coming form this, but something further abound, that I know, and is also evident, is the value of knoweldge.
 One factor that was near to this was of 'seeing what there was', in terms of things; as both a remedy of feeling awry by virute of not knowing, and cementing the means to more amply and merrily be. For the point was made that much of our wishes and wants, and ideas and or figuring to boot, surrounded the anticiapation of things; stating what was to come, relishing how good they were. And this runs a little contrary to the present experience, and feels more like a response to our lacking. I stated similarly, where I said 'sex and food are not poor, in themselves or as pleasures, but gluttony and horniness are otherwise; as they are throbbings, and in some cases unwarranted cessations'.
 Another point was the matter of responsibility, and how this bears me fundementally. Many relate, or compare rather, in their innate want of friends; and indeed I have this like as well. But I do not often connect with others on the basis of, or at any rate given the fact, that I am so responsible.
 Indeed this manner of oblige comes from observation and the appreance of things causing us to feel a certain way; which may come in the form of showing whats worthy of aversion or habit. As it were, our histroy with this began when we were a young man, and the cares of fun and ambition --or meager careers-- was lacking; and in our time otherwise we noticed that it was influenced by what we ate and whether we exercised [ie. running, stretching, recovery, and later on our manner of sitting]. Another element though is the accompying force of expectation, be it from family or 'teens in skinny jeans'. Of what we were looked at as lacking, so we overted from any alternative; nor had the fortuity to let the judgement slide. In our current day, the need for the former has left, or at least been levelled off --in both what were accepted for, and what we fairly do, with some promise also ahead. The latter though is tough, and the massive impression from media leaves me feeling a little burdened by the world. My views feel disjointed and sparese; and the rhetoric and display from others feels horribly mixed with idealism and dissmay (that I wasn't being active), but also phoniness and unworthy credulity. This perhaps speaks to any lingering shortness of a large foundation, 'whereby the norm of careers and family are not held, but we bear nothing else --of things so known and far set'. There is also the fact of our own want of credulity, as simmered during our late adolescence, when we though ourselves worthy and set for glory and acclaim, based on the valor and fruits of our responsibility. It is to some extent a dampener when watching so much media, as you feel there is no room for anything besides it, and thus you; for which your anticiation is faded. But foremsot, as our actual magnanimity and want has whithered away, so do we feel wrong for not being invovled 'as our path to glory would take us'. We feel then hidden and worthy of shame, and also pestered over the apparent discourse, 'for which change will rise from'. This makes the world feel meant for dissaray.
 All that is to say, that of our being, so is observation, or honesty and insight rather, the currator for your motions being re-aligned and set; as we are raised, make poor choices, learn and grow, and perhaps find something better, or realize something we were doing was for the worst. The honesty comes from noticing what is either good or off, while the insight is in connecting the cause. Reflection could be a general approach to assessing this; but in particular, it brings in the questions and or revelation of who you are.
 Where I lie then, is that of the good from things of the furthest sort, so they never appealed to me. And I never took much to them being deemed neccessary, but rather worth favoring: for which I didn't. And as I leaned in to the things I cared for, at the time at least, I then noticed how my diet and exercise effected these. And by attending to that, 'as if we were now working around some newly discovered truth', other interests came to bear, namely our love of the sun and outdoors --and later on, the blue hour.
