In arms togther, filling one another's heart with laughter and releief, 'as we work throogh our common debts'. We have a literal home, with bed, computer and tv that fails to suffice. Our mother is too caring, as is our father too giving. And our sister is but a nice aside. Our two cats, 'who replaced the darlings we lost', are as good as the gods have might. And the trees, oh my our scenario, and perhapds per a theme, our land, are at the peaks of swell. For mountains litter around, and vast scapes 'where farmers share the view'. In any bit, there is oppertunity and means to look at the sky: the vibrant blues before sunsent, and that deep purple grey upon her set. Just the air as we breathe, and output of the cheapest bread, makes this time one worth no worry, nor lack of grace. A friend though to share it with? Well I have Tristan, who like Matt is a gaming friend. I don't quite like that. But the moral of this story, and perhaps the point of all, that whether you have nothiing or alot, or as remarks whats possible and oppertune, that it is you who arrives, and must bear the endurance and or creation to thrive, 'so that for all who settle there, of peace even., that you may ampt take a hand'.
I see a great deal that we have done which is right; though not with much consistency. "Its as if you have a boatload of good behind you, but currently have flat sails."
 To portray my life, we must simply ask, 'are we done? Have we more to do?'. For if this is so, then why are we lacking what we do. Why aren't we purusing money and women, glory and fame?
 Is it perhaps so that you are not done? For you see what others might not, that what the world has to offer is there for those who move themslves in a certain way. By that count we are not done, merely handling and progressing that breadth of things. Things which let you view the stars, honor the sky, seek clouds over mountains, 'and find warmth on even the coldest of days'.
 What I know is merely such that there exists within us a sort of key; as if a flap of fabric that when tigthened reveals great humour and truth to things. Your expanded in a way, as if there were some quesiton you did not know was asked; and now you have your own anwser. Indeed, much of philosophy, or reflection at that, comes down to the wish you have to be right, and known as so, when your steadier state which brought on such inquiry and supposition teaters off into disease and want.
 It is worthwhile to state that what knowledge is to me are those insights that come upon the cease of this tottering; whereby we return to a healthy state; and not reset into our amorous glare, but rememebr what brought us away, and how things are in terms of what brings us there or not. Better put, we bear insight to there being a sort of lever, and that we managed it poorly. And it is one thing to take note of action, 'and how you may better grip the handle', and another to view the mechansim at play. For there are many things and many ways to proceed. "Were not merely re-tracing a line and blushing out a spot where we are coloring was off; rather, we are learning the methods of craft, as to make what our feelings drive us to. It is a bountiful process, rather than managed one. In that, the end is known, and you merely wish to foloow the procedure; while knowing the rules and direction to follow, you may twist and wind as you humours pull you. And indeed, such hunmours are what feel off when we've lost our way. And upon their re-entering, we are not only able to further see, but do: which is a nice pairing. "
What can I say, of Gods and men, peace and prosperity.
 The billing always fits itself to the bearings of honesty. For none who reap anything do so in being blatantly unso, and the more you go, the more you feel its abscence.
 I say to me and all, that inside lies a world of responsibility; and ceasing the shortcomings of one leads to dealing with others. I feel there is a vector for experiencing beyond this, 'when nothing pulls your side'; but that is a large development to bear. For the truth is that the cease in luggardness, 'which responsibility is souly primed for receding', leads to nothing more than unimpeded judgement and stride. "I need no compulsion, or will rather, to see the sun burning the evening sky: I am brought by it by my open eyes: and unwrestled by our weighty dinner, nor any conviction to bear us past our heavy stride."
 The truth is that we do things. And life is not merely about making smart choices, but bearing yourself to that which provides. We take for granted that home and stead are available. In fact we have more often than not experienced this: the restless reach for job and board. Yet, these are a different kind of regard; as is the case for the strenghts required to approach danger, but then deal with it's dealings. And so in life, ought we rememebr that we are a) not in danger, nor b) unemployed. Indeed, this latter has been taken for granted, for the ability to quench this is different than having it now. For you are not only out of shape when it comes to it's certain deed, but also in that restless search, whereupon you are looking for but without a job. Often we have been unemployed yet seen the ability to change when we wanted. Yet entering that pursuit brings with it the trouble and sense of difficulty.
 The point, is that cirucmstance leaves it's dangerous and or restless state --whereby we lack what we need, or find ourselves in frictioned pursuit--once were stable: at least, when were stable we have what we need, and the matters of regard switch to our life's qualities around this. For such needs are out of our control: we need food and thus what offers; and as such, we need to be as to earn. Indeed, that earning brings with it a mold by which our surrounding qualities, 'beyond what we've earned', can sit upon. But not, in error, abound. Fortune may have played our hand, and given us the taste or familiarity for things beyond neccessity; but we are not judging that, 'as if one should concede there finer livings and retreat to simplicity'. Were merely talking about the proper form of things.
There is this matter of being at home, and how it pertrudes our sense of propiety, or at least amends to things other than money. There is just too little room to hold these regards outside myself. Indeed, we may make this into principles, as to formulate what is inside, and by that give it some bearing and strucutre. And in a similar way, our desires bear an equal form. All things of a compulsive sort, noble or not, rub right against this sense. And if we forgo our confidence, 'in pleasure to come', then we are left with our lacking state amidst our broad oblige. An oblige which we cannot truly settle on, or sit with rather in slower times. In upright ones, we have no doubts nor grief from others. We have no desire though to be thwarted, nor duties which we need clarity to --to affirm.
 I think when it comes to girls, friends, money and fortune, that these things are never going to be sought or recieved. We simply have our prescence; and it may create oppertunity. But our energy is not enough, rather ouro invovlement with things. And I find ever truly that our bevy of deeds are an impingement to this, when they a)are not dealt with, nor b)surely seen. That is why it is a bit easier to live away from home, 'or away from those who speak clearly to you, yet in a way uncorresponding to me'. I feel wrong to luster and dream, and by that anticipate. At least, we take for granted how little were invovled, and willing at that. I imagine some possibiilty of different work to come. But I really can't find the care to do so now. And how this will change is so for finding true progress, 'where parts of your life are truly taken care of'. Otherwise we deal with them.
 I have expeirence with friends, in romance even; and I surely garulously watch porn, and view maps; as have I heard podcasts of people talking of things I like, or seeming either glorious and or wise. These things feel like they bring you something, and are thus worth pursuing. But the pursuit itself is not so obvious within this; nor does the attraction feel totally worthwhile, considering the foundations beneath it.
 There is something about practice and commisery, where you have some meaningfull gauge by which pleasures are avoided, or viewed with discretion; and how, generally good things such as eating whole, stretching, writing and or stability, that these provide a bounty of natural goods: the sun, the clouds, the street, games and personality (or hunour). The hardest part of making friends, is that even if you like the same thing, you still have to mention the pillars beneath, and how we are living with certain things in mind. That I ought, or in some cases should, sleep via prior steps, or refrain myself so I may write as wonderfully as I do. And games are no different; as they have this sensibility about them, in how long I play, when, how I consume media around them; how I anticapte them even. They are branches for my reaching arm. But such a reach is not disposed to anything but its regulated and also abundant want.
 The heart of philosophy, and what really remarks the merrit of those who've reached anything in it, is in the acts and or methods that brought their 'philosophizing' to fruition. Inquiry, skepticisim, referring to things and verifying sources, and elements at that. And as such, honesty is the true form of all things good and reasonable; for you admiit of your natural uncertainty in things, and by a process attempt to know, 'rather than make statements as if you already do'.
 I say this, for our directive is one merely born of insight, as perhaps born through mixutre of our history and character; whereby we had nothing, but were open and motivated to see things; things that upon certain efforts, we understood such effects and experiences could be had. We've stuck to those, struggling immensley to find any joy that did not come from this; as the most simple attempt is nearly unrelatablke or clear to others; whom without, there is little other pleasure, 'besides that which our own efforts have made come'. Truly this has been the mode of our way, from 2015 and on. So much so, that that first stage of this thought-based living, lead me to rely on it even more. I note that all our imaginging of what it would be otherwise holds little of the heart or matter which our own experience is judged by. Its as though we were looking at two maps, and judging them based on their areas, as to determine worthwhile interest. "For we could only travel one." One had great cities, a few at least, and many fine localles. But much of the land between them was bare. "Like Las Vegas." The other had little towns and villages; home to the same essentials, but with less height and decour in their towers --what housed their material. But around each settlement, ran belts of mountains, patches of grass, tree and shrub. The air was clear, and thus brought all grades of morning through night light. The countries position at any rate held a novel course for the moon. And despite the other land being relatively warm, it was more or less arid, and thus home to a conssitent haze. That, and there were not the natural ups and downs of rain, but thereby no accesible texture or scenery for the sky. "No whisps for the light; no coolness to the wind."
 The second you stop eating without discretion, you loose a hole bunch, in both ability and interest. You work too little for expensive food, nor find delight in eating what is beyond what is affordable and proper. Lacking a car has it's own drawbacks, as your limited to who you can see; at least, those who you would travel for are hard to come by. Overall, it's just hard to have fun when there are bits of scrutiny worthy to common things; nor as much enthusiasm by your free heart. I simply don't care, or much at that. Moreover, I'd just rather be somewhere else, despite the lonely land. There are animals though, as are we our own friend. The trees and land afford their own commisery, and I only miss one who would share my deeds for such venture.
I reckon that the woes and highs of life are not what writing is used for, but rather the insights which our high or low experience offers: writing affords us reflection for what that show.
 What I have been shown is the importance of eating and exercise. One element of the former is that certain foods are better in conjunction with effort, while others are good for rest. And indeed, exerxise done in right amounts, positions you to be in place where rest is welcomed, or that of action. Indeed, it should be said, that exercise equally has forms that are fitting for rest, and others for growth and times trying
The words of most worth are those which stear your path, 'and keep you away from wanting and or fearful moments'.
 We take for gratned that we breathe, but not that we work. And indeed, all of tradition surrounds this, as do our actions. But settling into this, 'for the deed is a reoccuring one, rather than soul feat', makes for a life which, per the constant demands, is peacefull: as the thing every going, in deed, is met. And much the virtues which bring you hear, to be so thoughtful and aligned, reward you with the sturdiness and ease that comes from such perpetuity. And indeed, the mind of someone whose so is open, 'in that their unperturbed nor unsure'; and this makes you susceptible to be lit by things around: be it pleasure and joy, or chance and oppertunity.
 It would be a shame for someone, be it by fortune or sense, to bare fruit but then live unreasonable. Do kings and millionaires not still have family, and civil efforts? Does self-regard not serve them there, and avoid them errors and discontent? Nor is it irrelevant that the nature of their fruits are not in themselves of the leisruely and or crafty sort. Indeed, oppertunity fits us with better handles to provide ourselves wiht, and less grough at that. "It also aids in our fraility and or futlity. Comfier landings when we fail and recalbirate." Ought we see the point though, be it with soft sides or not.
I'd be in a clearer mind for certain matters, but for this I'm fine.
 For one, I've written that our yoga need be standing, strenght/stability/energy, or seated. We have the means to link the end of a video (in its cool-down) for the prior two; while a seated video needs no skip. One reality is in describing videos --part of why I wanted thumbnails in our past venture. For one, we don't need an exhaustive amount of videos, just one's that work; and the titles become thereby known. You can always write description --of what she wears-- along with the progression of the video. In the notes you can speak more to the worth, and by that cement what is familiar and good to you.
 I am finding some value in legumes, compared to grains. I think they go really well with sauces; and act as a protein. There are many kinds, and again, having sauces really helps --be it tomato, butter, curry. Our mornigns are quite solid, with kale and nuts, oranges and smoothies, and granola + dried fruit also. And i feel good with potatoes, popcorn and a variety of bread. But grain is actually a little weird. I am not opposed to tuna or anything, if growth is your aim; but at any rate, lentils+corn+edamame is a great meal on it's own, and lighter in grain. I think thats the point, that you may use legumes as a base, as you would barley --as a heartier grain. Then you may add corn and edamamame or sauce. The storage may help when we a) change cupboards, but also b) our tupperware. We have so much variety that it would be best to have the ability to poor right from the bin.
Christmas has been swell. We got Dragons Dogma and Astrobot; nor are we bored of Diablo or Battlefield. Our Ark fuel is still for 9 days, and Witcher is calling me. I played a bit of Dragon's Dogma, and besides our odd arms while walking and the need to prompt each dialogue piece --hopefulyl both can be changed-- its actually quite fun, has great music, a really cool charaacter --though he doesn't speak. Combat is still getting familiar, but all in all its warm and promising. Though no worries; play as you will. Were playing Forest tomorrow, which is nice. Besides that, everything has gone well. The gravy and stuffing have me a little poor; but no yorkies by me and were good. Also, we did those bench pushups friday and saturday; the last day we did a bit too much and felt off sunday. Our structure is good though; and yesterday we did some free range and a bit of running; dropped and did one squat; then did that soul squat followed by supine --in our her black attire. That was good, and I feel much better being active in our full range, free range, a bit of running, and our standing, mixture of warrior and stability, and groundwork to. Fairly even. I find that were not excited for much food. We did have those chocolate oat bars, which are fairly whole. But I just wanted some food --with our sardines also, and milk, which has been nice. Potatoes remain a good, and dates afford a nice intro for something lighter yet sustaining. Bread remains varied and usefull. But I find cooking grain to be a consistently demanding, moderatley satsigying, nor any more clear affair. I think the balance of legumes, pouring tupperware and sauces can help. But I wont have yorkies, no way. Nor do I wish for chips or cereal; and yogurt just feels barely more satsifying then better things. Everything put together though removes the ware that comes from one thing completely absent, and thus our worry about emphasizing one particular thing --to the loss of all others not having their place felt.
Lets get specific. We need tuperware that lets me pour right from it. First, having this will allow us to store things in the outside fridge; which gives us free reign. But being able to pour helps tremendously, as you don't have to take lids off of each and scoop everything out. It encourages mixing to do it the pouring way.
 As for what we need, we have quinoa, which often sits alone, as does barley and lentils. There is also beans, corn and edmamame, which are shorter term things.
So I am looking for some sense of principal as regards the world of Sanctuary.
 But I will speak to our current builds. Notably, Heartseeker requires little resource management, as we get +4 to innervation, and the % chance from our dagger. Based on our high attack speed, the resources aren't an issue. Our bow can be modified for pure damage and defense.
 We have +4% DR on shrouds and the crit strike = shroud aspects on our perdition and cowl. This frees up our pants entirely. I haven't checked, but on our flurry build epsecially, our defenses are strong. Only having Tibaults leaves me quite weak. I need to clarify how much were benefiting from attack speed; our amulet may serve more utility than it currently does. Otherwise, we can have a utility/defense aspect on our pants; and indeed, +5 to heartseeker may serve as a comaprable multipler to tibaults. And our crit chance is already very high due to 45 from perdition and 20 from starless. We could also use Tassets, for extreme elemental resistance; which makes bossing easier.
 As for general rules, Obols are not to be overlooked; as you can often find the uniques you want; and if a pant, gloves, chest or helm, the cost is little. Two handers are much more, but still. The Goblin event happens to have given us alot; but we can still take account of pits, world events and obol sigils --see how much their worth. This can make way for bossing, which has a catch 22. gambling is a direct approach otherwise.
 Sigils are also precious now. There are two forms; those which a progressing character woudl appreciate, such as equipment delve, obols, and not sure what else. The point being, that as a developing character, you probally don't have so many sigils, and are likely to have weeaker ones --considering the small percentage for armoury's, breaches or horadrics. That, and the season gifts may not be present. There are great things at any rate; though, the point is perhaps that higher torments beenfit better sigils; and so when your lower, you might hold those off.
 Economy wise, tribute of harmony grant's runes. And indeed, better on higher torment. But, runes afford so much value, and are in esscence, the path to riches. At heart, runes are valued for their use in making mythics. There may be other ones; but I find this principle holds. Perdition, Cowl and Sarless skies are the most prized, and thus Jah, Bac and Ohm are most valued. There are a few other legendary runes of high value also. But some of the rare runes, such as Kry are worth a fair bit. Even some of the magic runes are worth alot. Clumps of magic runes are valued on some 100m scale, while those of rare are a couple hundred; while legendaries from 50-200 on their own, and 400-600 for Jah, Bac and ohm. This makes tributes of harmony extremely lucrative. And again, consideirng that the better you are the more stuff you'll get, which only exemplifies the power you can recieve to do better, that following these principles when your relvatively weak is still fine, in order to make that development. Indeed, I enjoy doing well, and fighting bosses. Killing t4 Duriel is immensely satisfying compared to t1. And doing so within an instant is also rewarding.
 Another thing is loot. Besides certain cache drops --from whispers surely-- you can sell your high GA gear for some good cache. Often, within your early hours of torment, you may find gear that has several affixes. From tortued gifts even. And these are worth a great deal. I sold a 4 GA Lucian for 1.5 bill. Keeps these options in mind. You might benefit from selling a triple GA weapon for 500 mill. Though, make sure it's not account bound.
So I liked Amon from the moment I saw her. On that first day, I noticed her. I developed an interest, and then some sense she liked me to. I recall her remarking, 'oh, your leaving'; and this meant something to me. I also remember speaking to her more or less on our own, and feeling like, 'we really don't know eachother, and my interest for you is all affection, rather than a bond'.
 Eventually I gave way to the likelihood that she did not like me; or at any rate, I just felt it was better to move on.
 And so we did, for a while. We had a similar want for Mikala, then Bella, the thought of Kassandra even came to mind. But then a month or so ago, I shared some enthusiasm with Amon, about her picking ability. Which, to be fair, is great. But for a few days I kept mentioniong this; and as far as I knew, we were laughing, and nothing much else. But then one day, as I came in and went to the LPs, she was there and gave me a hello that sprung from a sense of familiarity. Normally one says hello and gives a name, for formal reason. Or it is a hello in passing. But seldom do you say hello with familiarity. I do to Tristan, as we know eachother, and I need not speak his name. For whatever reason me and Amon are like that. And this is weird to me, as I can't help but feel that this is from some base of affection. And for the weeks following I took it to mean that way. This brought much angst and worry though, and feeling that we were far too involved, and with no reciprociuty from her. I heard someone make a solid remark, that if she liked you then she would show it. And Amon has shown no such thing.
 In some sense, I feel permitted to like her, and that if I did, she would be open to me. But we don't really like her: we want her to like use. For we are not truly there. That is what makes friendship so easy; as the Gods have given us right of reason, as to employ ourselves for our own tranquility and delight. And a friend is one who shares this with you; as opposed to one who you spend time with beyond your other efforts, and act as though the job is done. For those come back, and there is still more. "The spirit which binds all together; for you to see this fact of continuity."
 No matter, me and Amon are not like that. But what are we? I still feel frazzled when she is at work; and I have these thoughts and feelings about, 'oh, if she's over there, how cool would it be if I run into here'. Similarly, I notice when she leaves the department; as if she should be talking to me. I find reason to believe that her actions do not signal true want. But I feel that our bend on the matter is overly pitched, and that were noticing more irreverance on her part than is due.
 Perhaps it comes down to foundations, which hold all things. I don't believe the point is that if we just uphold ourselves, that we'll naturally be more open with her and recieve. I feel there is something more right than this. But, as for her place in all of this, or how we'd see her, that is something that we likely wont need to state.
 let her go is what I have to say. I don't wish to follow her and indulge in over-passion. I have not the offer to warrant that, nor the approval of such obssession. And as for the more reasonable oppertunity to bond, so is there no apparent connection or relatibility; nor the scenario to explore if there would be, considering that we work together, and that our prime motive is of said passion. We haven't even had a conversation with her. Yet this doesn't bother me, for were here to work, provided, and ahead of that, think, reckon, formulate and oblige. I believe that we wish for her to like me, as we did Ashley and all those lasses abound, for we did not truly have connection in mind, but rather an emotionally wish, 'while we kept on our way'. This was the impetus of mine and Holly's relationship. I quote, 'I want her, for she excites me and soothes our working time, but also gives comfort and warmth while I pursue our other passions, or efforts at least. Moreover, if I had her, think of what I could do; think of the fire beneath me that could lift me beyond our current bindings: the confidence we'd have to pursue.' And indeed, the moral of life, as I've experienced it, is that these wants, 'for ointments, fortune, approval and relations even', is all because you have some far and bearing want; which is itself born from the thing you lack: and the insight to such and determined revival and re-heading, is the moral. "Take from it the point which you need to work on now; be it in admission, determination, and forbearance."
So Thomas Taylor's version has the s-f issue, but the intro, which is very long, is normal. It seems to be comprehensive, and holds (what I noticed) some self-regard by the author, who makes a mention of this being his translaiton and preface; which I like, as this is his work and treatment, and thus I can see him forming some broad view by which Plato would be viewed --and it thus seems less demonstrable, but par for the norm, or at least from his persepctive; meanig I can both appreciate and respect his intro. The works themselves all have the f issue, as do their intros, so all there is is the main intro
There is a short bio-view on Taylor, I believe Thomas, and his reputation as a Platonist. Of old sort and thus well-written. Probally worth reading some of Taylor before this ---written by William E. Anon
One thing to note when looking for things to read, is that a) your looking for older texts, as modern ones are either dumbed down or full of overly-modern pedantry. That isn't bad, but there's little 'treatment', as it were, and is instead hyper-scholarly, and non-self-driven fluff. Old work has a great mixture, and births more ideas of our own, and appreciation for the merrit of the questions at hand. Secondaly though, is that considering this search, your not always going to have access to many books. Almost all are avaialble or not on archive, and if not, big whoop. My point though, is that if you can just find one treatment of Arisottle's Metaphysics, or one commentary of the Republic, or a great intro regarding kant and hume, this is enough.
 Building on this thought, the work from A.E Taylor, who gives a vast commentary on Plato as a man and his works, is one of those things that remarks the worth of any reading of this kind. As you learn about the world, elements of it which relate to his time in the Polis, or the academy; you also learn about other people who knew him or are comparable, such as Aristotle, Thrasymarcus, or some Roman counterpart. In equal fashion you learn the rules of philsophy, by which the treatment at hand is overlooking. In regards to A.E's work, it seems a little stiffer than I normally go for, but the heart of it is there, and the content, or offering rather, is nearly a pure delight, and thus a worthwhile oblige.
 One note is that given this comprehensive text, you may indeed rest on it and thus warrant some pleasure in finding other pieces. But do not let your search wander far, nor feel dissapointed if you find not; nor should the good found be measured lower based on it not having what you novelly sought. See if there is some written style you prefer, and in the realm of content you particualrly prefer. "A nicely written bio is all the more ripe." But accept what you have, don't add for the sake of adding, nor feel like your missing out; for we have other lands to go to, nor a stomach which can eat all this food; nor is it hard to be pleased with what we have, and that is indeed our priority. "Rather than increasing our anticiaption."
 I am just accepting the Plato work from Taylor --who may or may not be the esteemed Taylor. But no less, Aristotle is next, and there is a seamingly fine book by a gentleman named Ross.The intro, like most in books like these, is solid, and not overly long. Then there are sections for each of his subjects --not neccessarily on specific works. The Metaphysics shines for me most, and logic seems a fair ground --hoping we hear about the tenants and rules by which he makes his particular mark. There is a second chapter on the Philosophy of Nature which could be nice. I am going through a collection of essays, each treating a certain subject. But the intro is a clear exmaple of how style differs and thereby our taste. This one is just not to the point and feels more meandering than informative. "Our care for something else feels all the cleaer." I will note that for Aristotle I wish to learn, and generally about the rules of philosophy that he made prevalent, and by that grew. He differs from Plato in my mind, as he is more broad thinking, ie. in cosmology. Plato seems more like a historical root of philosophy, while Aristotle is a root of science and proper thought. Because of this, I am fine with hearing a story of Plato and the Republic; while in Aristotles case I wish for truer insight. Because of that, a chapter on Logic or Metaphysics is of worth in different books, as they are all treated a bit differently. Considering the varied levels of reward, those pieces that are less straighforward may afford clearer sight on things than those which otherwise are. In Barnes --the collector-- edition, the Logic chapter (written by Robin Smith) is initially intriguing, based on it's emphasis of Aristotle forming the basis for arguments and proper thought. I figure that we'd get different angles, and otherwise weights to the overal form of said subject; and likely more, as it has pieces (each written by a different author) on the standard subjects. A soul-author such as moore, 'who is an esteemedprofessor of Aristotle', may hold a more unified and diligent output, while this collection may have different stlyes and or hues per each piece. "And indeed, if we learn so much as to become aware of what there is to know, then good on it." I am going to look for one more thing, a alternat perhaps to a unified piece --one that is not so subject by subject. From Werner Jeigner, there is a historical development of Aristotles life and thought; this is great, but the addition has some pretty hainous underline. This really shouldn't matter,-considering the content of the book. J.L Ackrill houses a collection (again, but many authors of certain essays) on more particualr topics, on both Aristotle and PLato. These are much more tecnhical and seem to offer insight to the rigors of certain questions. Not entirely what I'm looking for now, but surely there's a few interesting one's in there.
 As it were, we have the two subject based pieces, one souly composed by Ross, and the collection by Barnes, each with their own appeal and take-away. Then we have Jaeger, and his historical development. Compared to Ackrills technical pieces, this is itself a wordier effort than those of the prior two; yet the content is of an appoachable sort, and something you want detail. Not in arduous ways, as to spend 100 pages on his first poop; but there seems great variety and development via the table of contents. Plato has many selections, but the one from Taylor seems fair enough. My interest in the Republic is more from familiarity, and likely we care for all things, ie. Meno, the academy etc. And we can specifcy later on. I will note, that from this I find appeal in developments of modern philosophy, as to learn the rules which we generally work from now. I ultimately wish for a better sense of why arguments are used, and what remarks their greatest form. These kinds of books we've been looking at seem like good inelts for such thought; and indeed I enjoy it.
So I came out of my tent, looking at the mountains north of me. My favorite sights they were; and so privaleged we are to be on the crest of our land, as to see further east also, and the big horn on the far edge of the range.
 I find myself taking notes, pondering a question which has become somehow clear, but not exactly so. It has to do with virute, or duty rather, and what I ought to do. I'll down the woody paths, which are themselves a great deilght; as they have a natural dip to the east and south, but to great fair; as this makes for fine ravines and rugged terrain; which is all fine to traverse if your on rocky and winding paths. "Hills are only bad on pavement."
 What my mind dwells on is this matter of a true cynic; someone who gleams at everyone as being unwise, and untruly happy. Even if they flaunt and fly during festivals or moments of pleasure and might, so is there no persistent peace or stability; nor a constance of leisure, of the richest and hand-made sort that is.
 Yet, why does he care, this cynic. I found this thought floating around me, as a state of curioisty tookover during our stroll through the woods. What struck me immedtiaedly is that the man, however bound in virtue and strenght, who deems other flawed, is not himself truly happy. And there is this air of, 'can't you see me over here, doing all that is right and well; and se how pleased I am, and in the fairest of ways'. Yet for all his happiness, it comes via load; nor is it so easy to see and keep to the path, as he lacks a friend. That is his true motive for (more or less) lashing out; he wishes others were like him, so he could work and achieve in union.
 I came out of the brush, near the edge of where one can clearly see the sea. The land here is generally flat, and full of grassy patches that glow in the wind. "A hint of the further stretching fields ahead --towards the mountains." As I strode along, wafting in the cool sea air, I had a well-formed thought. We have all the friends and material that we need. There may lack certain pleasure, rightly had or not; but we do have a fair amount; as folk move all around us, be it in family, peers or broader kin. What we lack is the ability to be present, and not luster after something which they may offer; be it sex, time, oppertunity, approval. These things speak of our lacking, and how this feeling that our admittedly strong man has, still feels absent; and as such, in my experiuence at least,'either condemns or invites relieving pleasure'. One way leads us astray, while the other reeks of stubborness.
 As I reached the fields, our thought went away. Part of what we saw withdrew; as it often does. And indeed, had I been thinking of an apple, and had such thought blurr away, I would not be mistaken or unclear as to the general thing which had become a haze. And indeed, this thought which drifted off, is one that often does, and is itself the mark of our inquiry. Our inability to say it is what gives it so ease in scuttling away, 'at the meagerest gust of wind --or distraction as it were'. And so I sat on the golden hill, where the land was near our home, flat, but edged by a faint yet pleasing dip; where I comfoortably sat, looking at the peaks ahead. The deepending blue sky behind them.
So we have a good selection of works for Plato and Arisottle. For the latter, we have the companion, which is there for Hume, and likely Kant also. These are nicely written, and uniquely at that, mixtures of pieces by different authors; which is nice, for there is usually some angle which you can appreciate, betweeen the technical and colorful side. I also have the history of Aristotle; and these all should suffice in providing insight to his doctrines. Similar for Plato, Taylors work has been nice. It's not too scrumptuous, but good enough. I know little of Plato, and have an interest of the scholarly sort; and Taylor's work gives a good sense of that, and is organized quite well. We'll keep to him.
 I want to read about Hume, and the cambridge companion (likes Barnes for Aristotle) is there. This may be enough. Similar to this is the roots and development of modern thought; in particular, the contrasts with what it was before, and how we arrive at the systems of Kant. Part of my search is to the merrit and or neccesity for the form of the argumnets, and how they compare to the stricter sciences ahead. It is nice to see how any such empiricism developed, and what gives merrit to it over the first-principle-deductions of the opposing side.
 I mean not to exhaust myself, but I do have some genuine intent. Compared to our studious wants, where might and will come together, our interests in reading are about taking something in; while study requires formation, 'and as such a hand that can handle what comes'. As such, our reading wants are able to be broad. I must not ware myself thin though looking for things; nor forget that the point is to read. Indeed, our reading finds much use in the heart of the subject; and so in the case of Taylor's plato, I made read only a 100 pages, but in that get a sense of Plato's life, general work and influence, and maybe something specific about the Georgias. This will set within me, 'like a spring rain: sufficient for the months to come, till were dry'. In other words, our reading is so as to please me now, and sprout the early seeds of further curiosity and seeing (for works to come, that we may better look for, with an aim in mind). No matter, the point is that I am looking for alot. And indeed, the prescence of these books, considering their role in histroy and science, is different to that of war; where the pikcings are generally slim, and the points within so precious, that I feel elated at a valid book, because oh my, 'we'll find something'. What I seek otherwise, is bound within the example of pre-relativity, and the similar thoughts and developments that occoured, and contrasting thought at that. I find this a great way to learn of the world and it's way; for you see not truly what relativity is, but where it came from, why it held merrit, where other folk were wrong. And indeed, wwe have no calcuations which we wish to make, nor any discipline we want to pursue. It's the idea, the frameowork which our view of the world rests on; and where in that lies truth, 'right pillars of inquiry'. That all being said, Hume and Kant stick out as noteworthy figures. Berkely seems interesting, but by his own hand; whereas the prior two feel like pivots to work around, be it in approachable treatment, or of their relation to prior and coming thought. Each book may offer something different; and indeed, this route is more primned for a liburary, than say Hume; who you may well lilkely not read, besides a few things. But in the case of treatments of history and development, there are hole angles and sides, which any array of authors can produce. Indeed, on example is the conflict of science based thinking, regarding physics for instance --as Biology is not particularly of interest, and Physics is itself a soul-relevance, and by that, more illiminuating to the principle differens in approach and doctrine.
 Indeed, for productiviy sake, have some sense of the books, as to know what you have. Dispose of them as you will, 'but there staying with you; and so know who enters and leaves your home, and why. What did they lack, what did you take'. And indeed, to produce all this, make sure to label/bookmark your pieces of one kind before moving onto the next. Currently, we have Plato and Arisottle, though we could use some marks for the companions/batches. Equal so will that be for Hume and Kant potentially. If there is no reason, either because they are not, or at least what they may lack is just found better elsehwere, that of more historical and wide treatment, so are these like Taylor's Plato, 'in need of a TC, intro, and first chapter or two'. Lets find 5-6 singular books, focusing on natural,empirocal philsophy and it's development, the contrast of science and principle, and in more particular, the hard vs substance/ether based physics. Pre-relativity thought and development for sure. Pivoting on Hume and Kant, and perhaps Descartes -if he be so relevant-- you can hopefully find less treatise/exegiscal treatments, and more interprative/judgement/meaning-assigned to them, for their worth, but certainly for their impact to come. Then a companion or two for each of them, which may hold that comvbination of pure treatment, with broader insights and commentary.
 Just a quick note, but glancing over Hume's companion piece, I could see from the starkest subjects, 'in time or causation', that the treatment was very approachable, initially at least full of commnetary, perspective and interprataion. And I had the thought that Hume invites this very nicely; for all his work and effort, he is still a skeptic, or makes the point of our senses being the source for our reasoning, yet there being a flaw, or at least haze to this. People can disagree, and in a sense profress their belief in something more certain. But it is easier to appreciate, or at least explicityl state someones less than sure views. I get a feeling of respect amongst these folk writing about Hume. For he needs no apolgaizing for, as Aristotle and Plato do; as they are so esteemed, but often being excused for their 'ancient origins', which is to say, when measured against future developments. Hume is merely a thinker, one of inquiry, and reaches sketpical merrit. There is a good theme then of his ideas being dismissed because they are not certain enough, or run against otherwise accepted views. The authors can then show where there is legitamate shortcoming, or unfortunate refusal on his warranted skepticism. David Fate Norton is the paralell to Jonathon Barnes Arisottle companion. He writes the intro, and the overview of Hume's thoughts. One precious aspect is the seuqence within each piece; which to be clear, is done by individual authors; which I like, for the nature of Hume's life and ideas is one that I like seeing a variety of views on, 'as it speaks to someone humble and proper to respect his way of thinking'. I connect with that. But also, in each of the pieces, they are broken up into segments, and the whole thing flows the better. In his skepticism, for instance, there is 'the dimunuantion argument'; which is a segment based on some notable instance or forumula within the bulk of his skepticism. This is a very approachable way. And indeed, Pyscholgoy feels more obscure than physics, and it is more about the noteworthiness and comparison between his causal and or spacial sentiments that I care for. But a) these are fairly readable, and full of non-techincal matter, and b) there are many general and or speicfic sentiments, such as religion, being skeptical, government, that afford a more whole interest, and learning at that.
 These Cambridge, or any other for that matter, compendiums/companion, which is to say collections of pieces by different others --on a rooting topic-- is quite useful, as each one naturally has a style and flair per the author; and there is a good balance between techinicals and interperation. Both, when seperately empahsized, often leaves you distant from a piced; at least it demands a certain state of mind, or want rather. In these cases, the most obscure to the most pleasant topics can be covered in a substantial way. Early Modern Philosophy has one of these, along with Hume's and Aristotles as noted. I have the fear that they will turn out to be too thin and general; this is so for the resource at hand is so seemingly full and rich, that I don't want to fool myself. No matter, I feel like there are many areas in which this style of book could offer us someting. Another note are books of a seemingly crass, or at least particular subject, yet of one no less interesting and fleshed out. Histroy of experimental philosophy is such one along with early modern skepticism. Again, very specific, and you don't know what your going to find. Much of my measure for seeing so are barred by people or ideas we already know. "We'll these relate to Hume?" But the TC can show the areas covered, and your interest can be the driving force. The style too, may be particular enough, and afford deeper insights to the broader rules of philsophy, by looking into a certain area. Just try them --but look little for more like them.
 So the companions (by cambridge) seem to be per subject, a set of essays by individual authors; usually of a professor sort; so the content and interpration is deep and satisfying. I can't say whether their all like this, but they seem to have sections, which is great, for it gives each piece digestibility. I want, for instance, to hear about Kant and his ideas, but if one part get's too bogged down in the technique, then a)the writing seems fair enough to move past this on it's own, and b) a new segment resets the course of the writing. And their all good. They feel like my essays: full of words and terms, descriptions for those familiar with something. It's not dumb, nor orbiting jargon and or commentary --as if the goal is to reach the middle through interepation; but all comments and or prefaces are but middling. While these are meant to teach and speak too. It feels as though these people want to spread this, but from their own credited standard. And there are a lot of things. Aquinas even. One that strikes out is Idealism and the Enlightenment. This shines as something more about the subject, and learninig through it's place to broader things. I find much promise in all of this.
So the thing I want, in terms of study, is to better form my view of the bound view of matter; yet maintain the inquiry of such basis for such bound. Take, for instance, two apparent objects, and consider them as seperate: easy to do. Yet, imagine the space between held similar elements, or fibres, as the objects were composed of; only, those in between the objects had a different bend, whereby the clumpier arrangment of the objects seemed more 'objective', yet there was in fact no void between them. There was merely a sea of fibres, some bent this way, others that way; and at any rate, arrangments here and there. "Indeed our vision responds as though there is space."
 The point being, that this view of mine, which I feel fine in how I just stated, is never so easy to parse. And indeed, some science would help. But it really comes down to that basis. The thing described is merely so as I feel fair in seeing it; which is to say, contrary to what we commonly view. But the aim is to further our view of how this clump of fibres move, for as soul and bound as all things are, where does this come from, how do we describe quanity; are the fibres seperate. And so forth.
 My study then is motivated to the modern world, and that of empirical and scientific development. Indeed, much of our thought is not based on some technical scheme, merely inspiration for our inquiry, and qualification at that. Idealism, the era beyond Kant, come as subjects worth our concern. For it is a time of change; albeit built off of the age prior. Indeed, science is a descriptivie process, and all things relativity and magnetics, 'merely convey the form of the object in which were finding the basis for'. We need only parse our view, as to better firm our un-sensical views. But how such basis ought be viewed, or has been, is fuel for me to be equally determined, or at least considerate, for such basis.
So my aim follows the line of modern philosophy and teacing, all so this odd reliance on argument, as to bear knowledge, may be properly, or at least historically understood.
 As such, I do have a fundemental wish for Aristotle and Plato, but other; considering their effect on later efforts, and their worth being so tied to the emphasis on certain qualities and or forsm: it is nice to know what these are.
 At any rate, we need not get distracted by Beethoven or William James. I want to hear about science vs rational physics, the empiricism of Hume, what Kant is all about; how this all relates to later 19th century science, and the adjacent analytic/positivist philosophy.
 Most of this can be found in the Cambrige companions. They have solid intros, many chapters of great and accessible lenght following; and as far as I can tell, the style and substance is there in depth and ease; and limited only by your interest.
 As such, let us look at our prospects, or choices rather, and determine their worth and our intent.
 For German Idealism, Kant and Kant and Modern Philosphy, I really just want to hear about the developments of the time, their roots and proceedings. I would like to know more about Kant's ingenuity, as I would for relativity. Indeed, that serves as an example, whereby learning abotu the ether and othering surroudning theories, and presumptions prior, gives insight to the grand insight to come. I wish I could speak more of that grand inight of Kants. Kant's particular book begins with a potential intro, and chapter on Kant's own development, which promises great insight to the world around, 'and what prompted him'; some fair context if you will. Indeed, much of our aim centers on understanding the layers in which current thought is bade by. And indeed, there may be merrit to what Kant says, but progress no less. It really amounts to us being able to wonder and doubt within our physical inquiries, based on different views within the demonstrative world. Kant, for instance, is noted for stating a requisite knowledge or premise of time and space, as if they are some given to our statements. This is per demonstratie reasoning, but the thought has some literal, or at least potential, physical inspiration. The final chapter offers some sight of the response to Kant, which is a great way to learn about him, as we hear about the conflict his views gave, 'and thus what such views were'. The first few lines seemed quite nice. Beyond the first and last chapter, it is all about Kant's work. The beginning 'Transcendantal Aesthetic' feels like a novel entry, as said Transcendence is the heart of Kant. Though it being tagged to Aesthetic is not clear to me. I am sure at this point we can get the jist of his core development. Similar to Hume's notion of association: we need not certainly explore what this means for things beyond this, but rather grip the baser point. This book is by Paul Goyer - Prof. of philosophy at Pennsylvania. Edited by Paul Goyer again, Kant and Modern Philosophy has a similar layouyt to the prior book, only I suppose it has more historical considerations. There is an into, then similar chapter layout; though each may promise more preceeding and proceeding regards, rather than strict treatment of the tenants. The final chapter shares receptions of his work in the short term. All in all, deep work here, but the transcendant chapter in Kant, both final chapters about his impact, and perhaps some of the modern Kant pieces, for their comparative approach regarding his work. "I seek much yet want little from Kant." German Idealism, by Karl Ameriks -Prof of philosophy Notre Dame-- has broader insights to idealism and the enlightenment. The first chapter deals with this specifically. 2nd chapter is about absolute idealism versus Kant's dualism. This could be promising. The final chapter has the legacy of idealism.
 Indeed, my effort here is to form comprehensive, though not awfully large library, as to hold some reference to Kant, Hume, idealism, early science and posturing, 19th century thinking; a seperate effort that holds similar interests but in the realm of science --which is not in the companions. But each work already comes with contents, and is styled similarly. Each piece inside is also segmented, which makes for easier digestion. Generally, the intro may be less appealling if your familiar with the topic, nor not so ardently interested. The final chapter also gives reflections and usually some thought on the effect of the subjects prior. Note, that each piece is fairly long, by essay standards. So it's no small measure to view a companion by its intro, first chapter or so, and the final one, as the bulk of our care. Bits in between may serve us on specific-seeking cicurmstnaces later on. While earlier chapters, or broad one's at least, may afford a genearl grasp of the particular dealings to come.
Montesque, for the law
 The Federalist
 Philosophical Methodology
 Modern Russian/Indian/Japanese/French/German Culture?
 Science and Religion
 Hegel and 19th century Philosophy
 Logical Empiricism
 Platos Republic --it is considered the root of philosophy, and so interepating the rules which come from the warrant of the substance
 Post-Modern Theology (likely pro/apolagetic)
 Critical Theory --a intro for later modern work
 Pascal --similar to the Republic, his merrit can illuminate more into math
So we have the logicians: Kant, his modern piece, German Idealism, Hume and plenty, early modern thought, Logical Empiricism. This latter is nice, as it encloses a great deal of the late 19th century positivism and non-seperate world thought. For Hume, his life and writing is of great interest to me; and he serves as a great pivot point. He also makes great points of a skeptical sort; and his notion of association is alos cool. Religion equally. There are many pieces in him which are of worth. The German idealism and Enlightnement section feels appropriatley historical and comparative; as does Kants dualism vs absolutism feel insightfull.
 Science and religion has less techincal but rather historical treatment; exactly what I seek. More particular, the handbook of physics (history), deals with such subjects in a comparative and storied way; with very little formula. Section 3 is all 19 century: optics, magnetism, thermodynamics. I feel odd when comparing 20th century to present day particle physics. But the history of these fields is of worth, and there is an entire section on them. And indeed, we may learn more, as to find stronger bearers in our own absolute views and inquiry; and at that, have better footing to pinpoint where we should look in recent memory.
 Earth science is important, in how nebula are formed and make stars, and then planets in their accretion. How earth's crust developed and land at that, and the early spring of live and climate. There is the 'story of earth', which is more simple, yet substantial no less; and go for the most part, as I am not truly familiar with much. A habitable world is much more technical, but seems less texbook than you'd expect. These are great options.
 I should note the scottish enlightenment, which, in relation to toher era/period dealnigs with science and philosphy, within the companions, is one of a kind --and there are not many, only early modern and german idealism, and logical empiricism. I am sure Hume will be mentioend; but there is this kind, gentle but also obvious tone to 'the scottish' in said part of the enligthenment. May we find others word our admiration, or learn something no less.
 Lastly, and indeed this may proove to be false, but by our first glimpse, 'molecular pyscholdogy' deals with vision and hearing and all things sense, through a physical lense. It is quite technical, but more like a handbook than a texbook.
So work is the return point of our lives. Something can assure anyone, is that without work, you'd have no food nor home, nor means for leisure. Indeed, we may be given this, out of privalege or infancy; but those who provide are in the position which you would be in were you to not be given. And so we can judge from there.
 For week feels tough and chore like, and indeed like something we wish didn't have to occur; and that somewhere else would be better. But think of it like an animal; they are given nothing but what they gather; and indeed, the supplies are limited, and so they have little room to ponder and wait. "Most barely make it over the basic line." People are no different. And indeed, what we earn is of a different sort; but we still need food and bed. Our economy works as to give people secure and comfortable space. Besides homelessness there is no other option. And so when we think of work, realize that we are in a life of need and rule, and that it is quite childish to consider such scenario as unfair. It is the nature of life to experience this. This is why though, when we accomplsih work, and find it not to be a burden, we can rejoice, for we have to do it. We don't elect.
As for work itself, I realize that we are, or were at least, presenting ourselves to Amon, as to bring about some connection. 'Making a move' so to speak is beyond this, and something I find myself at odds to do; nor with as much reason but of that which our enthusiasm spurrs in us. We just keep waiting for things to naturally occur. Yet our scenario at work makes it a struggle to do anything more. And indeed, because we act like so, and in some sense let ourselves accept our kind and courteous ways, we almost overfill the pre-requisite for such move to be made; that it almost feels harder to make it, seeing as it is more or less simple, when we have in fact done so much to prep it; but never actually done it. This is my state with Amon; for I felt she was not receptive to our enthusiasm, nor did I truly believe she wanted me to make a move. She may, as we were presenting ourselves for something to happen; but I have grave doubts that us doing so begged such question. As such, I feel, or felt at least, like we were way to overboard in our emotion for her. And that it was hard for me to conswider making a move. I think perhaps that in principle it is a little crass for us to see her as someone to casually date. I wanted her to have some genuine feeling for me; even though, I realized that she more than likely doesn't, for our interest was based on attraction, and not genuine care for her: so why would she have some for me.
 Something I've noticed, with everyone, is that its hard to anticipate our interaction with one anther. Some people are polite, others ignore you. Some are receptive, and offer even enthusiasm. Some people are less open, and seem to feel off. One trick is to do the eyebrow raise, where you acknoweldge someone in passing, with a sign of care, or familiarity, but without a cordial 'how do you do'; as if your strange enough to be merley polite.

 As for health, consistency is key. Though getting there can be hard. Working to much, or resting too little, making mistakes around this, and our natural form of work and sitting being so conseuqnrly off, makes for varied types of what's appropriate. Right now for instance, my left shoulder is tender, our neck is getting a little tight, and most of our joints are immobile. We were fine the week or two prior, having done some pushups and running; and I felt the benefit in work clearly. But we ate 24 springrolls, and searched endlessly for companiosn around the same time. Work today felt narrow, and we were quite tired. Our yoga is done though, and were but filling out the sides --like our neck, more standing and seated. Our suppleness just isn't there. And indeed, exercise breaths potential breathe and mold; yet without it, exercise becomes more difficult, and can bear trouble. Range of motion is the most precocious, as were just not stable enough. Nor is doing free range enough to bring that ppotential material. Running feels like the right course of action; and indeed, eating well, sleeping well, remaing true to your path and not rolling in headstrong, nor being to overly-active, should make room for such running and it's incorporation.
So, yesterday, the US government, in tandum with some part of the intelligence agency and the military, made an offensive strike in Venezuela, for the aim of extracting the president, Maduro. He is noted as an ill-legitmate president, and is not recognized by most western countries. The US have publicly stated that they beieve he is a supporter of drug trafficking in the United States. He is also more or less a fugitive in America.
 This is one of those things, like Epstein or some other noteworthy event, which draws up all this talk, criticism: media and opinons. And this bogs me down, truly. Having the constituionally sense, as to why this is more or less fair, and by that, not indicative of the other bits of government, is naturally reasurring. I must state that our need for such assurance comes from our combination of a lack of pleasure and our worn state; which in itself does not live, and must sit, or find pleasure; but if it is the former, news and other things of a compulsive or 'thin sort', ware me down. If only because we beg for them, in an addicting way; as they act as a sort of pleasure. Yet this creates rot views, and an abandoned sense of our thoughtfull form. Being good is paramount, as does information elevate us there but also protect us when we fall. Obama's bio feels lilke a calming entry, from what I recall. Pence's is alright, but more affirmative to Trump as a whole. I wish for general understanding of the government. "The width which true education brings allows for commentary to be what it is, and not a front position." I compare these interests with that of essays, which are a colleciton of bits. In their case I do like to arrange them, 'as I feel like I have a small amount to do'. It becomes overhwleminlg otherwise; and I loose this sense of what we have. Indeed we fall into this dilemma of choosing which ones, and feeling like some may be promising, and that were missing. For starters, each piece is substantial on it's own. The 'rethinking classical physics' is not some prelude to a more meaningfull piece, but an encompassing source itself. Similar to Kants development, or the enlightenment+idealism. The absolutism vs dualism is a bit more technical; and although interesting, is not so paramount. The point though, is that when surveying your choices, there is a mixture of promise and clarity. As the end of Kant is a 5 year note of his reception. This could be less of an oversigth and a more technical look, but the promise is there on the face of it. Mathematics in the new sciences seemed promising, but there was part of it that seemed unclear; we looked in, saw what it was, and could embrace the promise. Indeed, what I want are those overviews: Hume's Skepticism, the Vienna conention, the state of the enlightnement (in the scottish one). These are of apparent interest. There is the one on Hume's religion, and on the face of it, this seems nice, but there's part of me that is in a bit of doubt, similar to his politics. A brief scan shows me whether there is roots to this promise. And indeed, we find ourselves with a mixture of things, such as Kant's transcendental or Hume's association. These may not come to mind in comparison to science vs theology, but similar to Optics in the 19 century, my intent is there. And from a state of ability and want, so they'll come. Every piece could be valid then, but were limited. And our goal is not to learn everything, nor be excited for everything, but to reflect on our interest and what sparks desire. Some form of 20th century physics reflects this. As does the general early passages of the Earth's story or Habitable planet. Even the immunology in the cambridge science book. For one, certain groups may be dealt with later, such as the history of math, or the handbook of physics. Within the ones we do deal with, we may take what we want, glance over one's with potential and doubt, then leave it there. The one's left are generally those which would speak to our more specific interests. When we were reading Hume, and we wanted more insight to his many aspects, the several pieces on each one within his companion will serve us then. And indeed, we have them. But more imprtanlty, we knwo the form of companions, in the case say of Aristotle, which we can find.
What might I even say. Which terms will I use. Do I have the energy to even think and write? One note is that our sense of words comes from some shiftiness in us; which comes from some rearrangment within me, for better or for worse. And generally, this shift becomes clearer as we become more familiar with it; and so as it persists, and we take notes, we are able to see it for what it really is.
 What I would describe is our whole life, and how it has this form of a cloud, with dips here and cavities there; but that in certain states, which we often see measure the good by, this cloud is lofty, light but not overly soft, nor too flimsy and without structure. And there are opposities to this. What we deem good is that which makes our cloud like the former, and equally if not.
 The most important point comes down to our nature, as breathing animals, who need to eat, have shelter, and by that, some commonality and ability with people alike; if there is a community were part of --which is not easy to argue as being something non-preferable. The reality though is that this nature of ours, or condition, creates ware and tear, by our very effort; nor does it feel enjoyable as we bear it. And indeed, not only in dealnig with the effort that was, so do our senses get weighed down by the effort to come. Pleasuse ceases this, as if an oiintment. It lets our troubles go, and brings us away from any concern ahead.
 The oddest thing in life, which I may speak about well, if not coherently, is why there is anything but pleasure which should be sought; and why, if all toil was gone and pleasure was all we had, that any situation would fall flat in comparison. All I have is a reflection and inquiry; into our adaption within a heavy state, and how we personally changed to match this, but then found goods from such a state; our inquiry is into the truth in this matter, for it comes from a novel circumstance; rather than a real state that is manifested from true grounds. For we have the siutation where trouble is absent, and thus you have no grief needing to be soothed. You also have no pressure to be better, and by that experience the good from doing so.
 Indeed, our life is so, that we came to this better state through a worse one. But while the time at hand had as pointed to remove the weight in our life and earn better pleasures, we simply strenghtened, and by that grew grounds for joy and understanding. Yet, the weight of things wore on me, and we wished to remove then. We sought to live outside; and if not, we lived at home, where a space was given. We lack truth though in the matter; and indeed through inquiry I see it. But at any rate, that space was more or less temporary; and so we could not rest there. The grounds for good had to be self driven; for we were otherwise living in a dream, or deluded, 'as if change would not come'.
 I think its important to note that the feeling of weight creates a want of both relief (of weight) or ointment by pleasure. Whereas not feeling the weight leaves you open, like a child, and things of interert draw you. Emkoyment is the greatest of things, for it is a seemingly unending flow. Pleasure has this effect, but ultimately ends in satiety. Also, whatever sight we may have, of truth or steadyness while we ponder, that our excess state (above weight) produces more of this, and thus a spirit which binds our inefficeincys --as to say what we have potential to surpass-- and natural fortune --which we may not control, via our circumstance or what our body has ordained.
 I think that our late adolescence has fallen prey to a degree of standard which is only so to create the most living. Yet there is an error perhaps, in that having such a standard leads to no such living, or sustainably at least. For we've whithered thin; and find ourself falling out of proper action. Our consistency has been so, that what it demands of us to be proper is never wanted. We cannot work out as we'd please, nor do nothing. Falling out creates this moments of harshness. Yet, I find it hard to bear fruit elsewhere. Having friends is hard when you don't drink or smoke, nor snapchat and text, nor eat breakfast or formal dinner; nor do you eat sugar, sit in chairs, sleep in beds, or drive. And all of this is to say that we have isolated ourselves from all things irrelevant or contrary to virtue. Yet where does this mould come from? I'd mention that if we had more truth, that in our later youth we'd have maintained more friends and oppertunity; and by that, been in less of a drought --as concern these things now.
 I do not know in truth what lies beyond. If there is, it seems like were giving leave of our current propensity. As it fails, we look beyond; and feel nerved by that which either compells us, or drives right in, 'making us feel a swarm of fear and emotion'. That is the point of this inquiry, to motive if you will. If being well, as ourselves, is possible, and if so desired. It seems so off for me to say that doing so will not lead to friends, family, money nor romance, and that we will work in force-drive ways, out of bed early. So much pressure is one me to be me, and hold ourselves true to the choices and deeds which keep me so, and avoid me drifting off. All worry and doubt seem to arise within the grooves of where we have been, or are, so off. Thats what inquiry is for; to go past your current state, and let old knowledge and new inspiration form a view that brnigs you past your current state of precarity. When we are in a good state, no question is needed.
 Hegel makes the point that absolutism is not about reconciling the distinct reality of intuition and conception, but rather seeing them as two disctinions within a shared reality. "There different expressions of the same thing, rather than two things seperate which need reconcilation." As such, making a fortune, or findnig fortune, is so that it must come from the actions which create it. And as such, we can not judge ourselves by how things would be were they different, for the chance and or development has not got there. We must judge those, or prepare at least.
 I have said that we can make smart choices. But, in terms of my interest, or allocation, or prospect at that, writing to math teachers, dating --for the websites are so poor-- and anything online at that, fall beneath so. Our finances for instance are not in my grasp, and that is a poor choice.
With great rejoice I admitt the effect of having work to do, and being payed for that, and by that count, feeling the reward of what you need. "What animal wouldn't fight for a chance to be given sure food and stead." Work is required, but the question becomes about experience rather than legitimacy.
 Indeed, my life is banked upon the results of feeling free. You see everything, find interest, bear fruits of a formative or curious sort. And your leisure is ever nice; in the everyday walks, the sights of the sun and moons rise, transporting into new worlds, or others expression. I don't mean to undermine anything, but I do wish to qualify our words by what is primary. As some things may be nice, or not, but we ought start from the ground up; and perhaps see where certain things are extra and or superfluous.
 It seems that an abundnace of joy and craft come when your stable and well kept. They balance each out though in their ability to take sway; as it is really easy to be so stable; as far as Im comcerned you only need a room and food, and the few days of work that provide this. But work is tough, peoples own pleasure, novel or not, influence you, or ruin company; as do you seek things, be it in what you've experienced before, or conjured when you felt worn. Moreover, it is not so satsifying to work little yet live litte. The strongest path is one of reasonable work, smooth effort, self regard and utmost living.
 When I was 23 I found myself discontent with work. I also felt poor at work when we engaged in pleasure. Indeed there was no romance or riches, but I felt more comfortable with people; having no remission for food and comfort gave me more company than prior. But I felt awful; at work, and also in any real satiety. As it were, I could not move on, and so upon reading Epictetus I saw that we were an objective, and that we were treating it less than we could. Upon doing so, I felt great. And the deep effort we put centered on our choice to sleep and sit on the floor. In the prior months (and year) we had naturally gravitated towards this. The demand of work paired with our meager prescence of pleasure, had me doing what seemed good. I cannot recal the benefit we recieved, or if we saw; but it is perhaps better said that we took action upon what we noticed as poor; which we noticed because of a lack of pleasure. As though we were worse off, and our life of work positioned me to care about this.
 From a physical stand point, we felt great sleeping, but were mixed in sitting. We often just layed on our side, as the day cooled down. We often had crackers, and were not averse to the occaisional quiche. We also smoked during the fall.
 As winter came we had closed our aim for illustrator and took up writing instead. We sat on the floor, more or less cross legged, and wrote on our dinner tray. We did this very much, and felt no struggle in doing so. I have some recollection of us noticing how our general gait brought us to do this with ease; and that upon certain states, we felt less ease in returning.
 The throbbing fact is that during the summer, while we were personal training, we began to develop these aches during a class, and as time went on, felt so poor, 'unable to be it seemed'. I left early, and had to get off the bus. I laid down at the park we stopped at during our first trek during Septemebr the year prior --on broadway. I layed in the leafs, and this more or less helped. I recall being on the bus, and near the bridge bowing my head over; then, when I got home, it was as if I had this mixture of pinched nerves and a fever. This compares to the experiences I've been having this past year or so.
 Oddly enough this never happened again. Looking at it, we worked out from time to time during the week --before we had classes. This felt a little off the cough, and was generally centered on bearing some range of motion --for physique sake. From there on, through our time working at Superstore, we had a similar objective, working out after a shift, if only because we felt off for not doing so. Around this time we had a few fevers. Indeed, one of them felt like Covid, where we had terrible stomach pains. Something like this happened a few months ago. Also, prior to us sitting on the floor, we over-exerted ourselves, having ridden to Lougheed mall and back, and we got a fever then. Around this time we had stopped eating breakfast; and for a moment, we were only eating one large meal a day.
 During work, we often felt good, with some times hear or there feeling off. We would walk from the Cul-de-sac, do an eight hour shift, walk home, and walk many times after. And generally we felt great. I can't sit here and say that sitting on the floor or eating later in the day bore us flaws. We generally felt great, and were great. I can't say this ever stopped, from when we left, to Vancouver, when we came back home, to Vancouver again. It is worth mentioning that I had a similar 'insatiated push' for working out in Cen.Van. And I did notice that we would feel stiff and off, but not pained in any way. We ate more while we were there, as we'd have naan bread often. We would also eat plenty of fruit and veg during the day. Alot of great changes took place. And I did notice that we felt off about not working out, and would sometimes run during the night. I think our pleasure was in need, as we would buy cooked naan bread from the local stores. There were also a few nights that we got junk; which I should mention, is something we did from time to time in 2018, less so in 2019, sometimes in 2020, a fair amount in summer 2021.
 All of my pains started happening when I moved to Kamloops. Perhaps stress makes us be more active when we shouldn't be. For we do sit on the gorund, and have eaten less and less, and generaly worked out little; and so if we work thorugh the night, or program, or find books, or with Diablo even, this is something I see now, 'where were movnig from stiff grounds'; and perhaps being at home has you struggling to sit and breathe. I might mention that the greatest time of our lives was in Vancouver; and it was because we were in tandum with work, but able to wait for it. Be it a hot or rainy day, we saw work coming tomorrow, and could do what was right, rest on it, and enjoy our time prior with no hasty, mounrfull, nor on edge amends. I just have a hard time moving around at home, and this puts me into a less satisfied state; which begs more of me, for pleasure. This is more often than not wrong, yet activity is not always right. It feels like through 2023 we experienced our impingment from time to time; though it was more like a lack of ability, for which we did amidttedly sit down for. But last year, in 2024, we stopped eating junk entirely, wrote enormously, read as much. And our exericse became less about irregular workouts, but often 'free range' as it were. Indeed, we forced ourselves to workout prematurely; as we did not do it enough, but then would push ourselves in. Our terrible feelings began happening then. Though I don't recall exactly when they started. Almost certaintly I was aware of them prior to the move, and during new years, while playing Metor, it happened. Our neck just feels awful, or left hip feels like it's being grinded on. Our left posterior feels like its sinking into our hip, and our front hip-tendon-between our crotch and upper crest, gets rock hard. Our left hamstrings get more or less tender, and our inner thing becomes really strung. Our legs loose there uprightness, and everything feels pronated. And working from here, sitting from here, and in the worse scenarios, training from here, leaves my neck and head distraught. Recently our should has received the brunt; and only it.
 The truth is that I am worn down by all those things tempting and or promiscuous, when I have done wrong, or am in a narrow state. And pleasure feels all the less probable.
